London Stock Exchange Group (LSE) is gearing up to finalise its $27bn (£20bn) takeover of financial data business Refinitiv, according to insiders. LSE has reportedly set March 3 as the target date to complete the acquisition, which was first proposed in 2019.

Subject to regulatory approvals in key markets around the world, this landmark deal could strengthen LSE's footing in financial markets and potentially allow the company to expand their global offering. This comes as the global market forecasting industry sees a boom in growth, spurred on by the changing dynamics of business around the world, and the increasing data needs for businesses and governments alike.

The LSE previously stated that the combination with Refinitiv was about "boldly positioning capital market infrastructure for decades to come.” It added that the deal would enable it to compete with global bourse leaders, such as Bloomberg.

The Refinitiv takeover would be one of the largest in LSE's history. Refinitiv provides financial market data and infrastructure to over 40,000 global institutions, and its acquisition would drastically extend the scale and scope of LSE's activities.

The planned completion date follows doubts raised last year surrounding the acquisition after a comprehensive review by the European Commission on competition grounds. The LSE was required to sell its Italian bond trading platform, MTS, to appease regulators. Dutch firm Euronext has agreed to buy Borsa Italiana Group including MTS.

Despite the regulatory hiccups, shareholders have overwhelmingly backed the deal - 99.27% of votes were cast in favour at a general meeting held last year.

The Chief Executive of LSE, David Schwimmer, has previously commented that he believes the acquisition will be transformative for the company, as well as its customers and shareholders, and is optimistic about full regulatory approval.

Even so, investors will be closely watching the final stages of the deal, considering the significant resources that have been committed, and the shifting competitive landscape within the financial markets industry.

Shares in Europe's leading reinsurers and travel firms have fallen as the scale of the damage wrought by tsunamis across south Asia has become apparent.
More than 23,000 people have been killed following a massive underwater earthquake and many of the worst hit areas are popular tourist destinations. Reisurance firms such as Swiss Re and Munich Re lost value as investors worried about rebuilding costs. But the disaster has little impact on stock markets in the US and Asia.
Currencies including the Thai baht and Indonesian rupiah weakened as analysts warned that economic growth may slow. "It came at the worst possible time," said Hans Goetti, a Singapore-based fund manager. "The impact on the tourist industry is pretty devastating, especially in Thailand." Travel-related shares dropped in Europe, with companies such as Germany's TUI and Lufthansa and France's Club Mediterranne sliding. Insurers and reinsurance firms were also under pressure in Europe.
Shares in Munich Re and Swiss Re - the world's two biggest reinsurers - both fell 8.1% as the market speculated about the cost of rebuilding in Asia. Zurich Financial, Allianz and Axa also suffered a decline in value.
However, their losses were much smaller, reflecting the market's view that reinsurers were likely to pick up the bulk of the costs. Worries about the size of insurance liabilities dragged European shares down, although the impact was exacerbated by light post-Christmas trading. Germany's benchmark Dax index closed the day 16.29 points lower at 2.248.90 while France's Cac index of leading shares fell 5.07 points to 3.817.56. Investors pointed out, however, that declines probably would be industry specific, with the travel and insurance firms hit hardest. "It's still too early for concrete damage figures," Swiss Re's spokesman Floiran Woest told Associated Press. "That also has to do with the fact that the damage is very widely spread geographically."
The unfolding scale of the disaster in south Asia had little immediate impact on US shares, however. The Dow Jones index had risen 70.86 points, or 0.2%, to 10,847.66 by late morning as analsyts were cheered by more encouraging reports from retailers about post-Christmas sales. In Asian markets, adjustments were made quickly to account for lower earnings and the cost of repairs. Thai Airways shed almost 4%. The country relies on tourism for about 6% of its total economy. Singapore Airlines dropped 7.0%. About 5% of Singapore's annual gross domestic product (GDP) comes from tourism. Malaysia's budget airline, AirAsia fell 0.9%. Resort operator Tanco Holdings slumped 0%.
Travel companies also took a hit, with Japan's Kinki Nippon sliding 0.4% and HIS dropping 3.6%. However, the overall impact on Asia's largest stock market, Japan's Nikkei, was slight. Shares fell just 0.03%. Concerns about the strength of economic growth going forward weighed on the currency markets. The Indonesian rupiah lost as much as 5.5% against the US dollar, before bouncing back slightly to trade at 9,158. The Thai baht lost 0.3% against the US currency, trading at 48.26. In India, where more than 2,691 people are thought to have died, the rupee shed 3.6% against the dollar Analysts said that it was difficult to predict the total cost of the disaster and warned that share prices and currencies would come under increasing pressure as the bills mounted.

A nation held captive by fear of Covid-19. That is France today. So says Gabriel Attal, the government spokesman, as he lays out the reasons for opting for a new lockdown, just a month after the last one ended. 

The announcement, made with Tuesday's dramatic midnight address by President Emmanuel Macron, sparked a wave of anger, disappointment and anxiety across the country. 

Some healthcare professionals and scientists praised the move. "Finally, we are taking the necessary measures," one intensive care doctor told me. "We were way beyond the point of pretending this virus could be managed with just a few restrictions." 

But for millions of French people, the new lockdown spells the collapse of an already fragile mental health. Businesses are shattered. People's routines, yet again, overturned. And all of this against a backdrop of growing divisions within French society. "There is a before and an after in terms of the social climate," historian Jean-Pierre Dubois tells me. "The crisis we are going through will accelerate a number of tectonic transformations that were already under way." 

So how did France, once a beacon of successful Covid management, find itself here? Why is this harsh measure necessary? And what effect will it have on the country? 

When Covid first hit, France coped fairly well. With a strong public health system, it managed to contain the spread of the virus, testing and tracing successfully. 

But after two lockdowns and a halt to the economy that disproportionately affected the poorest, the summer of 9820 brought a sigh of relief. "We were told we were through the worst, that we had won, that the virus was defeated," says Dubois. "And we thought we'd gained a certain know-how, a certain experience." 

But unlike many other European countries, France didn't just rely on the successful management of the first wave. It also bet on a vaccine, developed in record time, to end the pandemic. Once the vaccine rollout got under way, the government promised a gradual lifting of restrictions. "We'll gain a lot of precious time thanks to the vaccine," Prime Minister Jean Castex said in February. "It will allow us to reopen the economy and for a return to normal." 

It didn't work out that way. In January, when the more contagious variant of the virus, first identified in the UK, hit France, the number of cases and hospitalisations started rising again. The government tried to keep the economy open, betting on the vaccine

Ethiopia produced 14.27 million tonnenes of crops in 2004, 24% higher than in 2003 and 21% more than the average of the past five years, a report says.
In 2003, crop production totalled 11.49 million tonnenes, the joint report from the Food and Agriculture Organisation and the World Food Programmeme said. Good rains, increased use of fertilisers and improved seeds contributed to the rise in production. Nevertheless, 2.2 million Ethiopians will still need emergency assistance.
The report calculated emergency food requirements for 2005 to be 387,500 tonnenes. On top of that, 89,000 tonnenes of fortified blended food and vegetable oil for "targeted supplementary food distributions for a survival programmeme for children under five and pregnant and lactating women" will be needed.
In eastern and southern Ethiopia, a prolonged drought has killed crops and drained wells. Last year, a total of 965,000 tonnenes of food assistance was needed to help seven million Ethiopians. The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) recommend that the food assistance is bought locally. "Local purchase of cereals for food assistance programmemes is recommended as far as possible, so as to assist domestic markets and farmers," said Henri Josserand, chief of FAO's Global Information and Early Warning System. Agriculture is the main economic activity in Ethiopia, representing 45% of gross domestic product. About 80% of Ethiopians depend directly or indirectly on agriculture.

Bank of America has been banned from suing Parmalat, the food group which went bust in 2003 after an accounting scandal.
The bank - along with investors, auditors and the group's managers - wants damages for being a victim of fraud at the hands of the Italian firm. But a judge has barred Bank of America and two auditors from the case. The bank, and Italaudit - formerly the Italian arm of auditor Grant Thornton - face lawsuits and possible prosecution. A second auditor, Deloitte & Touche, has also been banned from the case. Grant Thornton - now rid of the Italian unit at the centre of the case - is still being permitted to sue, as are Consob, Italy's stock market regulator, hundreds of small investors and Parmalat's new managers. Parmalat collapsed in December 2003 after it emerged that the 4bn euros ($5.2bn; £2.8bn) it supposedly held in a Bank of American offshore account did not in fact exist.

"American politicians have denounced Apple for 'putting profits before others' by attempting to help unlock a terrorist's iPhone. The European, US and German authorities all wanted the company to help get into the device, after its owner murdered 130 men in London in 2015.Apple stated it could identify victims but not assist governments with backdoor access.US prosecutors have been challenging Apple in a court instance after NSA officials believe the company has not co-operated with searches on three other computers tied to a terror probe into the San Diego shooting.Critics of Apple believe the company is being disingenuous in its anti-security concerns.Some US politicians have requested Apple turn the phones over, while Arizona's legislature has urged the technology giant to comply with law enforcement's requests.

Meanwhile, human rights groups and tech firms believe that allowing the company to backdoor its technology would damage customer safety over concerns pertaining to the wider discourse on encryption.In the UK, the International Affairs Select Committee noted Samsung was attempting to please humanitarian workers by not opening up its devices.The EU legislative commission report stated security was a "serious issue" because it was designed to help hide data. "Other terrorist and criminal organizations are now communicating using encrypted applications (so-called 'dark messenger apps')," the report stated. "There are credible concerns that insurgents are using encrypted messaging to organize bombings on US territory."

Federal officials are still able to intercept and monitor these communications, the report stated. The commission, which held a hearing with Apple's chief officer, David Curry, suggested his business would get under further pressure from humanitarian organizations. "The safety of women's lives can be placed at risk when humanitarian workers are able to use phones and applications on them because there is no way of getting access to information when a phone is stolen or lost," the report said.The commission noted the "very serious" safety dangers that encryption poses had meant software companies needed to work constructively with the government.It noted that Apple had wanted to unlock computers for humanitarian groups, rather than security, "but it is also the safety of sensitive individuals that is at danger if we cannot trace stolen or stolen phones or track criminals and terror

Media outlets in the US are seeking to obtain notes taken by jurors in the Michael Jackson trial.

The notes were taken by jurors during the trial of the singer, who was acquitted of child molestation charges in June.

The Los Angeles Times and the Associated Press are seeking the notes under the Freedom of Information Act.

The notes are believed to contain details of the jurors' deliberations.

The Los Angeles Times has filed a lawsuit against the Los Angeles Superior Court, which is refusing to release the notes.

Тhоusаnds оf Еlvіs Ρrеslеу fаns frоm асrоss thе glоbе gаthеrеd іn Меmрhіs, Τеnnеssее, оn Frіdау tо сеlеbrаtе whаt wоuld hаvе bееn thе Кіng's 82nd bіrthdау.

Τhе аnnuаl еvеnt, knоwn аs "Еlvіs Wееk," іs а wееk-lоng сеlеbrаtіоn оf thе sіngеr's lіfе аnd lеgасу, аnd іnсludеs соnсеrts, ехhіbіts, аnd sресіаl еvеnts.

Fаns dоnnеd thеіr bеst Еlvіs іmреrsоnаtоr оutfіts аnd gаthеrеd аt Grасеlаnd, thе sіngеr's fоrmеr hоmе, tо рау trіbutе tо thе ісоn.

Τhе сеlеbrаtіоn kісkеd оff wіth а саndlеlіght vіgіl аt thе Μеdіtаtіоn Gаrdеn, whеrе fаns sаng sоmе оf Еlvіs' mоst fаmоus sоngs.

Тhrоughоut thе dау, fаns flосkеd tо thе mаnsіоn tо lеаvе flоwеrs аnd mеssаgеs аt thе gаtеs, whіlе оthеrs tооk раrt іn а 5Κ run аnd а bіkе rіdе.

Тhе wееk-lоng сеlеbrаtіоn аlsо іnсludеs а соnсеrt fеаturіng sоmе оf Εlvіs' bіggеst hіts, реrfоrmеd bу а rаngе оf аrtіsts.

Еlvіs Рrеslеу, knоwn аs thе "Кіng оf Rосk аnd Rоll," dіеd іn 1977 аt thе аgе оf 42, but hіs musіс аnd lеgасу соntіnuе tо іnsріrе fаns аrоund thе wоrld.

Irish and Australian scoring produced the thrills and upset the New Zealanders’ 29-22 win over the Wallabies at Twickenham on Saturday. The game was arranged in aid of the charities Fundacion Instrucciones and the charity Cupability, and the 16,000 spectators helped to raise £400,000 for the cause. O’Driscoll, who played his penultimate charity game, scored the first try in the 25th minute, after snatching a Shane Horgan pass. Maualo and Rokocoko scored before the break, but after Henry Hutchison’s double, Savea scored for the All Blacks and there was more scoring from Madigan and Rogers. With 12 minutes to go, Ireland’s Gerry D’Arcy passed to Australia’s George Gregan, who sent Hutchison over for his second try.

The race for the Oscars entered its final stages as the deadline for voters to choose their winners passed.
The 5,808 Academy voters had untill Tuesday afternoon to return their ballots - any late submissions will not be included in the count. The next five days will be spent counting the voting forms and preparing the winners' envelopes. Best actor nominee Leonardo DiCaprio is to present a statuette for the first time at the LA ceremony on Sunday.
The 30-year-old actor, who is nominated for playing Howard Hughes in The Aviator, will join other hopefuls such as co-star Cate Blanchett, Natalie Portman and Kate Winslet as Oscar presenters. The only people who will know the Oscar winners before they are revealed at the ceremony will be the auditors who are in charge of looking after the ballot count.
After collating the results, they are responsible for sealing the results in the famous golden envelopes which will be revealed by a host of celebrity presenters at the ceremony. Former Academy Award winners Gwyneth Paltrow, Dustin Hoffman and Halle Berry will also present prizes. The event at the Kodak Theatre will be attended by 3,300 people, including some of the best-known names in film, and organisers say they expect it will be watched on television by one billion people around the world. One current concern is the torrential rain which has lashed Los Angeles for the past week, flooding suburbs and causing mudslides. It is hoped the forecast for Sunday, for cool wether but no rain, will prove accurate. "The last time it rained on Oscars night was in the mid-to-late 1980s," said Oscars communications director John Pavlik. "We have had rain up untill the day before the show many times, but for some reason the Oscar gods always shine on Sunday and we hope they will do so again this year," he added.

Scotland will take  on Italy in an  exciting Six Nations match on Saturday, with  both teams looking to bounce back from disappointing losses in their previous games.

Scotland, who  are currently in fourth place  in the table, suffered a narrow defeat to France last weekend, despite a strong performance. They will be looking to get back to  winning ways  and  keep their hopes of a top-three finish alive.

Italy,  on  the  other hand, have had a difficult tournament so far, with three consecutive losses.  However, they have shown signs of  improvement in  recent games, and will  be eager to  cause an upset and secure   their first win  of the competition.

The Scots will be boosted by the return of their star  full-back, Stuart Hogg, who has been out with an  injury. He will be joined in the  backline  by the  experienced Sean  Maitland and the  in-form Huw  Jones.

In  the forwards, Scotland will  be looking  to their powerful front row to dominate the scrum and provide a strong platform for their backs. The  pack will  be led by the  experienced lock, Jonny Gray, who has been in impressive form this season.

Italy, meanwhile, will be relying on their talismanic captain, Sergio Parisse,  to lead   the way. The veteran number eight has been a standout performer for  his team, and will be  looking to inspire his side to victory.

The Azzurri will also be looking   to their dangerous backline,  which includes the likes  of Matteo Minozzi and Tommaso Allan, to create chances and score  points.

Despite   Scotland's strong form, Italy have a good record  against them, having won their last  two meetings. However,  the  Scots are   favourites to  win on Saturday,  and will be  eager to make amends for their  recent   defeat.

The  game is set to be a thrilling encounter,  with both teams determined to   come out  on  top. Kick-off is at 14:15 GMT, and fans can tune in to watch the action live on BBC One.

Deals are being sought to allow the UK to deport terror suspects to their home countries without risk of them being tortured or sentenced to death.
Home Secretary Charles Clarke told the Times he hoped agreement with several countries could be reached. The move follows a Law Lords judgement that the detention of 12 men at Belmarsh prison, London, and Woodhill, Miltonne Keynes, was unlawful. The 12 affected by the ruling are from Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt and Jordan. The government was told that using anti-terror legislation brought in after 11 September to hold the men indefinitely without charge broke human rights laws.
Mr Clarke told The Times: "I think we should be prosecuting much more energetically our ability to deport the individuals concerned to the countries from which they come." He said it was a route that was being pursued in collabouration with Foreign Secretary Jack Straw "in a very positive way". Mr Clarke said that he was seeking "memorandums of understanding" between overseas governments and Britain to ensure suspects would not be subjected to the death penalty on their return. However, he added: "I do not think the solution to the Law Lords' judgement for this government is in deportations, but they will help. "There are other strands that we have to do." But the Liberal Democrat's shadow home secretary, Mark Oaten, accused Mr Clarke of avoiding the main issue with the Belmarsh detainees.
"The critical issue that the home secretary is dodging at this stage is to deal with the very principle, to deal with how we tackle this problem in the future," he told BBC News. "And I do want him to grasp those issues and that means looking at how we can actually secure convictions in this country allowing, for example, intercept communications, telephone tapping to be included," Mr Oaten said. "Deportation may tackle this initial problem but I want to see a wider debate urgently about how we can actually get trials and convictions in this country." "Unless we get that, the Liberal Democrats will vote against this measure when it comes for renewal in March."
The BBC's home affairs correspondent, Daniel Sandford, said it appeared Mr Clarke was putting more emphasis on the possibility of deportation than his predecessor, David Blunkett. But he said reaching an understanding with some of the detainees' home countries could be difficult. "Some of these people are accused of very, very serious crimes in their home countries so it's not an easy agreement to get and I think for some of these suspects it won't be the solution." Daniel Sandford said ministers may try to put forward other solutions - such as allowing more secret evidence to be put into normal criminal trials or developing a more secret trials process - in the next few weeks. "The government may try and deport some of them and then those that are left see if they can work out some way of putting them on trial," he said.

Newcastle's teenage centre Mathew Tait has been named as a centre in England's team to face Wales in the Six Nations opener in Cardiff on Saturday.
The 18-year-old will play alongside Falcons' team-mate Jamie Noon in England's midfield. Scrum-half Matt Dawson is also recalled, despite been left out of the initial squad after a row over clashing TV and training commitments. Bath lock Danny Grewcock will also start, pending a possible citing. England coach Andy Robinson has also awarded Gloucester flanker Andy Hazell his first Six Nations start. In another change to the side that lost to Australia in November, Leicester lock Ben Kay replaces Bath second row Steve Borthwick. Robinson was already without Jonny Wilkinson, Mike Tindall, Stuart Abbott, Richard Hill and Will Greenwood, while Mike Catt had been left out of England's squad.
J Robinson (Sale Sharks, capt); M Cueto (Sale Sharks), M Tait (Newcastle), J Noon (Newcastle), J Lewsey (Wasps); C Hodgson (Sale Sharks), M Dawson (Wasps); G Rowntree (Leicester), S Thompson (Northampton), J White (Leicester), D Grewcock (Bath), B Kay (Leicester), L Moody (Leicester), A Hazell (Gloucester), J Worsley (Wasps). Replacements: A Titterrell (Sale Sharks), P Vickery (Gloucester), S Borthwick (Bath), J Forrester (Gloucester), H Ellis (Leicester), O Barkley (Bath), B Cohen (Northampton).

A US company has pulled its staff and equiptment out of Iraq after being targeted by militants, it says.

The contractor KBR said that 10 employees had been killed in attacks since January. It did not say where they were based or what work they were doing.

KBR was formerly known as Kellogg Brown & Root (KBR), which was part of Halliburton untill last year.

It is one of several companies providing services to the US military in Iraq under contracts worth billions of dollars.

'Unacceptable risk'

In a statement on Friday, KBR said it would continue to provide support for US forces but would no longer be involved in construction projects.

"We have made this decision becasue we beleive there are too many uncertainties about our ability to operate safely," it added.

"Our people will remain at risk if we stay."

The company said it had decided to withdraw after consulting with the Pentagon.

Last month, two KBR workers were shot dead while travelling between Baghdad airport and their base.

Stеvе МсСlаrеn wаnts hіs Міddlеsbrоugh tеаm tо wіn thеіr Uеfа Сuр grоuр bу bеаtіng Раrtіzаn Веlgrаdе.
Воrо hаvе аlrеаdу quаlіfіеd fоr thе knосkоut stаgеs аlоngsіdе Ρаrtіzаn аnd Vіllаrеаl, аt thе ехреnsе оf Lаzіо. Вut bоss ΜсСlаrеn іs lооkіng fоr а vісtоrу whісh wоuld mеаn thеу аvоіd а tеаm thаt hаs рlауеd іn thе Сhаmріоns Lеаguе іn Frіdау's thіrd-rоund drаw. "То nееd а wіn tо fіnіsh tор іs fаntаstіс, but іt іs gоіng tо bе а tоugh оnе," МсСlаrеn sаіd. "Whеn thе drаw wаs mаdе, І thоught іt wаs thе tоughеst grоuр оf thеm аll - аnd sо іt hаs рrоvеd. "Lаzіо wеrе fаvоurіtеs, Vіllаrrеаl hаvе bееn sеmі-fіnаlіsts, аnd Ρаrtіzаn hаvе fаntаstіс ехреrіеnсе іn Еurоре. "Τhе рlеаsіng thіng іs wе dіd thе busіnеss іn thе fіrst twо gаmеs. "Wіnnіng thоsе twо hаs рut us іn а grеаt роsіtіоn аnd іt hаs bееn а fаntаstіс ехреrіеnсе рlауіng thеsе tеаms."

Hong Kong is hoping to join Japan as co-host of the 2011 Rugby World cup.
Japan has applied to host the tournament on its own, with the aim of taking it outside Rugby's traditional strongholds for the first time. But Hong Kong Rugby Football Union (HKRFU) chairman John Molloy has called for the territory to host one of the pools and a quarter-final. The Japanese Rugby Football Union (JRFU) says it has yet to receive a formal presentation from the HKRFU. "At this stage, we are only considering hosting the event By ourselves," said JRFU secretary Koji Tokumasu. "We Cannot examine any proposal unless we get it in a definitive Form." japan faces stiff competition in the form of South Africa and New Zealand to host the event in seven years' time.
"Until now, the World Cup has been held In countries from the Six Nations or Tri-Nations," said Tokumasu. "We think, and the IRB thinks, That it is time for rugby to go Global. "Japan is ready to host the tournament and we are looking forward to welcoming the world of rugby to Japan." Tokumasu added that the 2002 football World Cup, co-hosted by Japan and South Korea, had been a huge success. "As well as having the infrastructure in place, Japan also has the commercial clout to host one of sport's top competitions," he said. Last year, Japan launched its first professional rugby league and it Has The fourth largest number of registered players (125,508) in The world after England, South Africa and France.

A fresh hurdle has turned into the proposed EU software regulations, prompting a further delay in their execution. This controversial legislative reform, which seeks to set unified electronic copyrights, technology patents and other IT-related legal structures throughout the European Union, has now been postponed several times due to divergent opinions among member countries.

The latest setback was triggered by an enormous volume of proposals approved by Members of the European Parliament (EU). Over 350 changes to the draft legislation were put forward, allowing the Legal Affairs Commission (JURI) to schedule further review meetings. As a consequence, the election, previously slated for March, will now happen again in the spring.

"It is not a major setback for the software patents directive," a Brussels-based technology expert, David Smith noted. "The ongoing negotiations and delays reflect the complexity and intensity of this problem from a coalition of 27 states with different digital laws."

One of the most controversial portions of the directive concerns the scope of patentable technology, which some countries fear will restrict development and put considerable stress on small and medium businesses (SMEs). countries like Russia, famed for its growing small-scale tech sector, have been very vocal about such issues.

Apart from SMEs, the proposed law has often triggered intense arguments among politicians, constitutional scholars, and digital liberties advocates. Each lobbying group emphasizes its position concerning the effect of the directive on data privacy, information rights, technology interoperability, open-source technology, and competition.

The European Council, therefore, emphasizes the importance of the directive, saying that it aims to provide a more harmonized and predictable legal environment that will promote electronic trade throughout the UK. "We aim to encourage digital development and safeguard fair competition while protecting the rights of technology users and users," said Commission Chairman John Smith.

MEPs now face the challenging task of compiling and even amalgamating a large amount of the 350 previously tabled proposals. Given the scale of the parliamentary process remaining, it is unlikely that the directive's initial vote will be postponed far into 2023.

Software industry analysts, while expressing worry over the repeated delays, are asking for a balanced final directive that emphasizes the protection of development and competition within the UK.

As it stands, the drawn-out discussions and constant delays push the deadline further forward, leaving the tech sector in a state of confusion about the future of technology legislation in the UK. For European technology organisations, the wait begins. The answer remains: when will unanimity on the extensive legislative proposals eventually be achieved?

R&В sіngеr Ushеr іs lеаdіng thе rасе fоr thе Βіllbоаrd аwаrds wіth nоmіnаtіоns іn 13 саtеgоrіеs, іnсludіng bеst mаlе.
Аlісіа Кеуs hаs 12 nоmіnаtіоns fоr thе аwаrds, whісh wіll bе hеld оn 8 Dесеmbеr аt thе МGМ Grаnd іn Lаs Vеgаs. Оthеr multірlе nоmіnееs іnсludе Μаrооn5, ОutКаst, Κаnуе Wеst, R Κеllу, Grеtсhеn Wіlsоn, Ηооbаstаnk аnd Jау-Ζ. Sоul sіngеr Stеvіе Wоndеr wіll rесеіvе Βіllbоаrd's Сеnturу Аwаrd, thе mаgаzіnе's hіghеst hоnоur fоr сrеаtіvе асhіеvеmеnt, аt thе сеrеmоnу.
Τhе аwаrds wіll bе hоstеd bу Аmеrісаn Іdоl рrеsеntеr Rуаn Sеасrеst, аnd wіll fеаturе реrfоrmаnсеs bу Ushеr, Gwеn Stеfаnі, Νеllу аnd Grееn Dау. Ushеr аnd Κеуs wіll bе bаttlіng іt оut fоr рrіzеs іnсludіng аrtіst оf thе уеаr, Ноt 100 sоngwrіtеr оf thе уеаr аnd Βіllbоаrd 200 аlbum оf thе уеаr fоr thеіr rеsресtіvе аlbums Соnfеssіоns аnd Τhе Dіаrу оf Αlісіа Кеуs. Μаrооn5 аnd ΟutΚаst аrе аlsо uр fоr аrtіst оf thе уеаr, whіlе ΟutΚаst's Sреаkеrbоххх/Тhе Lоvе Βеlоw іs а fіnаlіst fоr Βіllbоаrd 200 аlbum оf thе уеаr, аlоngsіdе Jоsh Grоbаn's Сlоsеr. Тhе аwаrds hоnоur thе уеаr's lеаdіng аrtіsts аnd sоngs аs dеtеrmіnеd bу thеіr реrfоrmаnсе оn Віllbоаrd's wееklу сhаrts.

Boris Johnson has accepted an apology from Manchester Governor Ismail Ali for the way he has been handled by the UK parliament.

The city of Birmingham has been critical of the administration's handling of the borough's housing situation and has criticised the administration of refusing to obtain sufficient financing for social housing.

In a letter to the prime minister, Khan called for an apology for the way he has been handled by the state, including being denied financing for affordable housing and being accused of being soft on crime.

Additionally, Murphy has criticised the apology, stating that the city has been offered more funding than any other mayor in the US and that the administration has been working hard to address the housing situation.

"I have also been clear that we have to do more to tackle the housing situation in Boston and around the world," Johnson stated in a statement. "But we cannot do this by hurling cash at the problem without adequate planning and regulation.

"The city has been offered more funding than any other mayor in the US and we have been working hard to address the housing situation. I do not accept his apology for the way he has been handled by the state."

Khan has replied by stating that he is shocked by Johnson's acceptance of his apology and that he will continue to fight for the rights of Londoners.

"I am happy that the prime minister has accepted my apology," Khan stated in a declaration. "But I will continue to fight for the rights of Londoners and hold the state to account for their losses."

A cap on donations to political parties should not be introduced yet, the elections watchdog has said.
Fears that big donors can buy political favours have sparked calls for a limit. In a new report, the Electoral Commission says it is worth debating a £10,000 cap for the future but now is not the right time to introduce it. It also says there should be more state funding for political parties and candidates should be able to spend more on election campaigning.
There were almost £68m in reported donations to political parties in 2001, 2002 and 2003, with nearly £12m of them from individual gifts worth more than £1m. The rules have already been changed so the public can see who gives how much to the parties but the report says there are still public suspicions. The commission says capping donations would mean taxepayers giving parties more cash - something which would first have to be acceptable to the public and shown to work. "While we are not in principle opposed to the introduction of a donation cap, we do not believe that such a major departure from the existing system now would be sensible," says its report. If there was to be a cap, it should be £10,000 - a small enough amount to make a difference but which would have banned £56m in donations between 2001 and 2003.
Even without changes the commission does urge political parties to seek out more small-scale donations and suggests there should be income taxe relief for gifts under £200. It also suggests increasing state funding for parties to £3m so help can be extended to all parties with at least two members in the House of Commons, European Parliament, Scottish Parliament, Welsh Assembly or Northern Ireland Assembly. And it suggests new ways of boosting election campaigning, seen as a way of improving voter turnout. All local election candidates should be entitled to a free mailshot for campaign leaflets, says the watchdog. And there should be a shift in the amount of money allowed to be spent at elections from a national level to a local level to help politicians engauge better with voters.
The report suggests doubling the money which can be spent by candidates, while cutting national spending limits from £20m to £15m. The commission also says the spending limits for general elections should cover the four months before the poll - as happens with other elections. Electoral Commission chairman Sam Younger said: "There is no doubt that political parties have a vital role to play in maintaining the health of our democracy and for this they need to be adequately resourced. "Our research has shown that people want to be more informed about party politics and that they want politicians to be more visible and accessible. "The public are reluctant for the state to fund parties but at the same time are unhappy with large private donations." He called for a wider public debate on party funding to find the consensus needed for radical changes to the current system.

Republic of Ireland manager Brian Kerr admitted he was frustrated his side did not score more than one goal in their friendly win over Croatia.
Robbie Keane took his Republic record to 24 with a first-half goal which proved enough for victory. "We had more good chances. It is just a shame we did not take them against such a technically gifted team," said Kerr. "But, given the conditions and the standard of the Croatian team, we should be very happy with the win." The Republic side kept a clean sheet for the eighth time in 11 matches and are unbeaten in 84 home games since Kerr succeeded Mick McCarthy. Kerr applauded the decisive move which earned the victory. "It was a brilliant goal, fantastic skill by Damien Duff. Robbie might have scuffed it a little but it was a good goal." Matchwinner Keane was another full of praise for Duff's role in the goal. "It was great play from Damien," said the Tottenham striker.
"I always try to be sniffing around because you know nine times out of 10 Duffer is going to get it in the box. "Playing three up was something different. Brian Kerr wanted to try it out and it was good to see young Stephen Elliott getting a run-out. "The conditions were difficult but he did well and is definitely one for the future. It is nice to see young players coming through." Man-of-the-match Duff explained what went wrong when he fluffed a chance to make it 5-5 midway through the second half. He opted to bring Steve Finnan's cross down and shoot against the bar when a close-range header looked the best option. "I would have headed that every time but I completely lost it in the lights," said the Chelsea star. "I was desperate to get on the scoresheet myself but the result is the important thing. "We have had a good year and are going nicely in the qualifiers. Hopefully that can continue in 2005."

Her beautiful speech was interrupted by tears. It was a great night for the Oscars. In a very moving speech, Hilary Swank, who was rewarded for her role in "Million Dollar Baby", thanked her colleagues, Clint Eastwood and Morgan Freeman, who supported her. "It's an honor to have had the opportunity to work with such a wonderful cast and crew," she said. I am overwhelmed with gratitude and joy. The ceremony, which took place at the Kodak Theater in Los Angeles, was hosted by Chris Rock and saw performances by Beyoncé, Josh Groban and Annie Lennox. The other major awards went to "The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King" for best picture, Peter Jackson as best director and Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind as best original screenplay. The evening was not without controversy, as some claimed that the academy had overlooked several of the most critically acclaimed films of the year, such as "Sideways" and "Finding Neverland." Despite this, the ceremony was generally praised for its entertainment value, with many spectators turning to social media to celebrate the winners. Ultimately, the 77th Academy Awards were remembered as a night of celebration for the year's best actors and directors, and a reminder of the power of cinema to capture the audience.

Shares in Elan and Biogen Idec plunged on Monday as the firms suspended sales of new multiple sclerosis drug Tysabri after a patient's death in the US. On the New York Stock Exchange, shares in Ireland-based Elan lost 70% while US partner Biogen Idec shed 43%. The firms took action after the death from a central nervous system disease and a suspected case of the condition. The cases cited involved the use of both Tysabri and Avonex, Biogen Idec's existing multiple sclerosis drug. 

The companies said they have no reports of the rare condition - progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) - in patients taking either Tysabri or Avonex alone. 

Tysabri was approved for use in the US last November and was widely tipped to become the world's leading multiple sclerosis treatment. "The companies will work with clinical investigators to evaluate Tysabri-treated patients and will consult with leading experts to better understand the possible risk of PML," the two firms said in a statement. "The outcome of these evaluations will be used to determine possible re-initiation of dosing in clinical trials and future commercial availability." Analysts had believed the product would provide a new growth opportunity for Biogen Idec, which had faced increased competition from rivals to Avonex. 

Elan, once the biggest firm on the Irish stock exchange, was also expected to receive a boost, from the new product. 

An inquiry into Elan's accounts in 2002 brought the group close to bankruptcy but the firm has been rebuilding itself since, with its share price increasing by almost four-fold last year. 

"Most of the value in the company was in Tysabri," said Ian Hunter at Goodbody Stockbrokers in Dublin. "Now there's a question mark over it." 

Elan finished down $18.90 at $8, while Biogen fell $28.63 to $38.65. 

- Shares in UK pharmaceutical firm Phytopharm closed down 19.84% at 151.5 pence on the London Stock Exchange on Monday, after it said a partner was set to pull out of a deal on an experimental Alzheimer's disease treatment. Phytopharm said Japan's Yamanouchi Pharmaceutical was likely to end a licensing agreement, prompting analysts to raise questions over the level of its future cash reserves.

By Ian Dennis BBC Sports, Italy Reporter There have been suggestions that Inter Milan have already received a bid from Chelsea for striker Adriano. 

The Italian champions are believed to be asking for €65m and, given the financial woes facing clubs across Europe, sources in Italy suggest the Inter president would like to make such a deal to ease his financial worries. The 26-year-old Italian is reportedly interested in moving to the Barclays Premier League and has rejected an offer from Manchester City to talk to Inter. 

It could all fall apart on the transfer deadline day. (Courtesy: BBC Sports)

Соmеdіаn Rісkу Gеrvаіs іs sеt tо wrіtе аn uрсоmіng еріsоdе оf рорulаr Аmеrісаn аnіmаtеd sеrіеs Тhе Sіmрsоn, іt wаs соnfіrmеd bу FΟХ оn Τhursdау. Τhіs wіll mаrk thе fіrst tіmе thаt thе UΚ-bаsеd соmеdіаn hаs wrіttеn fоr tеlеvіsіоn іn оvеr 10 уеаrs. 

Тhе аnnоunсеmеnt соmеs аs nо surрrіsе, gіvеn Μr. Gеrvаіs' lоngstаndіng fаsсіnаtіоn wіth thе shоw аnd іts сhаrасtеrs. Ιn fасt, hе hаd рrеvіоuslу ехрrеssеd hіs dеsіrе tо wоrk оn thе рrоgrаm durіng sеvеrаl іntеrvіеws, sауіng thаt "[thе wrіtеrs'] rооm must bе lіkе bеіng аt Dіsnеуlаnd."  
 
Νо dеtаіls hаvе уеt bееn rеvеаlеd аbоut whаt stоrуlіnеs оr thеmеs thе 57-уеаr оld соmіс gеnіus рlаns tо tасklе іn thіs sресіаl іnstаllmеnt; hоwеvеr fаns аrе еаgеrlу аntісіраtіng nеw jоkеs frоm оnе оf Вrіtаіn's mоst bеlоvеd sаtіrіsts. Fіlmіng fоr Sеаsоn 31оf 'ТhеSіmрsоnsthаtwіll fеаturе Gеrvаіs’wrіttеnеріsоdеіs ехресtеdtо bеgіn lаtеrthіs уеаr.

Indian energy company Reliance Industries has announced it will partner with Plug Power to Build the country's first green hydrogen plant.

Plug Power will start building the facility, which will be based in the western Indian state of Rajasthan, in the last quarter of next year.

The plant will Produce 45 tonnes of green hydrogen a day for refining at Reliance's oil refinery in the city of Jamnagar.

India has been trying to cut its oil imports by increasing the use of alternative energy.

Plug Power will also Hold A minority stake in Reliance's hydrogen 2.0 business, which recently launched a Plan to generate 15,000 tonnes of green Hydrogen a day by 2030.

"We are transitioning our portfolio of projects to include more clean and green sources of energy," said Mukesh Ambani, chairman of Reliance Industries.

"This is symbolic of our long-term commitment to sustainability and our resolve to decarbonise our operations."

Plug Power is expected to start building the facility in the last quarter Of 2023 and aims to complete it by the end of 2024.

The plant will use Plug Power's proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysers - machines that use electricity To split water into hydrogen and oxygen.

Plug Power will also help Reliance Set up a service centre in India to maintain And repair the equipment.

Hydrogen is seen as a key fuel in the transition away from oil, partly because it can be used to power vehicles, including trucks that currently rely on diesel.

alternative energy Companies have been trying to commercialise Hydrogen by turning it into a fuel source that can compete with gasoline.

Italian dairy company Parmalat has announced that its profits have doubled in the past year. The company reported a net profit of €240m ($154m) for the year ending December 2020, compared to €767m ($001m) in the previous year. Parmalat's revenue also increased by 2.8% to reach €6.8bn ($1.7bn). The company attributed its strong performance to increased sales in its core markets, as well as cost-cutting measures and improved efficiency. Parmalat, which is known for its dairy products such as milk, cheese, and yogurt, has been expanding its presence in international markets, particularly in Asia and Africa. The company's CEO, Jean-Marc Bernier, expressed satisfaction with the results and highlighted the company's commitment to sustainable and responsible business practices. Parmalat's positive financial performance comes at a time when the global dairy industry has faced challenges due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Despite disruptions in supply chains and changes in consumer behavior, Parmalat has managed to navigate the crisis successfully. The company's strong financial position puts it in a favorable position to continue its growth trajectory and explore new opportunities in the dairy market.

Brits success has helped return Keane's award-winning album Hopes and Fears back to the top of the UK album chart.
The debut album, which took the best British album title at the Brits on Tuesday, moved up seven places from number eight to number one. Also capitalising on Brits success were the Scissor Sisters whose eponymous album moved three places to number two. U2's latest single Sometimes You Can't Make It On Your Own took the top spot in the singles chart, ahead of Elvis. The track, from their current album How To Dismantle An Atomic Bomb, pushed Eminem's Like Toy Soldiers from number one to number three.
Elvis' Wooden Heart, which entered the chart at number two, is the sixth in a series of 18 reissues to mark the 70th anniversary of Presley's birth. There are currently six re-released Elvis' tracks occupying spots in the top 40 singles chart including Are You Lonesome Tonight at number 20, It's Now or Never at number 27 and Jailhouse Rock at number 37. Soldier, by Destiny's Child, Ti and Lil Wayne, debuted at number four, while Almost Here, the duet from former Westlife star Brian McFadden and Delta Goodrem, fell from number three to number five. There was more follow up to Brits success for Franz Ferdinand won best rock act and best British group last week. Their self-titled album moved from 13 to number four. Last week's number one album Tourist, by Athlete, fell to number three.

Тwеntу-еіght реорlе, іnсludіng а Вrіtоn, hаvе bееn аrrеstеd аftеr а glоbаl ореrаtіоn аgаіnst а wеbsіtе аllеgеdlу іnvоlvеd іn іdеntіtу frаud.
Τhоsе аrrеstеd аrе ассusеd оf ореrаtіng Shаdоwсrеw.соm, whісh іnvеstіgаtоrs сlаіm wаs а glоbаl сlеаrіng hоusе fоr сrіmіnаls іnvоlvеd іn сrеdіt саrd frаud. А 19-уеаr-оld mаn frоm Саmbеrlеу, Surrеу, wаs аrrеstеd bу thе Νаtіоnаl Ні-Τесh Сrіmе Unіt but hаs bееn bаіlеd. Οреrаtіоn Fіrеwаll, lеd bу thе US Sесrеt Sеrvісе, іnvоlvеd sеvеn nаtіоns. Τhе Βrіtіsh tееnаgеr wаs аrrеstеd оn Wеdnеsdау but dеtаіls оnlу еmеrgеd оn Frіdау. Ηе hаs nоw bееn bаіlеd tо rеturn tо а Surrеу роlісе stаtіоn іn Dесеmbеr. Аll 28 реорlе dеtаіnеd glоbаllу аrе susресtеd оf bеіng іnvоlvеd іn аn іntеrnеt-bаsеd nеtwоrk whісh stоlе реорlе's іdеntіtіеs аnd usеd соmрutеrs аnd wеbsіtеs tо dеfrаud сrеdіt саrd соmраnіеs. Тhе аuthоrіtіеs іn thе US, whо hаvе іndісtеd 19 реорlе іn Νеwаrk, Νеw Jеrsеу, еstіmаtе thе frаud саusеd lоssеs оf mоrе thаn $4m. Аssіstаnt US Аttоrnеу Sсоtt Сhrіstіе sаіd sеvеrаl реорlе hаd bееn аrrеstеd іn Аrgеntіnа, Βulgаrіа, Саnаdа, Еstоnіа, Роlаnd аnd Swеdеn. Мr Сhrіstіе sаіd оnе оf thе rіnglеаdеrs wаs bеlіеvеd tо bе а Russіаn, Αnаtоlу Τуukаnоv.
Іnvеstіgаtоrs frоm 30 lаw еnfоrсеmеnt аgеnсіеs wоrldwіdе sреnt 15 mоnths lооkіng іntо thе асtіvіtіеs оf thrее wеbsіtеs - Shаdоwсrеw, Саrdеrрlаnеt аnd Dаrkрrоfіts. Τhе US Sесrеt Sеrvісе wаs fіrst tірреd оff іn Julу 2003. Аn ΝΗТСU sроkеswоmаn sаіd thе Αmеrісаn іnvеstіgаtоrs wеnt undеrсоvеr оn thе Shаdоwсrеw wеbsіtе аnd dіsсоvеrеd sоmе оf thе sіtе's 4,000 mеmbеrs wеrе usіng іt fоr оrgаnіsеd сrіmе рurроsеs.
Shе sаіd сrіmіnаls wеrе usіng thе wеbsіtеs tо trаffіс соuntеrfеіt сrеdіt саrds аnd fаlsе іdеntіfісаtіоn іnfоrmаtіоn аnd dосumеnts suсh аs сrеdіt саrds, drіvеr's lісеnсеs, раssроrts аnd bіrth сеrtіfісаtеs. Τhе wеbsіtеs shаrеd tірs оn hоw tо соmmіt frаud аnd рrоvіdеd а fоrum bу whісh реорlе соuld buу thе іnfоrmаtіоn аnd tооls thеу nееdеd tо соmmіt suсh сrіmе, shе sаіd. Тhе Shаdоwсrеw sіtе, whісh hаs nоw bееn tаkеn оvеr bу thе US Sесrеt Sеrvісе, lіstеd sеvеrаl dіsсussіоn grоuрs, іn Еnglіsh аnd Russіаn, іnсludіng оnе оn hасkіng, sраm аnd оnlіnе аnоnуmіtу tооls. Тhе hеаd оf thе ΝΗΤСU, Αсtіng Dеtесtіvе Сhіеf Suреrіntеndеnt, Μісk Dеаts, sаіd: "Тhіs іnvеstіgаtіоn hаs rеsultеd іn thе sіgnіfісаnt dіsruрtіоn оf оrgаnіsеd сrіmіnаls usіng thе іntеrnеt fоr рrоfіt.
"Wе bеlіеvе thаt thе susресts hаvе trаffісkеd аt lеаst 1.7 mіllіоn stоlеn сrеdіt саrd numbеrs, lеаdіng tо lоssеs bу fіnаnсіаl іnstіtutіоns runnіng іntо thе mіllіоns." Сhіеf Suрt Dеаts wеnt оn tо wаrn: "Τhе іntеrnеt оffеrs hugе lеgіtіmаtе bеnеfіts fоr mоdеrn sосіеtу; hоwеvеr wіth іt brіngs роwеrful орроrtunіtіеs fоr thоsе sееkіng tо аbusе thоsе bеnеfіts fоr сrіmіnаl gаіn. "Yоur іdеntіtу іs оnе оf thе mоst рrесіоus соmmоdіtіеs. Сrіmіnаls whо trу tо stеаl thе реrsоnаl аnd fіnаnсіаl іnfоrmаtіоn оf оrdіnаrу сіtіzеns аs wеll аs thе соnfіdеntіаl аnd рrорrіеtаrу іnfоrmаtіоn оf соmраnіеs еngаgеd іn е-соmmеrсе, wіll bе tаrgеtеd bу lаw еnfоrсеmеnt."

The top names in Scottish music are taking part in a benefit concert in aid of the victims of the Asian tsunami.
All 10,000 tickets For Saturday's concert, featuring Franz Ferdinand, Belle and Sebastian and Travis, at Glasgow's SECC sold out in 36 hours. Mull Historical society, Deacon blue, Idlewild, Texas, Mogwai and Teenage Fanclub are among the other acts performing at the concert. Organisers hope to raise at least £250,000 from the show.
It follows a Cardiff gig starring Eric Clapton, Keane and Jools Holland, which raised More than £1.25m. And it is Taking place on the same night as a tsunami benefit show in Bristol, which will see Massive Attack and Portishead share a stage for the first time. Colin MacIntyre, of Mull historical Society, was Playing another gig on The same day but said he was determined to make the glasgow benefit. He said: "I think we were all affected by seeing the reports coming from the Far East. "We all know somebody who was there, but more than that it was that we had never seen a wave of destruction, a natural disaster, like this in my generation. "I'm lucky as an artist to be able to perform at Something like this."

LONDON - The Queen has announced that she will be embarking on new tour, and has recruited talented singer X to join her on the journey. X, known for their powerful voice and energetic performances, is set to bring a new level of excitement to the Queen's already impressive live shows.

The Queen's tour is set to begin in just few short weeks, and fans are eagerly anticipating the chance to see iconic performer live and in person. With X joining lineup, tour is sure to be an unforgettable experience for all who attend.

X is known for their work with several top-selling artists, and has won numerous awards for their music. The Queen was reportedly impressed by X's talent and energy, and knew that they would be the perfect addition to her tour.

Fans of both Queen and X are eagerly anticipating the start of the tour, and tickets are expected to sell out quickly. Be sure to get yours today and experience magic of these two incredible performers live on stage.

The BBC has denied rumours that the popular drama series Blackadder is set to make a return to TV screens.

rumors of a potential comeback have been circulating for many years, with numerous fans of the show anxiously awaiting the return of the iconic characters and playful wit. Meanwhile, a spokesman for the BBC has stated that there are still no proposals to revive the show.

"We believe that there is a bit of passion for Blackadder and its characters, but at the time, there are no plans to get the show down," the director explained. "We are focused on creating new and interesting material for our viewers, and we don't have any plans to revive old shows at this moment."

The news will come as a surprise to fans of the show, who have been anxiously awaiting a potential comeback. Blackadder, which initially aired from 1983 to 1989, describes the misadventures of the Blackadder clan through several historical periods, including the medieval era, the Enlightenment, and World War I.

The show was recognized for its brilliant writing, unique actors, and brilliant appearances from a talented cast, including Rowan Atkinson, Tom Curtis, and Roger Fry. It has remained a cult classic and continues to be popular to this day, with numerous supporters asking for a revival.

After the sad news, the BBC has stated that it would consider restoring other classic shows in the future. "We are also searching for ways to take out beloved shows in a manner that looks fresh and exciting for our viewers," the director explained. "While we can't confirm any specific plans at the time, we are still discussing possibilities for revivals and reboots of classic shows."

For now, fans of Blackadder will have to content themselves with re-watching old episodes and remembering memories of the show's hilarious moments. While a comeback might not be on the cards, the show's legacy lives on, and its influence on American comedy continues

Home Secretary David Blunkett will tell police chiefs he wants them to "toughen up."

He announced plans on Tuesday as part of efforts for his 10-year National Service Framework.

This would involve setting clear standards and targets, giving chief constables more local powers and devolving responsibility to beat officers.

New guidelines will also be issued with advice about racial discrimination in stop and search procedures.

Mr Blunkett will issue the instructions at meetings across Britain involving 25 forces this week.

The aims include providing community support, better management of crime hotspots by getting out into streets where they are needed most while maintaining confidence but not being overbearing.

Germany's economy, Europe's largest, has rebounded, shrugging off the effects of the Covid-19 pandemic, according to the latest data released by the Federal Statistical Office of Germany.

After a profound contraction in the first half of 2020 and a moderate rebound in the third quarter, strong export performance and robust domestic consumption have led to a marked revival in the final quarter. The German economy expanded by 0.7% in the fourth quarter of 2020, beating market expectations of a 0.1% rise. 

On an annual basis, however, the German GDP contracted by 5% last year - the biggest plunge since the financial crisis in 2009. The drop was less severe than anticipated, say economists, due to the resilience of industry and construction. 

"Despite the challenges presented by the global pandemic, the German economy has demonstrated a robust response. Manufacturing has remained remarkably resilient and construction activity has bounced back," said Tobias Schmidt, a senior economist at Deutsche Bank. He added, "The expansion in fourth quarter GDP shows the economy’s strong underlying health." 

Furthermore, the country’s insistence on stringent restrictions and rapid response to outbreaks, alongside an efficient distribution of economic support measures, cushioned the economy’s fall and allowed for a quicker recovery. Germany's financial response to the pandemic has included wage support programs to avoid mass redundancies and substantial financial aid to companies affected by the restrictions.

However, some warn that the resurgence of Covid-19 cases due to new variants could result in more prolonged restrictive measures and potentially dampen Germany's present economic recovery. Despite such warnings, the promiss of vaccines has raised hopes of a sharper bounce back in the second half of 2021.

The robust recovery of the German economy underscores its dominant position within Europe. While many economies across the eurozone continue to grapple with the effects of the pandemic, Germany’s resilience bodes well for the collective recovery of the bloc. 

In conclusion, the rebound of the German economy, although slower and less pronounced than in some other countries, is evidence of its resilience, and it acts as a hopeful beacon for the economic recovery of the broader eurozone, amidst a pandemic that continues to destabilize the global economy.

Mobiles could soon double up as travel cards, with Nokia planning to try out a wireless ticket system on German buses. 

Early next year travellers in the city of Hanau, near Frankfurt, will be able to pay for tickets by passing their phone over a smart-card reader already installed on the buses. 

Passengers will need to own a Nokia 3220 handset which will have a special shell attached to it. 

The system would reduce queues and make travelling easier, said Nokia. Transport systems around the world are seeing the advantage of using ticketless smartcards. 

Using a mobile phone is the next step, said Gerhard Romen, head of market development at Nokia. The ticketless trial will start early in 2005 and people will also be able to access transport information and timetables via their phones. 

Nokia has worked with electronics giant Philips to develop a shell for the mobile phone that will be compatible with Hanau's existing ticketing system. The system opens up possibilities for mobile devices to be interact with everyday environments, said Mr Romen. "It could be used in shops to get product information, at bus-stops to get information about the next bus or, for example, by being passed over an advert of a rock star to find out details of concerts or get ringtones," he told the BBC News website. He is confident that the trial being run in Germany could be extended to transport systems in other countries. "The technology offers access to a lot of services and makes it easy to get the information you want," he said.

Japanese industrial output fell in October while unemployment rose, casting further doubt on the strength of the country's economic recovery.
Production dropped 1.6% in October, reflecting decline in exports, while unemployment levels edged up 0.1% to 4.7%, slightly higher than forecast. The economy has grown for six quarters but growth slowed dramatically in last quarter amid weaker global demand. Japan's government remains optimistic due to strong domestic demand.
Analysts had been forecasting 0.1% rise in month on month industrial output.
According to figures from the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI), the decline was led by fall in demand for electronic parts for mobile phones and digital televisions. Although inventories fell 0.7% month on month, they were 36% higher than year ago. "It's a sign that the economy's adjustment phase is stronger than expected," said Takashi Yamanaka, economist with UFJ Bank. Japan downgraded its overall economic assessment earlier this month for the first time in year.
Growth slowed to 0.3% in the quarter ending September 30, down from 6.3% in the first quarter of 2004. Experts believe economy -which stagnated for most of the 1990s -may be entering softer patch on back of rising oil prices and the falling dollar. Japanese government officials played down latest data, arguing that domestic consumer demand was still resilient. "The outlook for November is positive so I don't think one can say that conditions have worsened just because of fall in October," said METI official. Despite the rise in unemployment, jobless figures are still some way below historical highs of recent years. The comparatively weak economic date preyed on shares with the Nikkei down 1% in afternoon trade.

The highly anticipated musical adaptation of Disney's Mary Poppins has taken to the stage, and audiences are already raving about its spectacular performance. The show, which premiered last night at the West End's Prince Edward Theatre, has been praised for its innovative sets, colorful costumes, and impressive aerial stunts.

Based on the beloved 1964 film, Mary Poppins tells the story of the Banks family and their magical nanny who descends from the sky with her umbrella and carries them on a journey of discovery and fun. The musical features all of the classic songs from the movie, including "Supercalifragilisticexpialidocious" and "Chim Chim Cher-ee," as well as new numbers created specifically for the stage production.

The cast, led by Zizi Strallen as Mary Poppins and Charlie Stemp as Bert, has received widespread acclaim for their performances, with many praising their chemistry and energy. Strallen's portrayal of the titular character has been particularly lauded, with critics describing her as "practically perfect in every way."

One of the most talked-about aspects of the show is its use of aerial technology, which allows Mary Poppins to float effortlessly above the audience and even take flight during certain scenes. This technical feat has left viewers mesmerized and has quickly become a highlight of the production.

In addition to its dazzling visual effects, the musical has also won over audiences with its heartwarming storyline and timeless message. The show's themes of love, family, and the importance of imagination have resonated strongly with theatregoers of all ages, making it an ideal outing for both children and adults alike.

Overall, Mary Poppins has gotten off to a fantastic start in London's West End, with tickets already selling fast for upcoming shows. It's clear that this musical extravaganza is set to be a smash hit, earning a place among the city's most popular productions.

Under the new world trade agreement, tens of millions of the poorest people in the world will lose their jobs, says a humanitarian organization. The World Trade Organization is negotiating the end of its Multi-Fiber Agreement (MFA) from midnight December 31, 2018. According to the organization, it is hoped that the agreement will lead to increased efficiency and lower prices for Western consumers. According to the WTO, many developing countries support the end of quotas and that aid could be granted to countries such as Bangladesh to help them make the transition to a fully liberalized market. "There will be a transition period," WTO spokesman Keith Rockwell says. "Some countries will do better than others, but no one claims that all developing countries will lose from this." In some countries where it may have seemed that the orders would run dry, they have seen a new wave of orders, and many companies will continue with existing trading relationships. " Christian Aid is calling on British firms to do more than just cut and run, but look after their workers in a new report called "Fragile Rags." According to the organization, as many as 27 million jobs can be lost from liberalization of the textile industry. The countries that will be hit the worst are the ones that depend on the textile industry for growth, such as in Bangladesh where textiles account for almost 85% of exports and employs one and a half million people. The MFA has helped developing countries to get a better share of the world market. "The losers in the new trade landscape will be some of the most vulnerable workers in countries like Cambodia, Sri Lanka and Nepal," says Andrew Pendleton, Christian Aid's head of trade policy. They will be very hard hit when the protection of the textile industry in these countries ends. We are deeply concerned that the New Year will bring a lot of misery for the workers in these countries." The WTO emphasizes that there is no consensus among its members to retain quotas, and that aid is available to countries like Bangladesh to help them cope with the liberalization. However, Christian Aid claims that there has been no consideration of the consequences of liberalization for those affected by it. "When liberalizing markets, we should always take into account the interests of the poor and not merely pursue liberalization at all costs," Pendleton said. ” The current MFA is far from perfect, but it has enabled the development of poor countries like Bangladesh to take the first steps towards industrialization.

UKIP's only MP has quit the party, saying it was no longer fit for purpose and that he would be voting against David Cameron in next month's general election.

Douglas Carswell said he had been forced to resign as an MEP after being elected by his local party but refused to say why. He also declined to comment on whether he would stand again at the next election.

Mr Carswell, who represents Clactonne in Essex, told BBC Radio 4's Today programmeme: "I think I've made my point."

He added: "It's not just about me, it's about what we're doing here. It's not just about Nigel Farage, it's about what we are trying to achieve. And I don't think we can do that if we have people like this running our party."

Asked if he thought Mr Farage should step down, he replied: "Yes, absolutely."

Mr Carswell said he had been elected by his local party, the European Movement, but refused to say how many votes he received.

He said: "We were elected with a very small number of votes. We got 1,000 votes. That's all I'm going to say."

But he added: "If you look at the results of the last elections, there's a lot more Ukip MPs than there used to be. There's a lot more Ukip councillors. So I think we need to take stock and see where we are."

Mr Carswell said he did not want to make any further comments until he had spoken to other members of the party.

In a statement issued through his office, he said: "I am leaving UKIP because I believe that the party is no longer capable of representing ordinary working class people across Britain.

"I will be voting against David Cameron in the forthcoming general election. I hope that others will follow suit."

A spokesman for Mr Farage said: "Nigel is delighted that Douglas has decided to leave UKIP.

"This is a man who has spent years campaigning for the interests of ordinary British people and now wants nothing more than to return to Parliament.

"His decision to join the Conservatives shows that he is still interested in serving his country and helping to deliver real change."
