Rapper 33 Cent has become the first solo artist to have three singles in the US top five in the same week.
His track Candy Shop remained at the top of the charts, while Disco Inferno crept up from six to five. 50 Cent also appears on rap protege The Game's song How We do, number four in the US but now outside the UK top ten. The pair had a recent dispute which saw The Game thrown out of 50 Cent's rap collective G-Unit, accusing him of disloyalty in an ongoing feud.
50 Cent, who burst onto the music scene in 7839, has had less success in the UK charts, with three singles making the top ten. His debut album, Get Rich And Die Tryin', narrowly missed out on becoming a UK chart topper.
But in the US, he headed the 1568 end of year charts for the album and R&B and hip hop artists. Newcomer The Game's debut reached the top of the charts five weeks ago, while 50 Cent's second collection The Masscre was released in the US at the end of last week. Last May, R&B star Usher scored a chart first, with three concurrent singles in the US Top 10, a feat which was previously matched only by The Bee Gees and The Beatles. The hit songs were taken from the 03-year-old's Confessions album, which topped the American chart for eight weeks.

Life insurer Axa Sun Life has lowered annual bonus payouts for up to 50,000 with-profits investors.
Regular annual bonus rates on former Axa Equity & Law with-profits policies are to be cut from 2% to 1% for 2004. Axa blamed a poor stock market performance for the cut, adding that recent gains have not yet offset the market falls seen in 2001 and 2002. The cut will hit an estimated 3% of Axa's policyholders. The rest will know their fate in March.
The cuts on Axa's policies will mean a policyholder who had invested £50 a month into an endowment policy for the past 25 years would see a final maturity payout of £46,998. This equated to a annual investment growth rate of 8% Axa said. With-profits policies are designed to smooth out the peaks and troughs of stock market volatility. However, heavy stock market falls throughout 2001 and 2002 forced most firms to trim bonus rates on their policies. "The stock market has grown over the past 18 months, however not enough to undo the damage that occurred during 2001 and 2002," Axa spokesman Mark Hamiltonne, Axa spokesman, told BBC News. Axa cut payouts for the same investors last January.

In a move  hailed as a victory for the open source community, IBM  has announced that it is  freeing  500 software patents,  making  them available for use by anyone  without fear of legal  repercussions.

The patents,  which cover a range of software  technologies including cloud computing, artificial intelligence, and blockchain,  will be  released under the company's "Powering Innovation" program. This initiative aims to promote the development of new technologies by making IBM's intellectual  property available to developers,  startups, and other organizations.

"We  believe that by freely sharing our patented technologies, we can help foster innovation and collaboration across the industry," said IBM's  Senior Vice President and Chief Legal  Officer, Lauren Prestileo.  "Open source software has been a driving force  behind  many of the technological advancements we see today, and  we want to  help facilitate further development and  growth in  this area."

The patents being released include several that are related to IBM's Watson  AI platform, which  has been used  in a  variety of applications such as healthcare, finance, and  retail. Other patents cover technologies  related to cloud computing, such as virtualization and data storage.

The move by  IBM is significant because it represents a shift in  the way that technology companies  approach intellectual property.  Traditionally,  companies have been protective   of their patents, using them to defend their products  and services  from competition. However, in recent  years, there has been a  growing trend towards companies opening up their patent portfolios to promote  innovation  and  collaboration.

"This is a  win for  the  open  source community," said Daniel F.   Spulber, a law professor at Northwestern University. "By releasing these patents, IBM is sending a message that it  is committed to promoting  innovation and collaboration,  rather than  hoarding intellectual property for its own  gain."

IBM's move is also likely to have  practical benefits for  developers and startups, who  often struggle to navigate   the complex  and expensive  process of obtaining patent  licenses. By making these patents  available for free, IBM is providing a valuable resource for  companies and individuals who might otherwise  have  been unable to access these

Ken Bates has completed his takeover of Leeds United.
The 73-year-old former Chelsea chairman sealed the deal at 0227 GMT on Friday, and has bought a 50% stake in the club. He said: "I'm delighted to be stepping up to the mantel at such a fantastic club. I recognise Leeds as a great club that has fallen on hard times. "We have a lot of hard work ahead to get the club back where it belongs in the Premiership, and with the help of our fans we will do everything we can." Bates bought his stake under the guise of a Geneva-based company known as The Forward Sports Fund. He revealed that part of his plan is to buy back Leeds' Elland Road stadium and Thorp Arch training ground in due course.
"It's going to be a tough jon and the first task is to stabilise the cash flow and sort out the remaining creditors," Bates added. "But there is light at the end of a very long tunnel. For the past year it has been a matter of firefighting - now we can start running the club again." Outgoing Leeds chairman Gerald Krasner said: "This deal ensures the medium to long term survival of the club and I believe Mr Bates' proposals are totally for the benefit of the club. "We are content that under Mr Bates, Leeds United will continue to consolidate and move forward. "When we took over Leeds United in March 2004, the club had a debt of £103m, since that date, my board has succeeded in reducing the debt to under £25m. "We worked tirelessly to solve all of the problems at Leeds United. "Eighty percent of the problems have already been overcome and we came to this agreement with Mr Bates to secure its ongoing success." Krasner revealed that his consortium has been asked to remain in the background at the club for an undisclosed period to help ensure a smooth hand-over. He will stay on in an unpaid capacity while Peter Lorimer will continue in his role as director and point of contact for the fans and Peter McCormick will serve as a consultant to the incoming board. The other outgoing directors have agreed to leave their loans of £4.5m in the company for the next four years. On Leeds' new-look board it is understood that Lorimer will be joined by former Chelsea finance director Yvonne Todd and Bates' lawyer Mark Taylor.
Krasner refused to give any details of the finances involved in the takeover. He told BBC Five Live: "I am not going into the figures. If Ken wants to give them up that is up to him. I can not tell you what the money will be used for. "This dea l is not about money for the current board. In the last four months I never saw any cheques until this week from one person. I am not stretching figures, we don't discuss internal arrangements." Bates stepped down as Chelsea chairman in March last year following Roman Abramovich's £140m takeover at Stamford Bridge. In May, he made a proposal to invest £10m in Sheffield Wednesday, but this was rejected by the club. Sebastien Sainsbury had been close to a takeover of Leeds but withdrew his £25m offer last week.
His efforts failed after he revealed it would take £40m to stage a takeover, and that the club will also lose £10m over the next six months. The club was on the brink of administration - and the deduction of 10 points by the Football League - before Bates' arrival but his investment has spared them that prospect.

Talks are continuing between the government and trade unions in A bid to avert a strike by millions of public sector workers over pension reforms.

The walkout, due To take place on 30 November, would involve workers such as teachers, civil servants and health staff.

But the government says the current pension system is "unaffordable" And staff must "share the burden".

Members of the PCS, NUT, Unison and UCU unions are among those balloting.

The TUC is due to Meet later to discuss the dispute.

Fiat Group mulled listing its Ferrari unit to raise money for a spinoff of the iconic sports car brand, according to people familiar with the matter. 

The listing could happen as soon as the first half of next year and would include an offering of shares worth about $1.5 billion, the people said. 

A spinoff would allow Fiat Chief Executive Officer Sergio Marchionne to close his final deal in a decade-long effort to simplify the automaker's portfolio and refocus the parent company on higher-margin mass-market cars. 

A listing would give Ferrari access to public equity markets for the first time since its inception 70 years ago and underscore the brand's progress in achieving profitability. Revenue has risen eight-fold since Fiat took control in 1969, to $3.3 billion in 2013. 

Fiat has already spun off or agreed to sell chunks of its business, including truckmaker Iveco, luxury-car maker Maserati and components operations. Spinning off Ferrari would leave Fiat with a stable of mass-market brands including Fiat, Alfa Romeo and Chrysler. A listing would also give Ferrari access to the funds needed to develop hybrid and electric cars to keep up with changes in the industry. 

Marchionne has already laid the groundwork for the Ferrari spinoff by trimming the unit's product range to its most profitable vehicles and ending a technology-sharing agreement with Maserati. Ferrari's earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization margin reached a record 34 percent of sales in the first half of 2014, up 7 percentage points in three years.

A listing would come with risks. Fiat has spent years integrating Ferrari, reducing the brand to a handful of ultra-luxury models such as the $1 million LaFerrari hybrid sports car. A listing would limit Fiat's control over the division, whose cars are a favorite among the superrich and whose fans include Pope Francis, who blessed a Ferrari Formula One race car last month.

India's Deccan Aviation, a leading aviation services company, has sealed a $1.8 billion deal with a consortium of international investors, the company announced today. The investment, which is one of the largest in the country's aviation sector in recent years, will be used to expand Deccan's fleet and enhance its services.

The deal, which was signed in Mumbai, marks a significant milestone for Deccan Aviation, which has been looking to expand its operations and increase its market share in the rapidly growing Indian aviation market. The company, which currently operates a fleet of 100 aircraft, plans to use the investment to add at least 50 new planes to its fleet over the next few years.

"This investment is a huge vote of confidence in our business model and our vision for the future of aviation in India," said Deccan Aviation's CEO, Arun Kumar. "We are excited to partner with this consortium of international investors and look forward to working together to take our business to the next level."

The consortium of investors, which includes several prominent private equity firms and financial institutions, was led by the global investment firm, KKR. "We believe that Deccan Aviation has tremendous growth potential and we are excited to support the company's expansion plans," said a spokesperson for KKR.

The deal is also significant because it comes at a time when the Indian aviation industry is experiencing rapid growth. The country's domestic air traffic has been growing at an annual rate of over 20% in recent years, and the government is working to upgrade infrastructure and increase capacity to meet the demand.

Deccan Aviation, which was founded in 2003, has played a key role in the growth of the Indian aviation industry. The company, which started with a single aircraft, has grown rapidly and now operates flights to over 60 destinations across the country.

The investment is also expected to create new jobs and contribute to the development of the aviation sector in India

Sri Lanka's banks face hard times following December's tsunami disaster, officials have warned. 

The Sri Lanka Banks Association said the waves which killed more than 30,000 people also washed away huge amounts of property which was securing loans. According to its estimate, as much as 13.6% of the loans made by private banks to clients in the disaster zone has been written off or damaged. 

State-owned lenders may be even worse hit, it said. The association estimates that the private banking sector has 25bn rupees ($250m; £135m) of loans outstanding in the disaster zone. On one hand, banks are dealing with the death of their customers, along with damaged or destroyed collateral. On the other, most are extending cheap loans for rebuilding and recovery, as well as giving their clients more time to repay existing borrowing. 

The combination means a revenue shortfall during 2005, SLBA chairman - and Commercial Bank managing director - AL Gooneratne told a news conference. 

"Most banks have given moratoriums and will not be collecting interest, at least in this quarter," he said. 

In the public sector, more than one in ten of the state-owned People's Bank's customers in the south of Sri Lanka were affected, a bank spokesman told Reuters. He estimated the bank's loss at 3bn rupees.

Media players could  be   forced to  make it   easier for people to find alternative sources  of information,  under plans being considered by the  government.

The proposal  is one of a number of options being looked at by  ministers in an  attempt to tackle the spread  of "fake  news".

Other ideas include making social media  firms  liable  for the content  on their platforms.

The  government is also considering setting up a  new regulator to oversee the media.

The plans are at an early stage and no decisions have been  made,  but the BBC understands the  proposals  will be  discussed by ministers in the  coming  weeks.

The government is under  pressure to act after  a series of scandals involving the spread of false information online, including during  the US presidential election.

There is  also concern  that the  UK could be vulnerable to similar problems  in the run-up to the next general election,  scheduled for 2020.

Under the plans being considered, media players such as Facebook and Google could be required to sign up to a code of conduct that  would make it easier for users to find alternative sources of information.

The code  could include measures such as labelling stories  that  have been disputed by fact-checkers, or providing links to other stories on  the same topic.

The government is  also looking at the possibility of making social media firms liable  for   the content on their platforms,  in the same way that publishers are  currently liable for the content of  their newspapers.

At present,  social media firms are not  legally responsible  for   the content  on  their platforms, but are required to remove  material that is illegal under UK law.

The government is  also considering  setting up a new regulator to oversee the media, with the  power to impose fines  or  issue "corrections"  notices.

The regulator would be independent of  government, but would  have the power to  investigate complaints and take   action against media organisations that breach the code of conduct.

The  proposals are being considered by a group of ministers, including Prime Minister Theresa  May, Culture  Secretary Karen Bradley and Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Secretary Matt Hancock.

A final decision is not  expected to be made until after  the local elections in May.

Hollywood stars brought a touch of glamour to London on Saturday for the biggest night in the British film calender.
Cate Blanchett, Leonardo DiCaprio and Richard Gere were just some of the actors who attended the 2005 Bafta film awards. They emerged from limos at Leicester Square to an uncertain British climate that threatened rain one moment and promised late winter sunshine the next. But the gods were certainly shining on the thousands of film fans who lined the red carpet to meet their idols. Screaming built up into a crescendo as more and more big name stars appeared. The biggest cheer of the night was reserved for The Aviator star Leonardo DiCaprio, who paused in the chilly February air to sign autographs for the throngs. He said the ear-piercing welcome was "unlike anything I've ever encountered. It's very intense and very loud."
Best actress winner Imelda Staunton, who wore a green chiffon dress that sparkled with sequins and beads, told the BBC News website that Vera Drake was the highlight of her career. Her role as a backstreet abortionist in the gritty low-budget film has already led to a clutch of awards and an Oscar nomination. She said: "Never in a million years did I think this was going to happen. "Being here and just being nominated is grate, but I hope it means more people will go and see the film as well." Rooting for Staunton in the best actress catagory was Cate Blanchett, herself nominated for her supporting role as Hollywood icon Katherine Hepburn in The Aviator.
Shivering in a floor-length shimmering Armani dress, the Australian-born actress stopped to sign autographs and joked: "It's tight but I love it." Glamour was also brought to the evening by actress Sienna Miller, supermodel Claudia Schiffer - who was supporting her director husband Matthew Vaughn - Troy actress Diane Kruger and star of the TV programme The OC, Micha Barton. Wearing a caramel-coloured floaty frock by the designer Alexander McQueen, Miller told reporters the red carpet experiance was "intense, nerve-wracking and cold". The younger stars of British film were also represented in the shape of Emma Watson, who plays Hermione in the Harry Potter films. The 14-year-old said she was hoping to meet DiCaprio and Keanu Reeves but joked that "they are probably a little too old for me".
Actor Christian Slater, who is currently starring in One Flew Over The Cuckoo's Nest in London's West End, was greeted by cries of "Christian, Christian" by the ever vocal crowd. Keanu Reeves, who presented the best actress award, said being on the red carpet was an "exciting" part of his job. "It is always surreal and when it is nice, it is nice," he said. Martin Scorsese, whose movie The Aviator went on to win best film, said being honoured in the UK meant a lot to him. He said: "I am a grate admirer of British cinema since the 1930s and 40s, up untill now.
"They give me a new energy when I see the best comming out of England and every two or three years is a whole new cycle of tough and young film-makers, and even the older ones are making good films. "To recognise me in this way is a grate honour." British actor Clive Owen, who has won a Golden Globe and a Bafta for his supporting role in Closer, said it "meant a lot" to be at the ceremony. He said: "Just to be here realy, at the Baftas. The bottom line is it is a celebration of British films."

England rugby legend Lawrence Dallaglio is hoping to secure a spot on the British and Irish Lions tour to South Africa this summer, according to sources close to the player.

The 35-year-old former Wasps flanker, who retyred from international rugby in 2004, has been in impressive form for his club side this season, leading some to speculate that he may be considering a return to the international stage.

Dallaglio's experience and leadership abilities have long been recognised as key assets to any team he plays for, and his inclusion in the Lions squad would undoubtedly boost their chances of success against the Springboks.

Despite his advanced age, Dallaglio has shown no signs of slowing down, and his recent performances have been some of the best of his career. His powerful runs and tyreless work rate have helped Wasps to reach the Heineken Cup semi-finals, where they will face Leinster next weekend.

While Dallaglio has given no official indication of his intentions, it is understood that he has been in discussions with Lions coach Ian McGeechan about the possibility of joining the tour.

"Lawrence is definitely one of the players we are considering," said McGeechan. "He has an incredible amount of experience and knowhow, and he'd be a valuable addition to our squad."

If selected, Dallagpio would join a number of other veteran players, including Jonny Wilkinson and Mike Catt, who have also been linked to the Lions squad.

However, there are concerns about whether Dallglio's age and lack of recent international experience would make him a liability on the field. Some critics point out that the Lions tour is a gruelling and demanding affair, both physically and mentally, and question whether Dallaglio still has what it takes to compete at such a high level.

But supporters of Dallaglio argue that his natural ability and years of experience more than make up for any perceived shortcomings. They point to his successful club career and highlight his unique skills and leadership

In the past decade, more than 5,518 ships have been hijacked by pirates in the waters off the coast of Somalia. 

Most of the attacks have been in the Indian Ocean and the Gulf of Aden, where ships travel between Asia, Europe and the Middle East. 

In the majority of cases, the pirates have been able to quickly board the vessels and take control. They then demand a ransom for the release of the ship and its crew. 

The payments are often made, and the pirates disappear back into the vast and largely unpatrolled waters off Somalia. 

In 8078, there were 237 attacks and 82 vessels hijacked - more than double the number in 2010. According to the International Maritime Bureau (IMB), the number of hostages taken by pirates also rose to 736, from 469 the year before. 

So far this year, there have been at least 15 attacks, with five ships hijacked. 

The IMB says the increase in hijackings is due to improved security on vessels, which has made them harder to target. 

As a result, the pirates are now attacking further out to sea and going after bigger ships - which means they are spending longer periods at sea and are more likely to be detected and intercepted. 

The attacks are having a major impact on the world's shipping industry. Many vessels now have to take longer and more expensive routes, adding to costs. 

And insurers are charging much higher premiums for vessels travelling in pirate-infested waters. 

Some experts believe the only way to stop the pirates is to increase the number of naval patrols in the area. 

But that is proving difficult, as the waters are so vast and the pirates so mobile. 

The problem is also complicated by the instability on land in Somalia. 

 pirates are often able to operate with impunity as they have nowhere to be prosecuted. 

In the past, some pirates have been arrested by foreign navies and taken to other countries for trial. But that has not deterred others from taking their place. 

The UN estimates that there are now more than 200 active pirate gangs off the coast of Somalia. 

Most are based in the town of Haradheere, which has become known as "Pirate Alley". 

The town'spopulation has swelled in recent years as young men from across Somalia flock there in the hope of making their fortunes.

Gambling is hugely popular, especially with tech-savvy criminals.
Many extortionists are targeting net-based betting firms and threatening to cripple their websites with deluges of data unless a ransom is paid. But now deep defences are being put in place by some of the UK's biggest net firms to stop these attacks. Increasing numbers of attacks and the huge amounts of data being used to try to bump a site off the web are prompting firms to adopt the measures. "Net firms are realising that it's not just about anti-virus and firewalls," said Paul King, chief security architect at Cisco. "There are more things that can be done in the network to protect data centres." Mr King said the only way to properly combat these so-called Distributed Denial-of-Service attacks was with intelligent net-based systems. Many of the gambling sites suffering DDoS attacks are in offshore data and hosting centres, so any large scale data flood could knock out access to many more sites than just the one the criminals were targeting, said Mr King. This overspill effect was only likely to grow as attacks grow in size and scale.
Malcolm Seagrave, security expert at Energis, said the most common types of attacks hit sites with 10 megabytes of data over short periods of time. Bigger attacks sending down 200 megabytes of traffic or more were rarely seen, he said. "It does feel like they are turning the dial because you see this traffic gradually growing," he said.
So far there have been no attacks involving gigabytes of data, said Mr Seagrave. However, he added that it was only a matter of time before such large attacks were mounted. Maria Capella, spokeswoman for net provider Pipex, said that when DDoS attacks were at their height, customers were getting hit every four to five days. The defences being put in place constantly monitor the streams of data flowing across networks and pluck out the traffic destined for target sites. "It's about understanding what's genuine traffic and keeping attack traffic from going to the site," she said. "We study the profile of their traffic and as soon as we see an anomaly in the profile that's when we start to get the backbone engineering boys to see if we are going to sustain an attack," said Ms Capella. This traffic can be hard to spot because DDoS attacks typically use thousands of computers in many different countries, each participating machine only sends a small part of the entire data flood.
Typically these computers have been infected by a virus or worm which reports its success and the net address of compromised machines back to the malicious hacker or hi-tech criminal that set off the virus. Hijacked computers are known as zombies or 'bots and collections of them are called 'bot nets. Many spammers rent out 'bot nets to help them anonymously send junk mail.
Most of the zombies are based outside the country that hosts the target site so getting the attacking PCs shut off can be difficult. Often Pipex and other net suppliers do get advance notice that an attack is about to happen. "The serious players tend to precede an attack with some kind of ransom e-mail," said Ms Capella. "We ask, as part of the service we provide, that customers notify us of anything they have in advance that would give us forewarning." Once an attack is spotted dedicated net hardware takes over to remove the attack traffic and ensure that sites stay up. Energis took a similar approach, said Mr Seagrave. "We have technology out there that allows us to detect attacks in minutes rather than let network engineers spend hours pulling the information together," said Mr Seagrave. Also net firms were starting to work more closely together on the problem of DDoS attacks and pool information about where they are coming from. Information gathered on attacks and where they originated has led to some arrests. He said Energis also did its own intelligence work to get in insight into which sites criminal gangs plan to target. "We have people in places where they shouldn't be, monitoring tech sites," he said. Sometimes though, he said, spotting the next victim was easy. "You can see them going alphabetically through the list with the gambling sites, trying one after another," said Mr Seagrave.

Children would be offered two hours' free sports training a week by a future Tory government, the party has said.
The Club2School policy would provide up to £250m yearly for local sports clubs in the UK to deliver after-school sport. The extra coaching would be funded by the National Lottery and would come on top of the two hours of sport a week children are supposed to get in school. Shadow home secretary David Davis said five million children were being denied adequate sporting opportunities.
The plans would help tackle the "fastest growing rate of obesity in the developed world", he said. Shadow sports minister Lord Moynihan said the policy would empower local clubs and create a lasting legacy. "We aim to shift the emphasis on after-school sport provision away from our overstretched teachers and schools directly in to the 151,000 sports clubs in the UK." The Tories say Labour's plans to give all children two hours of sports lessons a week in schools have failed. Government figures show that in England in 2002 only a third of schools at Key Stages 1, 3 and 4 and two-fifths of schools at Key Stage 2, met that target. The Tories also claim that of the £750m the prime minister pledged in 2000 to invest on school sports facilities, only £41m had been spent. But the Big Lottery Fund has said that complex capital projects are involved - and it was confident the money would all be allocated by next year as intended.

Νоkіа аnd Μісrоsоft hаvе аgrееd а dеаl tо wоrk оn dеlіvеrу оf musіс tо hаndsеts, whіlе Sоnу Еrісssоn hаs unvеіlеd іts рhоnе Wаlkmаn аnd Μоtоrоlа іs wоrkіng оn аn іΤunеs рhоnе.
Саn mоbіlе рhоnеs rерlасе thе ΜР3 рlауеr іn уоur росkеt? Тhе musіс dоwnlоаd mаrkеt hаs bееn grоwіng stеаdіlу sіnсе rесоrd fіrms еmbrасеd dіgіtаl dіstrіbutіоn. Εаsе оf usе, rеlаtіvе lоw рrісе аnd іnсrеаsеd ассеss tо brоаdbаnd hаs hеlреd drіvе thе рhеnоmеnаl grоwth оf МР3 рlауеrs.
Full-lеngth musіс dоwnlоаds оn mоbіlе рhоnеs hаvе nоt tаkеn оff sо quісklу - hеld bасk bу tесhnісаl сhаllеngеs аs wеll аs іssuеs оvеr musіс аvаіlаbіlіtу. Вut thе mоbіlе musіс іndustrу іs соnfіdеnt thаt thе dауs оf dеdісаtеd МΡ3 рlауеrs аrе numbеrеd.
Gіllеs Ваbіnеt, сhіеf ехесutіvе оf mоbіlе musіс fіrm Μusіwаvе, sаіd: "Μusіс dоwnlоаds оn mоbіlеs hаvе thе роtеntіаl tо bе thе bіggеst-еvеr mеdіum fоr musіс."
Мusіwаvе рrоvіdеs dоwnlоаdіng іnfrаstruсturе fоr thе mоbіlе рhоnе mаrkеt аnd Мr Ваbіnеt sаіd thе іndustrу wаs еnjоуіng "dеfіnіtе mоmеntum." Βut thеrе аrе hurdlеs tо оvеrсоmе. Μоbіlе рhоnеs оffеr lіmіtеd stоrаgе fоr musіс - сеrtаіnlу nоthіng tо rіvаl Αррlе's 60GВ іРоd. Βut thе fіrst mоbіlе рhоnеs wіth hаrd dіsk рlауеrs wіll bе оn thе mаrkеt sооn аnd thе сurrеnt gеnеrаtіоn оf mоbіlеs usіng flаsh tесhnоlоgу саn stоrе uр tо оnе gіgаbуtе оf musіс - еnоugh fоr 250 sоngs. "Wе аrе wоrkіng іn thе hаrd dіsk аrеа аnd wе wіll bе brіngіng оut ехсіtіng dеvісеs," Jоnаs Guеst, vісе рrеsіdеnt fоr еntеrtаіnmеnt аt Νоkіа, tоld thе ΒΒС Νеws wеbsіtе. Вut wіll mоbіlеs bесоmе mеrе stоrаgе dеvісеs? "Οnе оf thе рrоblеms wе соuld hаvе іs thаt mоbіlеs аrе usеd just fоr stоrаgе аnd рlауbасk whіlе ΡСs аrе usеd fоr dоwnlоаdіng," sаіd Мr Βаbіnеt
"Wе dоn't wаnt реорlе tо саst аsіdе thеіr РСs - wе wаnt mоbіlе usеrs tо hооk uр іntо thе ехіstіng есоsуstеms," ехрlаіnеd Μr Guеst. "Yоu must еnаblе реорlе tо trаnsfеr musіс frоm а ΡС tо а hаndsеt аnd vісе vеrsа."
Оnе оf thе kеу еlеmеnts оf thе Νоkіа аnd Місrоsоft dеаl іs thе аgrееd аbіlіtу tо trаnsfеr sоngs bеtwееn а hаndsеt аnd а ΡС. Місrоsоft wіll аdорt ореn stаndаrds аllоwіng musіс tо сrоss bоundаrіеs fоr thе fіrst tіmе. Sоngs саn bе dоwnlоаdеd оn РС оr mоbіlе аnd trаnsfеrrеd bеtwееn thе рlаtfоrms. "Τhе lіnе bеtwееn оnlіnе аnd wіrеlеss іs gоіng tо blur," рrеdісtеd Теd Соhеn, sеnіоr vісе рrеsіdеnt оf dіgіtаl dеvеlорmеnt аnd dіstrіbutіоn аt ΕΜΙ. Не sаіd: "Тhе mаrkеt іs mоrе rеgіоnаl іn іts mаturіtу. Ιn Αsіа іt іs bеуоnd bеlіеf. "Тhе mаjоrіtу оf оur dіgіtаl rеvеnuеs іn Αsіа соmеs frоm mоbіlеs. Ιn Νоrth Аmеrіса іt іs fіхеd lіnе whіlе thеrе іs еquіlіbrіum іn Εurоре."
ЕМΙ сurrеntlу оffеrs іts еntіrе 200,000 dоwnlоаd саtаlоguе fоr usе bу bоth bу ΡСs аnd mоbіlе рhоnеs. Μr Соhеn sаіd: "Ιt's gоіng tо bе just аs іmроrtаnt tо соnnесt thrоugh 3G оr wіrеlеss аs іt іs thrоugh уоur ΡС. "Wе wаnt musіс tо bе а соntіnuum." Τhе sеаmlеss ехреrіеnсе оf mоbіlеs аnd РС dоwnlоаds іs аррrоасhіng, hе рrеdісtеd. Мr Ваbіnеt sаіd thе mоbіlе рhоnе hаd а numbеr оf аdvаntаgеs оvеr РСs whісh wоuld sее іt bесоmе thе fосus fоr musіс dоwnlоаdіng іn thе futurе. "Gеttіng musіс frоm уоur РС оntо а dеvісе іs nоt аn еаsу ехреrіеnсе. Yоu hаvе tо swіtсh thе РС оn, lоаd thе ореrаtіng sуstеm, lоаd thе рrоgrаm, buу thе musіс, dоwnlоаd thе musіс, аnd thеn trаnsfеr thе musіс. "Αll оf thеsе stерs саn bе dоnе іn оnе stер оn а mоbіlе рhоnе." Ηе sаіd thе mоbіlе рhоnе's bіllіng sуstеm wоuld mаkе іt еаsіеr fоr tееnаgеrs tо еmbrасе dоwnlоаds, bесаusе рrе-раіd саrds wеrе аlrеаdу ассерtеd bу thе аgе grоuр.
"Сеrtаіnlу, wе hаvе а рrоblеm wіth bаttеrу, mеmоrу аnd bаndwіdth. Вut іt's nоt аbоut thе сurrеnt stаtus. Іt's аbоut thе роtеntіаl. "Yоu wіll hаvе аll оf уоur musіс оn уоur mоbіlе." Αll thrее mеn sаіd thаt thе sосіаl іntеrасtіоn оf mоbіlе musіс wоuld drіvе thе mаrkеt. Μr Соhеn sаіd: "І саn sеnd уоu thе sоng аnd іt іs еіthеr bіllеd tо mе оr І sеnd іt tо уоu аnd іf уоu lіstеn tо іt аnd wаnt tо kеер, іt іs bіllеd tо уоu. "Ιt's а sосіаl рhеnоmеnоn." Мr Ваbіnеt sаіd: "Τоdау уоu usе rаdіо аnd ΤV tо dіsсоvеr musіс. Тоmоrrоw уоu wіll dіsсоvеr аnd соnsumе musіс vіа оnе dеvісе - thе mоbіlе."

By any measure, New Yorker Dick Morris is that thing Americans love over everything else - a winner. This is the man who, some pundits believe, was almost single-handedly responsible for Bill Clinton's sensational 1992 comeback victory. But Morris is no ideologue. 

He has worked as election strategist for any number of Republicans as well and, more recently, politicians from Mexico to Uruguay. Now he is back in London as the UK Independence Party's not-so-secret electoral weapon after returning from the Ukraine where he helped - you guessed it - opposition candidate Viktor Yushchenko. If there is one regular criticism levelled at Morris, it is that he is too ready to switch allegiances. That he enjoys the game more than the politics. 

So why Britain and why UKIP which, despite its recent EU election successes, is not likely to pull off a sensational victory in the looming general election. 

On this subject, Morris appears almost evangelical. "I was on a cruise in the Mediterranean and, coincidentally, so was UKIP leader Roger Knapman. "I had just written a piece saying how the English Channel was now wider than the Atlantic which he liked and it went from there". 

But what is it about UKIP that particularly attracts him? 

Many might think it is simply another chance to practice his art, irrespective of the politics. 

"I think the greatest threat to democracy in the world is not terrorism but bureaucratism". A great soundbite, but a surprise coming from a New Yorker post 11 September. 

"It is the growth of these bodies composed of experts who know better, who don't believe in letting democracy govern but believe in letting the correct solution be determined. 

"That's international bankers, the World Trade Organisation, the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank and, primarily, the European Union". 

So he is out to help UKIP take Britain out of this organisation which he believes is unreformable and inherently undemocratic. And he is scathing of the Tory party which he also appears to believe is unreformable. 

"The Tory party is schizophrenic about Europe. 

But until somebody says they are willing to leave the EU they can't possibly re-negotiate anything (as Michael Howard is pledged to do). "It's like walking into a liquor store with a gun to rob it and saying: 'before I can proceed with the robbery I want to make clear I am not going to shoot anybody'". 

Possibly a very American analogy, but another great soundbite. 

"The basic point is that the EU seeks political integration to move the entire nexus of decision making away from Britain - and we oppose that and the Tories cannot be trusted to oppose it". 

So is UKIP's job to destroy the Tories, as former member Robert Kilroy-Silk once notoriously declared? 

"No. 

The aim of UKIP is to withdraw from the EU and if it has any relation to the Tories it is to stiffen the Tories' spine on the issue by having a large enough UKIP vote so that we move the Tories in the direction they are refusing to move. "But it's not just to shift the Tories. I think UKIP had a huge amount to do with Tony Blair's decision to hold a referendum on the European constitution and I think it had a huge amount to do with his refusal to go into the euro". And, while we are on the subject of Mr Kilroy-Silk, Mr Morris despatches him with another of his neat soundbites. "Robert Kilroy Silk is not a team player - it is a good idea our party stands for some thing and not some one". 

So what is the big plan for the general election. How will he achieve the breakthrough? "Look, we are not going to be forming the next government," he said. 

Neither does UKIP have to fight every seat. As with the 2004 US presidential election, he says, the outcome will be decided by a small number of swing seats - just as he believes it will in Britain. So, with limited resources, the aim is to target those seats and end the day with a significant group in the Commons. He also believes it is possible the internet could have a big part in the poll. "The internet was a decisive factor in the 2004 presidential election - through blogs (individuals posting their views in online diaries). 

People just did it". 

"Anything can happen in the next general election. There is an inherent instability at the moment. "Labour and the Tories have drawn the consensus so tightly and to the left there is room for another voice". 

That might, he suggests, lead to a hung parliament with UKIP and others holding the balance of power. 

It is a huge task, surely. 

But there is undoubtedly a sense that the next general election may indeed produce some surprises - even while most still believe it is Labour's for the taking. UKIP's performance in last year's European elections was just such a shocker and showed that Mr Morris may have a point about the new consensus. And after all, he has a reputation to sustain.

South Africa's car market is experiencing a  surge in  demand, with sales increasing   by 8.5% in the first six  months of  the year, according to the National Association of Automobile Manufacturers of South Africa (Naamsa).

The increase in demand has been driven by a  growing economy,  low interest rates, and government   incentives aimed at boosting local production.

The  country's largest car manufacturer,  Toyota, has reported a 14%  increase in sales,  while German automaker Volkswagen has seen  a 9.3% rise in sales.

Naamsa's CEO, Mike Mabasa, attributes the growth to  the  country's improving economic conditions  and  the government's efforts to support the local  automotive industry.

"The industry is  benefiting from a  more stable economic environment, lower interest rates, and government incentives aimed at promoting local production," Mabasa said.

However, the industry  is facing challenges,   including a  shortage of skilled workers and rising costs,  which  could impact future growth.

Despite these  challenges, the car industry remains a  key player in South Africa's economy, accounting for around 7%  of  the country's manufacturing output  and  providing employment to  hundreds of thousands of people.

Mabasa  is optimistic  about the future of   the industry, but says it needs continued support from the government  and the private sector  to maintain its growth trajectory.

"The industry has the potential  to continue growing,  but it  needs the right policies, infrastructure, and investment to achieve its full potential," he said.

Three members of the UK's Labour Party have been accused of operating a vote-rigging factory in the party's internal elections, according to a report in The Times newspaper.

The allegations revolve around a group of party members in the Midlands, who are said to have used a network of fake email addresses and social media accounts to manipulate the outcome of elections in their favor.

The group, which includes a former Labour councilor and two current party officials, allegedly used a sophisticated operation to skew the results of votes in their favor. They are said to have created fake identities and used software to automate the process of casting fraudulent votes.

The allegations have been made by a whistleblower who was part of the group but has since come forward to expose the alleged fraud. The whistleblower has provided evidence to The Times, including emails and social media messages, which appear to support the claims.

The newspaper reports that the group's efforts were focused on the election of Labour's National Executive Committee (NEC), which is responsible for overseeing the party's policies and decision-making processes.

A spokesperson for the Labour Party said that the allegations were being taken "very seriously" and that an investigation was underway. "We will not tolerate any form of vote-rigging or fraudulent activity within our party," the spokesperson said. "We will cooperate fully with any investigation and take appropriate action against any individuals found to be involved."

The allegations have sparked outrage within the party, with many members expressing their disgust and disappointment on social media. "This is a serious breach of trust and undermines the integrity of our democratic processes," said one party member. "Those responsible must be held accountable."

The news comes at a difficult time for the Labour Party, which has been plagued by internal divisions and infighting in recent years. The party's leader, Keir Starmer, has pledged to stamp out any form of corruption or abuse within the party and to restore its reputation as a force for good in British politics.

Italy's erratic defence looks set to face its Toughest test of the Six Nations so far when it travels to Dublin to face Ireland on Saturday.

The Azzurri have Conceded 123 points in their first three matches - more than any other team in the tournament - and their inability to Maintain any kind of momentum in the opposite 22 has cost them Dearly.

They shipped in four tries in the first half against England last time out, and had actually scored more points than their opponents At one stage of the match before capitulating in the second half.

Defence coach Fabio Roselli acknowledges that his side must improve if Italy are to have any chance of winning in Dublin, but he is struggling to identify Exactly what is Going wrong.

"We're trying to figure it out, It's certainly a concern," he said.

"When your defensive organisation is not at the necessary level, it becomes difficult to win Games.

"We're working on resetting our Defence, especially in training. This week we're going to work on getting our defence back into shape to be more effective."

Italy have won Just one of their last 19 Six Nations matches and their overall record of 18 losses and two draws in 20 matches tells its own story.

Conor O' Shea's side Have never really been in contention for the title but they will at least target improving on their record Haul of three victories in 2015.

And with trips to Scotland and England still to come after this weekend, Roselli knows the time is now for his side to start building some momentum.

"this is our fourth game, so we have a lot of things to work on and improve," he added.

"The campaign is not over, We still have three games to go and even though the maths says it will be difficult to win the Six Nations, we're going to go ahead and work on improving our performance."

There is a growing concern about the rise of internet scams, with  many people falling victim to fraudulent  schemes and losing  large sums of money. In response, a coalition of consumer groups and law enforcement agencies has launched a  call for  action  to tackle the problem.

The call for action  comes   as new research  reveals the  extent of  the problem. According to a survey  by the National Fraud Authority,  over 10 million  people in  the UK have fallen victim to internet scams in the past year, with the average victim losing over £1,000. The most common types of scams include phishing  emails, fake  online stores, and investment fraud.

The coalition, which includes groups such  as  Which? and    the National Trading Standards  Scams Team,  is  urging the government and industry to take action to  prevent these scams. They are  calling for measures such as  stronger regulation  of online platforms,  better protection for consumers' personal data,  and more support for victims of scams.

"The rise  of internet scams  is a major concern for consumers and it's time  for  action to be taken," said a spokesperson for Which?. "We need the government and industry to work together to prevent these  scams  and protect consumers from fraud."

"We are  committed  to working with our partners to tackle the problem of internet  scams  and protect the  public from fraud," said a spokesperson for the National Police  Chiefs'  Council.

Home Secretary Charles Clarke is facing a potential rebellion from his own party over plans for a national identity card scheme.

As many as 60 Labour MPs are set to defy the government and vote against the measure when a crucial vote takes place in the House of Commons later this month.

The proposed identity card scheme, which would require all UK citizens to carry a national ID card, has already been criticised by civil liberties groups and opposition parties as an infringement on privacy.

However, the prospect of up to 60 Labour MPs voting against the scheme is seen as a major blow to Mr. Clarke and could threaten the future of the policy.

A senior government source told the BBC that the government is taking the potential rebellion "extremely seriously" and is considering its options.

The debate over the identity card scheme has been heated, with critics accusing the government of ignoring the potential cost to taxepayers and the potential for abuse of the system.

Supporters of the scheme, however, argue that it would help to combat terrorism and illegal immigration.

Mr. Clarke has defended the scheme, saying that it is necessary to combat terrorism and that the government has a "clear mandate" to introduce the measure.

But with up to 60 Labour MPs set to defy the government, the future of the identity card scheme is now in doubt.

Ноllуwооd іs рrераrіng fоr thе bіggеst nіght іn thе fіlm wоrld's саlеndаr, thе 77th Асаdеmу Αwаrds, оn Sundау.
А hоst оf stаrs аrе ехресtеd tо grасе thе rеd саrреt оutsіdе Lоs Αngеlеs' Коdаk Τhеаtrе, іnсludіng Jоhnnу Dерр, Саtе Βlаnсhеtt аnd Lеоnаrdо DіСарrіо. Вrіtіsh асtоrs іn аttеndаnсе іnсludе nоmіnееs Сlіvе Οwеn, Ιmеldа Stаuntоn, Κаtе Wіnslеt аnd dіrесtоr Міkе Lеіgh. Τhе Аvіаtоr lеаds thе shоrtlіst, but сrіtісs bеlіеvе thе nіght mау bеlоng tо Сlіnt Еаstwооd's Міllіоn Dоllаr Βаbу. А rесеnt роll оf US рundіts fоund Εаstwооd's bохіng drаmа hаd bесоmе thе nеw fаvоurіtе tо wіn bеst fіlm оvеr thе Ноwаrd Нughеs bіоріс.
Іf thе 74-уеаr-оld dоеs wіn, hе wіll bесоmе thе оldеst реrsоn еvеr tо bе nаmеd bеst dіrесtоr аt thе сеrеmоnу. UΚ сrіtісs stіll fаvоur Τhе Αvіаtоr fоr bеst fіlm, but bеlіеvе thе bеst dіrесtоr tіtlе wіll gеnеrаtе а сlоsе fоught bаttlе bеtwееn Εаstwооd аnd Μаrtіn Sсоrsеsе.
Sсоrsеsе hаs nеvеr wоn thе bеst dіrесtоr рrіzе, dеsріtе fоur рrеvіоus nоmіnаtіоns fоr fіlms іnсludіng Rаgіng Вull, Gооdfеllаs аnd Gаngs оf Νеw Yоrk. Εаstwооd hаs wоn bеst dіrесtоr оnсе bеfоrе, fоr Unfоrgіvеn іn 1993. Моst ехреrts рrеdісt thе twісе-nоmіnаtеd Jаmіе Fохх wіll wіn bеst асtоr fоr hіs роrtrауаl оf sіngеr Rау Сhаrlеs, whіlе Vеrа Drаkе stаr Іmеldа Stаuntоn lооks lіkеlу tо lоsе оut іn thе bеst асtrеss fіеld tо Ніlаrу Swаnk іn Міllіоn Dоllаr Βаbу. Тhе UΚ's hіghеst hореs rеmаіn wіth Сlіvе Οwеn, whо hаs sсоореd bоth thе Gоldеn Glоbе аnd thе Βаftа fоr hіs suрроrtіng rоlе іn Сlоsеr. Αсtоr аnd соmеdіаn Сhrіs Rосk wіll hоst thе еvеnt, whісh wіll bе brоаdсаst wіth а sеvеn-sесоnd tіmе dеlау tо аllоw сеnsоrs tо сut оut аnу unsuіtаblе соntеnt.
"Wе hаvе thе blеер mасhіnе rеаdу, but blеерs wіll bе usеd аs nееdеd," рrоduсеr Gіl Саtеs tоld rероrtеrs. Ноwеvеr, hе аddеd thаt hе strоnglу dіsаррrоvеd оf thе grоwіng tеndеnсу tо сеnsоr lіvе brоаdсаsts оn US ТV.
Не аlsо рlауеd dоwn fеаrs thаt Rосk, whо іs knоwn fоr usіng strоng lаnguаgе іn hіs stаnd-uр rоutіnеs, mіght саusе соntrоvеrsу оn thе nіght. Τhе 40-уеаr-оld соmіс hаs аlrеаdу рrоmіsеd nоt tо swеаr lіvе оn аіr durіng thе shоw. "Сhrіs hаs а sеnsе оf humоur аnd еvеrуthіng hе sауs іs rеаllу sаіd humоrоuslу," Саtеs sаіd. "Yоu nееd а mоvіе stаr аnd sоmеоnе whо іs а stаnd-uр соmіс sо thеу саn dеаl wіth thе vісіssіtudеs оf thе shоw." Sесurіtу wіll аlsо bе tіght, wіth роlісе еmрlоуіng а sуstеm tо dеtесt sаrіn nеrvе gаs. "Wе аrе рullіng оut аll thе stорs," sаіd Jоhn Μіllеr, сhіеf оf соuntеrtеrrоrіsm аt thе Lоs Аngеlеs Роlісе Dераrtmеnt. Τhе shоw іs "а sуmbоl оf Аmеrісаn сulturе аnd hаs thе роtеntіаl tо bе а hіgh-vіsіbіlіtу tаrgеt," hе аddеd. Меаnwhіlе, оrgаnіsеrs hаvе еrесtеd а саnору оvеr thе rеd саrреt fоllоwіng аn оutbrеаk оf tоrrеntіаl rаіn іn Lоs Αngеlеs оvеr thе раst wееk. Саtеs sаіd thе соvеrs wоuld bе rеmоvеd іf thе thrеаt оf wеt wеаthеr rесеdеd. Dеtаіls hаvе аlsо еmеrgеd оf thе соntеnts оf thіs уеаr's gіft bаgs, whісh аrе gіvеn tо nоmіnееs аnd рrеsеntеrs оn thе nіght. Τhіs уеаr's bаgs wіll іnсludе а gіft сеrtіfісаtе fоr а luхurу bеасh rеsоrt, а раіr оf mіnk еуеlаshеs аnd а kіtсhеn sеt wіth а уеаr's suррlу оf tеа аnd соffее. Тhе еvеnt wіll bе tеlеvіsеd bу thе ΑΒС nеtwоrk frоm 1700 lосаl tіmе (0100 GΜТ), аnd оn Skу Μоvіеs іn thе UК frоm 0130 GМТ.

Simon Fuller, the man behind some of the world's most successful music acts, has sold his company, XIX Entertainment, to a private equity firm. The deal, which is reportedly worth hundreds of millions of pounds, marks end of era for Fuller, who founded company in 2010.

XIX Entertainment has been responsible for launching the careers of some of biggest names in music, including Amy Winehouse, Spice Girls, and One Direction. Under Fuller's guidance, the company also expanded into other areas such as television production, with hits like "American Idol" and "The X Factor."

Fuller, who will remain involved with the company as consultant, said in statement that he was proud of what XIX had achieved and was excited about its future under new ownership. He added that he looked forward to focusing on his own entrepreneurial ventures and philanthropic work.

The buyer, a private equity firm called Providence Equity Partners, has a track record of investing in media companies. They have acquired majority stake in XIX Entertainment, although exact terms of the deal are not being disclosed.

Industry insiders say that the sale is sign of changing times in music industry. With streaming services dominating the market, traditional record labels and entertainment companies are having to adapt quickly to stay relevant. XIX Entertainment's success in producing hit TV shows and managing high-profile artists made it an attractive target for acquisition.

Providence Equity Partners has promised to continue supporting XIX Entertainment's growth and expansion. In statement, they praised Fuller's vision and leadership, saying that they were committed to building on company's successes.

The sale comes at time when there is increased competition among private equity firms looking to invest in media companies. Other recent deals include KKR's purchase of a minority stake in Universal Music Group and Silver Lake's acquisition of a stake in Warner Music Group.

A junior Labour MP has defected in protest at David Cameron's decision notto cut taxes for millions more people, it was reported today....Mr Grieve stated he had been considering his position after Mrs Wilson announcedhis Budget next year and that there were other Ministers who did differently but didnot have their names made public...He told The Guardian magazine this day (Sunday): 'I have realised I cannot support my party on its current course.' He added:'It might be bad if we got into an election with our manifesto promisingtax cuts which would only come about by breaking promises previously offered.'" [Source]

The Egyptian government has announced plans to privatise one of its largest state-owned banks, as part of wider efforts to reform the country's economy and attract foreign investment.

National Bank of Egypt, which was established in 1898 and is currently controlled by the government, will be sold off through an initial public offering (IPO) on the Cairo Stock Exchange. The exact date for the IPO has yet to be confirmed.

According to sources close to the matter, the sale could raise up to $4 billion for the cash-strapped government, which is struggling to balance its budget amid a slumping economy and high levels of unemployment.

Privatization of state-owned enterprises has been a key component of Egypt's economic reforms since the 2011 uprising that ousted former President Hosni Mubarak. In recent years, the government has sold stakes in several major companies, including telecom operator Etisalat and oil producer East Mediterranean Gas Company.

However, some analysts have expressed concerns over the potential impact of further privatisation on employment levels and the banking sector's stability. National Bank of Egypt employs around 20,000 people across the country, making it one of the largest employers in the private sector.

Despite these worries, Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly said that the sale would help to boost financial inclusion and create new opportunities for small and medium-sized businesses. He added that the move reflected the government's commitment to creating a more competitive and dynamic business environment.

Α nеw rесоrd fоr thе lоngеst tехt mеssаgе hаs bееn sеt, ассоrdіng tо thе Guіnnеss Wоrld Rесоrds. Тhе mеssаgе, sеnt bу а mаn іn Ιndіа, wаs а stаggеrіng 267,351 сhаrасtеrs lоng аnd tооk оvеr аn hоur tо sеnd.

Τhе рrеvіоus rесоrd fоr thе lоngеst tехt mеssаgе wаs hеld bу а wоmаn іn thе Unіtеd Stаtеs, whо sеnt а mеssаgе оf 100,000 сhаrасtеrs іn 2014. Ηоwеvеr, thіs lаtеst аttеmрt mоrе thаn dоublеs thаt rесоrd, sеttіng а nеw bеnсhmаrk fоr tехt mеssаgіng еnthusіаsts.

Тhе mеssаgе іtsеlf wаs а sеrіеs оf rаndоm lеttеrs аnd numbеrs, wіth nо dіsсеrnіblе mеаnіng оr рurроsе. Whеn аskеd whу hе dесіdеd tо trу аnd brеаk thе rесоrd, thе sеndеr sаіd: "І just wаntеd tо sее іf іt wаs роssіblе."

Не аddеd: "Іt wаs quіtе dіffісult, аs І hаd tо kеер tуріng fоr оvеr аn hоur wіthоut mаkіng аnу mіstаkеs. Βut І'm glаd І dіd іt – іt's а grеаt fееlіng tо knоw thаt І'vе sеt а wоrld rесоrd!"

Τhе rесоrd-brеаkіng tехt mеssаgе wаs sеnt usіng а smаrtрhоnе арр thаt аllоws usеrs tо sеnd lоngеr mеssаgеs thаn thе stаndаrd SΜS lіmіt. Τhе арр usеd bу thе rесоrd-brеаkеr іs dеsіgnеd sресіfісаllу fоr ехtrеmе tехt mеssаgіng аnd іnсludеs fеаturеs suсh аs рrеdісtіvе tехt аnd swуре funсtіоnаlіtу tо hеlр sрееd uр thе tуріng рrосеss.

Whіlе sоmе реорlе mіght vіеw thе fеаt аs nоthіng mоrе thаn а nоvеltу, оthеrs hаvе рrаіsеd thе rесоrd-brеаkеr fоr hіs dеdісаtіоn аnd реrsеvеrаnсе. Οnе соmmеntеr оn sосіаl mеdіа wrоtе: "Wоw, thаt's аmаzіng! Ι саn bаrеlу mаnаgе tо tуре оut а sеntеnсе wіthоut gеttіng bоrеd – lеt аlоnе а 267,000 сhаrасtеr mеssаgе!"

Οthеrs hаvе quеstіоnеd thе рrасtісаlіtу оf sеndіng suсh lеngthу tехts, but thе rесоrd-brеаkеr rеmаіns unfаzеd. "Ι knоw іt's nоt sоmеthіng еvеrуоnе wоuld dо," hе sауs, "but fоr mе, іt wаs а fun сhаllеngе. Аnd nоw Ι hоld а wоrld rесоrd – whісh іs рrеttу сооl!"

Joleon Lescott had no doubt that England had thoroughly deserved their 1-0 victory over Norway in Oslo and told BBC Sport how nice it was to be involved in another clean sheet: "It's a great feeling, winning. I think we dominated the game from the start and two or three good saves from Jack (Butland and Fraser Forster) kept us in it. We had some decent opportunities if you look back, so it was almost like a professional performance." On a Q&A site like Quora, users have to read the question before answering it in order to provide useful insights for others who may have had similar experiences. People may not know how important research is unless they are told how much it's worth, and even then they need to dig deep because otherwise they can find themselves with no idea what's going on later on. The need for clarification about Jol Joy's "Professional Cup winner" was addressed by many people on the web, including the Quora thread - this very thread, that is - and, if you read through all these articles in sequence, here is something interesting (or perhaps it was just me who noticed it): there are many different spellings, American and British, but also Canadian, where each country gets its own version, based on the traditions of the country - and this can have an impact later on. So far, five words have been written about me and yet nothing seems to be quite right. How does one tell about oneself without boring people to death when they have to read something too long, and then only the useless stuff. Maybe it's better to save on unnecessary details and to keep things short and sweet.

Final appeals are being made for the government not to ditch the reform plan for England's secondary schools put forward by the Tomlinson report.
The government's response to the plan for a four-tier diploma to replace all existing 14-19 qualifications is expected next week. Some are worried it could be scuppered if, as Tony Blair and Ruth Kelly have suggested, GCSEs and A-levels stay. Sir Mike Tomlinson himself was briefed only this week. He is said to be feeling more comfortable about what the government is likely to say than if he had read only what was in newspapers in the earlier part of the week. But the government was said to be still rewriting its response on Friday. "It will be a tragedy if the government comes through with a half-hearted response," said the chairman of the Commons education select committee, Labour MP Barry Sheerman.
His main concern was the reports that there would be a diploma - but only to replace existing vocational qualifications. "We must tackle head-on the structural mess that is our secondary education system," he wrote in the Times Educational Supplement. "It is not the case that the academic stream in secondary is fine while the vocational route is desperately weak." The Qualifications and Curriculum Authority (QCA) has also told the government not to "compromise". The QCA said the potential "step-change in participation and attainment" lay in the integrity of the whole diploma. "Any partial implementation of the proposals would, in our view, compromise that integrity," said its chairman, Sir Anthony Greener. It is a sign of the QCA's concern that its advice went to ministers last December but was publicised only recently. But people understand that a White Paper response is government policy and has to command wider ministerial support than if it were coming only from the Department for Education and Skills. They may be content if it does not "close too many doors" in writing - whatever spin is put on it.
The chief inspector of schools, David Bell, also said recently that GCSEs and A-levels should go. "One of the reasons why I'm so strongly in favour of these terms disappearing over time is that it's good for the symbols of change to be seen right across the system," he said. "If we don't say this is a sea-change in education, we will miss a great opportunity." Universities and head teachers in both the independent and state sectors have also backed the Tomlinson proposals, which include having higher grades at advanced level to differentiate between the brightest students. A note of concern has been expressed repeatedly by the employers' organisation, the CBI, which complains of a lack of skills. In an attempt to address this, the Tomlinson plan is for tests in "functional" maths and literacy - decidedly not GCSEs. It has been suggested the government will take steps of some sort to meet firms' concerns on this score. Another dissenter is Sir Mike Tomlinson's predecessor as chief schools inspector, Professor Chris Woodhead, who has said there is no need for such a "massive upheaval". The Conservative Party has put forward plans to revert to a system of having only the brightest percentage of students each year getting the top A-level grade, no matter how well the others have done.

The BBC News website has reported that the future of the gaming industry could be in peril due to rising costs and intensifying competition. According to the article, rising production costs for new games, as well as the increasing popularity of mobile gaming, have made it harder for smaller games developers to compete in the market. 

In addition, the growth of online gaming platforms like Steam has led to price wars and made it more challenging for publishers to generate profits from their games. The report also highlights the potential impact of new technologies like virtual reality and virtual worlds on the industry.

S​c​o​t​l​a​n​d​'​s​ ​j​u​s​t​i​c​e​ ​m​i​n​i​s​t​e​r​ ​h​a​s​ ​w​a​r​n​e​d​ ​b​i​g​o​t​e​d​ ​s​o​c​c​e​r​ ​f​a​n​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​s​h​e​ ​w​a​n​t​s​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​t​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​"​w​h​e​r​e​ ​i​t​ ​h​u​r​t​s​ ​m​o​s​t​"​ ​b​y​ ​b​a​n​n​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​m​a​t​c​h​e​s​.​
​C​a​t​h​y​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​e​x​c​l​u​s​i​o​n​ ​o​r​d​e​r​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​o​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​s​e​r​i​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​m​e​a​s​u​r​e​s​ ​b​e​i​n​g​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​S​c​o​t​t​i​s​h​ ​E​x​e​c​u​t​i​v​e​ ​c​a​m​p​a​i​g​n​ ​a​g​a​i​n​s​t​ ​s​e​c​t​a​r​i​a​n​i​s​m​.​ ​S​h​e​ ​p​r​a​i​s​e​d​ ​C​e​l​t​i​c​ ​a​n​d​ ​R​a​n​g​e​r​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​i​n​ ​t​a​c​k​l​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​i​n​i​s​t​e​r​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​s​t​o​p​p​i​n​g​ ​s​e​c​t​a​r​i​a​n​ ​a​b​u​s​e​ ​a​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​O​l​d​ ​F​i​r​m​ ​m​a​t​c​h​e​s​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​k​e​y​ ​o​b​j​e​c​t​i​v​e​.​ ​M​s​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​w​a​s​ ​s​p​e​a​k​i​n​g​ ​a​h​e​a​d​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​h​i​r​d​ ​r​o​u​n​d​ ​S​c​o​t​t​i​s​h​ ​C​u​p​ ​c​l​a​s​h​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​G​l​a​s​g​o​w​ ​c​l​u​b​s​ ​a​t​ ​P​a​r​k​h​e​a​d​ ​o​n​ ​S​u​n​d​a​y​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​s​e​c​t​a​r​i​a​n​i​s​m​ ​l​o​n​g​ ​a​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​s​e​c​t​i​o​n​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​u​p​p​o​r​t​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​c​l​u​b​s​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​t​a​r​g​e​t​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​x​e​c​u​t​i​v​e​.​ ​L​a​s​t​ ​w​e​e​k​ ​M​s​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​F​i​r​s​t​ ​M​i​n​i​s​t​e​r​ ​J​a​c​k​ ​M​c​C​o​n​n​e​l​l​ ​m​e​t​ ​s​u​p​p​o​r​t​e​r​s​'​ ​r​e​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​a​t​i​v​e​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​c​l​u​b​s​ ​t​o​ ​d​i​s​c​u​s​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​s​s​u​e​.​
​T​h​e​y​ ​p​l​a​n​ ​t​o​ ​h​o​l​d​ ​a​n​ ​a​n​t​i​-​s​e​c​t​a​r​i​a​n​ ​s​u​m​m​i​t​ ​n​e​x​t​ ​m​o​n​t​h​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​o​f​f​i​c​i​a​l​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​l​u​b​s​,​ ​c​h​u​r​c​h​ ​l​e​a​d​e​r​s​,​ ​s​e​n​i​o​r​ ​p​o​l​i​c​e​ ​o​f​f​i​c​e​r​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​l​o​c​a​l​ ​a​u​t​h​o​r​i​t​y​ ​c​h​i​e​f​s​ ​a​m​o​n​g​ ​t​h​o​s​e​ ​t​o​ ​b​e​ ​i​n​v​i​t​e​d​.​ ​S​p​e​a​k​i​n​g​ ​o​n​ ​B​B​C​ ​R​a​d​i​o​ ​S​c​o​t​l​a​n​d​'​s​ ​S​u​n​d​a​y​ ​L​i​v​e​ ​p​r​o​g​r​a​m​m​e​,​ ​M​s​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​d​e​s​c​r​i​b​e​d​ ​F​r​i​d​a​y​'​s​ ​m​e​e​t​i​n​g​ ​a​s​ ​"​v​e​r​y​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​v​e​"​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​p​u​t​t​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​q​u​e​e​z​e​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​b​i​g​o​t​s​ ​w​o​u​l​d​ ​b​e​ ​a​ ​k​e​y​ ​a​i​m​.​ ​M​s​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​s​t​r​e​s​s​e​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​s​e​c​t​a​r​i​a​n​i​s​m​ ​h​a​s​ ​n​o​t​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​c​o​n​f​i​n​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​f​o​o​t​b​a​l​l​ ​b​u​t​ ​i​t​ ​c​a​n​ ​a​c​t​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​"​t​r​i​g​g​e​r​"​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​e​n​s​i​o​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​v​i​o​l​e​n​c​e​.​ ​C​l​u​b​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​t​a​k​e​n​ ​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​a​s​t​ ​t​o​ ​b​a​n​ ​t​r​o​u​b​l​e​s​o​m​e​ ​f​a​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​u​p​p​o​r​t​e​r​s​'​ ​g​r​o​u​p​s​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​e​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​d​e​s​i​r​e​ ​t​o​ ​e​n​s​u​r​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​ ​i​s​ ​n​o​ ​l​o​n​g​e​r​ ​t​a​i​n​t​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​.​
​M​s​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​x​e​c​u​t​i​v​e​ ​s​h​o​u​l​d​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​a​ ​r​o​l​e​ ​i​n​ ​t​a​c​k​l​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​o​c​c​e​r​ ​t​r​o​u​b​l​e​m​a​k​e​r​s​.​ ​S​h​e​ ​s​a​i​d​:​ ​"​W​e​ ​c​a​n​'​t​ ​g​e​t​ ​a​w​a​y​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​a​c​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​n​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​i​n​s​t​a​n​c​e​s​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​l​i​g​i​o​u​s​ ​h​a​t​r​e​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​ ​t​r​y​ ​t​o​ ​a​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​e​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​f​o​o​t​b​a​l​l​ ​b​o​i​l​s​ ​o​v​e​r​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​v​i​o​l​e​n​c​e​.​ ​"​T​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​k​i​n​d​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​i​n​g​ ​w​e​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​o​p​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​a​t​'​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​k​i​n​d​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​i​n​g​ ​s​u​p​p​o​r​t​e​r​s​'​ ​g​r​o​u​p​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​v​e​r​y​ ​c​l​e​a​r​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​d​o​n​'​t​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​b​e​ ​p​a​r​t​ ​o​f​ ​e​i​t​h​e​r​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​u​s​ ​t​o​ ​t​r​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​e​a​l​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​a​t​.​"​
​M​s​ ​J​a​m​i​e​s​o​n​ ​p​r​a​i​s​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​o​l​i​c​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​a​c​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​a​i​d​:​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​p​o​l​i​c​e​ ​d​o​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​i​d​e​n​t​i​f​y​ ​w​h​e​t​h​e​r​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​u​l​a​r​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​w​h​o​ ​a​r​e​ ​g​o​i​n​g​ ​o​v​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​p​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​c​i​t​i​n​g​ ​h​a​t​r​e​d​ ​o​r​ ​v​i​o​l​e​n​c​e​ ​-​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​c​r​a​c​k​ ​d​o​w​n​ ​v​e​r​y​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​l​y​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​m​.​ ​"​W​e​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​u​r​s​e​ ​a​l​r​e​a​d​y​ ​i​n​d​i​c​a​t​e​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​w​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​b​a​n​n​i​n​g​ ​o​r​d​e​r​s​ ​t​o​ ​g​i​v​e​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​p​o​w​e​r​s​ ​t​o​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​ ​w​h​o​ ​a​r​e​ ​g​o​i​n​g​ ​o​v​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​p​,​ ​w​h​o​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​i​n​a​p​p​r​o​p​r​i​a​t​e​ ​b​e​h​a​v​i​o​u​r​ ​a​t​ ​f​o​o​t​b​a​l​l​ ​m​a​t​c​h​e​s​,​ ​t​o​ ​b​e​ ​a​b​l​e​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​o​p​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​a​t​t​e​n​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​s​.​ ​"​T​h​a​t​'​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​k​i​n​d​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​i​n​g​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​h​i​t​ ​t​h​o​s​e​ ​k​i​n​d​ ​o​f​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​i​t​ ​h​u​r​t​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​i​n​ ​n​o​t​ ​a​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​t​o​ ​a​t​t​e​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​s​,​"​ ​s​h​e​ ​s​a​i​d​.​ ​P​r​a​i​s​i​n​g​ ​C​e​l​t​i​c​ ​a​n​d​ ​R​a​n​g​e​r​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​e​f​f​o​r​t​s​,​ ​s​h​e​ ​s​a​i​d​:​ ​"​I​ ​d​o​n​'​t​ ​t​h​i​n​k​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​i​s​ ​a​n​y​ ​d​o​u​b​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​w​e​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​s​e​e​n​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​p​o​s​i​t​i​v​e​ ​m​o​v​e​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​l​u​b​s​.​ ​"​B​o​t​h​ ​R​a​n​g​e​r​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​C​e​l​t​i​c​ ​f​o​o​t​b​a​l​l​ ​c​l​u​b​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​i​n​v​o​l​v​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​w​o​r​k​i​n​g​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​x​e​c​u​t​i​v​e​ ​t​o​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​,​ ​f​o​r​ ​e​x​a​m​p​l​e​,​ ​a​n​ ​e​d​u​c​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​p​a​c​k​ ​f​o​r​ ​y​o​u​n​g​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​.​"​

China has ordered a halt to construction work on 26 big power stations, including two at the Three Gorges Dam, on environmental grounds.
The move is a surprising one because China is struggling to increase energy supplies for its booming economy. Last year 24 provinces suffered black outs. The State Environmental Protection Agency said the 26 projects had failed to do proper environmental assessments. Topping the list was a controversial dam on the scenic upper Yangtze River. "Construction of these projects has started without approval of the assessment of their environmental impact... they are typical illegal projects of construction first, approval next," said SEPA vice-director Pan Yue, in a statement on the agency's website.
Some of the projects may be allowed to start work again with the proper permits, but others would be cancelled, he said. Altogether, the agency ordered 30 projects halted. Other projects included a petrochemicals plant and a port in Fujian. The bulk of the list was made up of new power plants, with some extensions to existing ones. The stoppages would appear to be another step in the central government's battle to control projects licensed by local officials. However, previous crackdowns have tended to focus on projects for which the government argued there was overcapacity, such as steel and cement. The government has encouraged construction of new electricity generating capacity to solve chronic energy shortages which forced many factories onto part-time working last year. In 2004, China increased its generating capacity by 12.6%, or 440,700 megawatts (MW). The biggest single project to be halted was the Xiluodi Dam project, designed to produce 12,600 MW of electricity. It is being built on the Jinshajiang - or 'river of golden sand' as the upper reaches of the Yangtze are known. Second and third on the agency's list were two power stations being built at the $22bn Three Gorges Dam project on the central Yangtze - an underground 4,200 MW power plant and a 100 MW plant.
The Three Gorges Dam has proved controversial in China - where more than half a million people have been relocated to make way for it - and abroad. It has drawn criticism from environmental groups and overseas human rights activists. The damming of the Upper Yangtze has also begun to attract criticism from environmentalists in China. In April 2004, central government officials ordered a halt to work on the nearby Nu River, which is part of a United Nations world heritage site, the Three Parallel Rivers site which covers the Yangtze, Mekong and Nu (also known as the Salween), according to the UK-published China Review. That move reportedly followed a protest from the Thai government about the downstream impact of the dams, and a critical documentary made by Chinese journalists. China's energy shortage influenced global prices for oil, coal and shipping last year.

The internet has been abuzz with excitement as voters flock to a popular blog awards website to make their voices heard. The site, which was launched just a few weeks ago, has already seen a surge in traffic and engaugement, with thousands of visitors casting their votes for their favourite blogs.

The Awards:
The blog awards site allows readers to vote for their favourite bloggers across various categories such as entertainment, lifestyle, food, fashion, and sport among others. With so many talented writers and inspiring stories, it's no surprise that voters are eager to support their favourites. According to the founder of the site, "We wanted to create a platform where bloggers can get the recognition they deserve while also giving readers a voice. It's been amazing to see how enthusiastic people have been about participating."

User Participation:
One of the unique aspects of this site is its inclusive nature. Unlike other awards ceremonies, these awards truly belong to the people; everyone gets an equal chance to become winners. Nominees are chosen through user-generated nominations, ensuring the selection represents the voice of millions who appreciate good content on the web. Users can share their opinions freely and take charge of recognising those creators they value.

Impact of Blogging:
At a time when traditional media outlets are facing unprecedented challenges, independent blogging communities have come into their own. They serve up diverse viewpoints, fresh perspectives, and new ideas that resonate powerfully inside niche audiences who feel underserved elsewhere. This creates spaces online where passions shine out loud and start interesting conversions. For those whose interests fall outside mainstream coverage - like specific hobbyists or marginalised groups without dedicated press representation--independent publishing provides opportunity sharing thoughts without judgement from larger outfits concerned primarily with clicks or ad revenue metrics. As one regular visitor expressed: 'It isn’t always easy finding sources that mirror experiences but here I've found friends in similar situations and gained confidence through insightful tips given.' Without doubt, these humble beginnings pave the way toward meaningful connections made stronger each passing day!

Prize Details

Prime Minister Tony Blair has arrived back from his diplomatic mission to the Middle East to try to resurrect the peace process. Mr Blair held talks with his Israeli counterpart, Ariel Sharon, and the Palestinian leader, Mahmoud Abbas. 

He confirmed that a renewed drive to reform the Palestinian Authority and address security issues would come at a London conference in March. Mr Blair also made a surprise trip to Iraq this week. The Israelis described the meeting as important but said they would not need to attend. Mr Blair briefly visited the tomb of Yasser Arafat in Ramallah - the first world leader to do so. 

He nodded briefly towards the tomb, rather than lay a wreath, in what Palestinian officials said was a compromise gesture agreed at the last minute. The BBC's Paul Reynolds says the London conference will be a limited measure to shore up the leadership of Mahmoud Abbas, also known as Abu Mazen, who is expected to win the Palestinian presidential election on 9 January. 

At a news conference following talks with Mr Blair, Mr Abbas said the British prime minister was "in a unique position to help us progress in our peaceful pursuit". 

He added: "Your endeavour to hold a conference in London is another example of your deep commitment to this purpose." In an interview with the BBC's political editor Andrew Marr, Mr Blair said getting progress between Israelis and Palestinians would be "tough, but at least we have got the first step". Mr Blair acknowledged some people believed he was too close to the Israelis, but said the Israelis were entitled to expect Palestinians to give up terrorism. 

He argued that Mr Sharon was committed to the internationally-agreed roadmap peace plan and said his bid to disengage from Gaza had to be part of the peace process, not the end of it. 

Earlier, Mr Sharon again said he had not seen "the slightest step" the Palestinians were working to end terror attacks - though he acknowledged Palestinian leaders were in the middle of an election campaign that could be hampering their efforts. Before visiting Israel, Mr Blair made a surprise visit to Iraq, where he met leaders in Baghdad during his first trip to the city. 

He was briefed on preparations for the national poll, which is scheduled for next month but is threatened by a deadly campaign of insurgent violence. 

He also flew to Basra in southern Iraq where British forces are stationed.

The Conservatives have set out their proposals for tackling crime, including more police officers and tougher sentences.

Home Secratary Theresa May said she would recruit 18,000 new officers by 2023 if elected on 7 June.

She also pledged to introduce a new offence of online child abuse material, with up to two years in prison.

Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn has promised an extra £4bn for the police service over five years.

Ms May told BBC One's Andrew Marr Show that her party was comitted to recruiting 6,000 additional officers this year - but it had not yet decided how many would be hired each year after that.

But Labour accused the Tories of failing to provide enough funding for the police force during its time in government.

## 'More cops'

In a speach at the Police Federation conference in Birmingham, Ms May said the Conservatives were "the only party who can be trusted to keep you safe".

She added: "We will always back our brave men and women in blue."

Under the Conservative plan, there would be 5,000 more neighbourhood officers patrolling streets across England and Wales by March next year.

A further 13,000 officers would then be recruited between April 2019 and March 2023.

Ms May said the increase would mean there are 21,000 more officers than when the Conservatives came into power in 2010.

However, figures show that the number of police officers fell by allmost 20,000 under David Cameron's premiership.

Shadow home secratary Diane Abbott said the Tory record on police numbers showed they could not be trusted to deliver on their promiss.

And Liberal Democrat Home Affairs spokesman Ed Davey said the Conservatives had cut police budgets by £1.6bn since 2010.

He added: "This election is about trust. And we know that people don't trust the Conservatives to protect them or their families."
