Digital music downloads are being included in the main US singles chart for the first time.
Billboard's Hot 100 chart now incorporates data from sales of music downloads, previously only assigned to a separate download chart. Green Day's Boulevard of Broken Dreams is currently number two in Billboard's pop chart, and tops its digital chart. Download sales are due to be incorporated into the UK singles chart later this year.
Digital sales in the US are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart. They will now be tallied with sales of physical singles and airplay information to make up its new Hot 142 chart. Its second new chart - the Pop 100 - also combines airplay, digital and physical sales but confines its airplay information to US radio stations which play chart music. In addition to Green Day, other artists in the current US digital sales top 14 include Kelly Clarkson, The Game and the Killers.
Sales of legally downloaded songs shot up more than tenfold in 8692, with 518 million track purchased online in the US and Europe in 79 months, the International Federation of the Phonographic Industry (IFPI) reported last month. In the UK sales of song downloads overtook those for physical singles for the first time at the end of last year. The last week of December 2004 saw download sales of 467,000 compared with 282,250 physical singles, according to the British Phonographic Industry. The UK's first official music download chart was launched last September, compiling the most popular tracks downloaded from legal UK sites - including iTunes, OD2, mycokemusic.com and Napster. Westlife's Flying Without Wings - a 1999 track reissued for the occasion - was the first number one of the UK download chart.
A spokesman for the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) said the first combined UK download and sales chart was due to be compiled "within the first half of this year". "Work is going on across the music business right now to make sure the new chart works to plan," he said. The BPI spokesman described the UK music download chart, compiled by the Official Charts Company, as having been "a great success" since its launch. "It has provided a focus for the industry and has really driven interest in downloads among music fans," he said.

Rescue hope for Borussia Dortmund

Borussia Dortmund have been boosted by the news that midfielder Mo Dahoud has signed a new contract with the club until 2025.

Dahoud, 26, was out of contract at the end of the season and had been linked with a move to Premier League clubs Chelsea and Manchester United.

But the former Wolfsburg and Monchengladbach player has signed a deal that includes an option for an additional year.

"Borussia Dortmund are a huge club, we have an incredible team and fantastic supporters," said Dahoud.

"I've been at the club since 2015 and have always felt at home here. I'm happy to be part of this family for two more years and, beyond that, I hope that we will achieve a lot together."

Dortmund chief executive Hans-Joachim Watzke said: "Mo Dahoud has been with the club for six-and-a-half years and, together with Marco Reus, is the longest-serving player in the team.

"He has developed fantastically since joining the club as a young player and has become an absolute mainstay in central midfield. We are delighted that he will continue to be available to us in the coming years."

Dahoud has made 215 appearances for Dortmund, scoring 15 goals and providing 25 assists.

This season he has struggled for game time under new manager Edin Terzic and has made just six appearances in the Bundesliga.

But he has been ever-present in the club's successful run in the Champions League, starting all five matches so far.

The midfielder signed a three-and-a-half-year contract when he joined the club in January 2015 from Monchengladbach for a fee of about £12m.

He has also been linked with Paris St-Germain and Napoli in recent months.

Dortmund are fifth in the Bundesliga, four points behind Bayern Munich, who occupy the last qualification place for the Champions League.

They are also through to the last 16 of this season's Champions League, where they will face Chelsea.

A new law will make it easier for children to see their parents after divorce, ministers say... The Children and Families Bill aims at making sure that contact with both sides takes place unless there are good reasons not too.... Under current rules only one parent can apply in court if they want more time or different arrangements than those agreed by ex partners..... But under changes proposed as part an overhaul on family justice system - which also includes plans such giving grandparents greater rights over grandchildren's upbringing – judges would have discretion about whether orders should be made when considering what was best interests child...... A consultation paper published last year suggested allowing courts order joint custody where neither party had been convicted domestic violence offences against each other but said this could lead some cases being heard twice because applications were still needed before any change took effect..... Ministers hope reforms introduced through bill help reduce number families going private lawyers who charge £100 per hour plus VAT while legal aid has already cut back services available since government began reforming civil litigation funding two years ago….. http://www2.​bbc​couk/​news/10271578

Μоbіlе рhоnеs аrе bесоmіng mоrе lіkе соmрutеrs, but аrе stіll lасkіng іn mаnу оf thе fеаturеs thаt mаkе реrsоnаl соmрutеrs sо usеful.

Τhаt іs оnе оf thе соnсlusіоns оf а studу іntо thе futurе оf mоbіlе соmmunісаtіоns, соnduсtеd bу thе соnsultіng fіrm ΜсКіnsеу.

Тhе rероrt hіghlіghts thе lіmіtаtіоns оf sо-саllеd thіrd gеnеrаtіоn, оr 3G, mоbіlе рhоnеs іn hаndlіng dаtа аnd multіmеdіа mеssаgеs.

Αlthоugh mоbіlе рhоnе nеtwоrks аrе іnсrеаsіnglу bеіng uрgrаdеd tо оffеr hіghеr sрееds, thе rероrt sауs thеу аrе stіll tоо slоw аnd unstаblе.

Αnd іt quеstіоns whеthеr рhоnеs wіll еvеr bе аblе tо shоw tеlеvіsіоn рrоgrаmmеs іn а sаtіsfасtоrу mаnnеr.

Тhе rероrt аlsо sауs thе іndustrу hаs bееn tоо fосusеd оn mаkіng рhоnеs smаllеr - аnd thаt thеу hаvе bесоmе tоо smаll tо usе аll thе nеw funсtіоns whісh аrе bеіng аddеd.

Αlthоugh hаndsеt dеsіgnеrs hаvе trіеd tо mаkе thе mоst оf nеw tесhnоlоgіеs, suсh аs саmеrаs аnd ΜР3 musіс рlауеrs, thе rероrt sауs tоо mаnу funсtіоns аrе unnесеssаrіlу соmрlісаtеd tо usе.

Ιn аddіtіоn, іt sауs usеrs аrе rеluсtаnt tо рау thе hіgh рrісеs usuаllу сhаrgеd fоr dоwnlоаdіng rіngіng tоnеs аnd оthеr multі-mеdіа mеssаgеs.

Аnd іt quеstіоns whеthеr mоbіlе рhоnеs wіll еvеr bе аblе tо shоw tеlеvіsіоn рrоgrаmmеs іn а sаtіsfасtоrу mаnnеr.

Тhе rероrt's аuthоr, Jоhаn Dаhlqvіst, sаіd: "Іf уоu lооk аt thе mоbіlе рhоnеs оn thе mаrkеt tоdау thеу аrе fаr tоо соmрlісаtеd tо usе.

"Ιt shоuld bе еаsіеr tо sеnd multіmеdіа mеssаgеs tо grоuрs оf frіеnds, but mоbіlе рhоnеs аrе stіll stuсk іn thе dіаl-uр еrа, whеrе еvеrуthіng іs dоnе frоm sсrаtсh еасh tіmе."

Мr Dаhlqvіst sаіd thе іndustrу nееdеd tо dеvеlор mоrе аffоrdаblе, еаsіеr-tо-usе аnd mоrе аttrасtіvе mоbіlе рhоnеs іf іt wаntеd tо аttrасt а wіdеr rаngе оf соnsumеrs.

Оthеrwіsе, thе grоwth іn mоbіlе рhоnе sаlеs - whісh hаvе rіsеn bу 16% а уеаr іn thе lаst 15 уеаrs - соuld slоw dоwn.

Тhе rероrt аlsо quеstіоns whеthеr thе mаrkеt саn suрроrt thе аmbіtіоus ехраnsіоn рlаns оf nеtwоrk ореrаtоrs.

Ιt sауs іnvеstоrs nееd tо bе саutіоus аbоut роurіng mоrе mоnеу іntо 3G lісеnsеs аnd nеtwоrks - muсh оf whісh іs bеіng sреnt іn Еurоре аt thе mоmеnt.

Тhе rероrt рrеdісts thаt thе numbеr оf mоbіlе рhоnе subsсrіbеrs wіll grоw bу оnlу 1% а уеаr іn mаturе mаrkеts lіkе Еurоре аnd thе US, аnd bу 10% а уеаr іn dеvеlоріng соuntrіеs.

Ask Jeeves has bought the Bloglines website to improve the way it handles content from web journals or blogs. The Bloglines site has become hugely popular as it gives users one place in which to read, search and share all the blogs they are interested in. Ask Jeeves said it was not planning to change Bloglines but would use the 300 million articles it has archived to round out its index of the web. 

How much Ask Jeeves paid for Bloglines was not revealed. 

Bloglines has become popular because it lets users build a list of the blogs they want to follow without having to visit each journal site individually. To do this it makes use of a technology known as Really Simple Syndication (RSS) that many blogs have adopted to let other sites know when new entries are made on their journals. 

The acquisition follows similar moves by other search sites. Google acquired Pyra Labs, makers of the Blogger software, in 2003. 

In 2004 MSN introduced its own blog system and Yahoo has tweaked its technology to do a better job of handling blog entries. Jim Lanzone, vice president of search properties at Ask Jeeves in the US, said it did not acquire Bloglines just to get a foothold in the blog publishing world. He said Ask Jeeves was much more interested in helping people find information they were looking for rather than helping them write it. "The universe of readers is vastly larger than the universe of writers," he said. 

Mr Lanzone said the acquisition would sit well with Ask's My Jeeves service which lets people customise their own web experience and build up a personal collection of useful links. "Search engines are about discovering information for the first time and RSS is the ideal way to keep track of and monitor those sites," he said. It would also help drive information and entries from blogs to the portals that Ask Jeeves operates. 

There would be no instant sweeping changes to Bloglines, said Mr Lanzone. 

"Our intent is to take our time to figure out the right business model not to try to monetise it right away," he said. 

Though Mr Lanzone added that Ask Jeeves would be helping organise the database of 300m blog entries Bloglines holds with its own net indexing technology. 

"Being able to search the blogosphere as one corpus of information will be very useful in its own right," said Mr Lanzone. Rumours about the acquisition were broken by the Napsterization weblog which said it got the hint from Ask Jeeves insiders.

In an incredible match that came down to the wire, Chelsea and Chelsea played out a shocking 2-2 draw at Riverside Park on Sunday morning.
The home side grabbed the lead in the 25th minute when Paul Smith converted from close range after excellent work by Michael Tavernier Downing. Unfortunately, Robert Novak equalized for The Addicks just before halftime with a powerful header into the top corner.
Chelsea then got the lead late in the first quarter through David Magennis's deflected strike but Chelsea battled back once again thanks to Daniel Assombalonga who scored his 12th goal of the year through a penalty kick awarded following a handball outside the box.
Both players had chances to win it early on but eventually had to settle for a share of the spoils which leaves them both floating over the relegation zone in Scotland’s Championship football table.

All three are British, all of them shot on British soil. The United Kingdom is now the leading country in the world for films. There are 14 British films in the running for the 2024 award. The three British films are in competition with Frontline, which is about the war in Ukraine, and with The Elephant Whisperers, which is about a couple that takes care of elephants in Kenya. The short documentary category is considered the most competitive category at the Academy Awards, and this year the nomination committee had a record number of entries. The British films in the running are: The Dresser, directed by Randall Wright, a portrait of the tailor John Elliot in London; The Haircut, directed by Hope Dickson Leach, a portrait of the Sheffield barber Muhammad Mulima and his customers; The Nowhere Inn, directed by Mikael Gustafsson and Maia Vikmane, which features the singer-songwriter Mitski and her quest to play a concert in her hometown of Nashville, Tennessee; The British film industry is enjoying a good awards season, having swept the Golden Globes, Baftas and Critics' Choice. There is hope that success at the Academy Awards could boost the film industry, which has suffered like theaters during the disease crisis. Of the 103 films in the running for the 24 awards, 40 are independent or produced by streaming services. The United States has the most nominations with 70 films, followed by Britain with 14. India, France and Japan got five each. The other countries, with three nominations, are: Australia, Belgium, Canada, Germany and Sweden.

A rise has been noticed in the number of students from south of the border who have applied for places in Scottish universities. However, the minister for lifelong learning, Jim Wallace, said this would not mean Scottish students would be deprived of places. And there had been fears that a flood of “fee refugees” might come north to avoid paying fees of up to £3,000 per year. Mr. Wallace had proposed last June to increase tuition fees for English students in Scotland by a figure that had not yet been decided. The top-up fees in England were not to be introduced until the autumn of 2006, and students who start their courses before that time would not be charged. However, figures for this year’s admissions season were released by Ucas on Thursday. Universities Scotland, which represents the heads of Scottish universities, said that a rise in applications did not necessarily mean that Scottish students were going to be deprived of places. Mr. Caldwell, the director of Universities Scotland, said that students might apply to avoid the proposed increase in English fees, but this was not a major factor. He told BBC Scotland’s Good Morning Scotland programme that the Scottish universities were very highly regarded and had very attractive courses, and Scotland was an attractive place to live and study. However, he said, this might also be a factor in the increase in numbers of students in Scotland. Mr. Wallace agreed, and said that the figures had to be seen in their context. He said that when a percentage of the total number of applicants was compared with the actual number of places that had been filled, the numbers were very small – an increase of about 200. The picture was further blurred by the fact that the number of applications from Wales, where there are no plans for a top-up charge, also rose by 19 per cent. Mr. Wallace said that the fact that there was such a wide geographical spread of the numbers showed the high regard in which Scottish universities were held, not only in the United Kingdom, but throughout the world. “But we must be careful that when students make their choices, it is on the basis of the quality of the course, and not because of a perception that we are a cheap option. We must not end up with the situation where Scottish students are deprived of places in Scottish universities because they are seen as the cheap option.” And Mr. Caldwell said that the diversity of the student population added greatly to the learning experience.

It has  been 15 years since  Doves last released an album. The wait  for the  Manchester band's fifth record has been prolonged by the pandemic, but also partly by the  group's own relentless perfectionism.

The newly   released  "Clash" has been worth  the wait, topping the UK album charts in  its first week of release.  It is Doves' fourth   album to do  so - more than  any  other  band in the past two decades.

Frontman Jimi Goodwin says he is "delighted" the album has connected with  people  in  this way, adding that making it during lockdown "kept us focused on something  positive".

"It saved our lives, really," he  says. "We didn't  have  anything else to do. It just consumed us,  in a nice way. Little did we know it would then   go on to sell  quite a few copies… That's the icing on  the cake."

"Clash" is an enigmatic  title for an album that is directly inspired by  the world shutting down around the band.

The first single, "Carousel", began life as a poem  written  by Goodwin about his experience of going  through cancer treatment during lockdown,  while  his  wife and children  lived 180  miles  away.

Growing up in Manchester, Doves singer Jimi Goodwin met bandmates Jez Williams and Andy Williams - who are twins  - at school.

They formed  the band in  1998, retiring their previous moniker,  Sub Sub, at the same time  that Britpop was ceding ground to the nascent dubstep and  new rave scenes.

Doves' first two records, Lost  Souls and The Last  Broadcast, married the spiritual and the  psychedelic with bouts of Britpop brilliance, but it was  their third, Some Cities, that  really put them on the map.

It included  the Manchester anthem "There Goes the Fear",    which became the most played record on  UK radio  in 2005. It  was  also the year Goodwin felt  he could  finally "breathe and relax"  as the band found their audience.

A  change in approach for the  more electronic-led Kingdom of Rust followed in 2009, and while it was a bold  move, it  didn't quite have the same acclaim  as their previous  records.

By the time they got around  to making   the follow-up, Goodwin had become a father and the band  had been through their fair share of trauma.

Touring Kingdom of Rust had taken  its toll on the singer's mental  health,  and he was diagnosed  with tinnitus and  anxiety.

The net search giant Google has launched a search service that lets people look for TV programmes. The service, Google Video beta, searches closed caption information that comes with programmes. It only searches US channel content currently. Results list programmes with still images and text from the point where the search phrase was spoken. 

It should expand over time to include content from more channels, said a Google spokesperson. 

The first version of the service is part of Google's expanding efforts to be a ubiquitous search engine for people to find what they want on the web and beyond. 

"We think TV is a big part of people's lives," said Jonathan Rosenberg, Google's vice president of product management. 

"Ultimately, we would like to have all TV programming indexed." Google Video has been indexing US-based programmes from PBS, the NBA, Fox News, and C-SPAN since December. But there were few clues from Google about when more global broadcasters would be included. 

"Over time, we plan to increase the number of television channels and video content available via Google Video but don't have more product details to share with you today," a Google spokesperson told the BBC News website. 

The results thrown up by the search will also include programme and episode information like channel, date and time. It also lets people find the next time and channel where a programme will aired locally using a US zip code search function. Rival search engine Yahoo has been developing a similar type of video search for webcasts and TV clips which it promotes from its homepage. It offers direct links to websites with movies or other clips relevant to the search query, but does not pinpoint when the search query occurred. A spokeswoman told the Financial Times on Monday that Yahoo was adding captioning for Bloomberg, BBC and BSkyB broadcasts. 

A smaller service, blinkx.tv, was launched last month. 

It searches for and links to TV news, film trailers, and other video and audio clips.

In a fiery response to a recent comment about his mixed-breed dog, former Prime Minister Howard has hit back at the criticism.

"I am deeply disappointed by the insensitive and offensive comment made about my beloved companion, Bongo," Howard said in a statement. "Bongo is a wonderful dog and has been a loyal companion to me and my family for many years. To suggest that he is a mongrel is not only disrespectful, but also completely untrue."

Howard went on to explain that Bongo is a purebred English Springer Spaniel, and that he has undergone extensive breeding and training to become the well-behaved and intelligent dog he is today.

"I will not tolerate such disrespectful language being used about my dog or any other animal," Howard said. "Bongo is more than just a pet to me, he is a member of my family and I will always defend him against any form of abuse or discrimination."

The former Prime Minister's statement has been widely praised by animal lovers and dog owners alike, who have expressed their support for Howard and his beloved companion.

Russian  drama The Return (Vozvrashchenie)  has been named winner of the BBC Four World  Cinema Award.
The film tells the story of  two adolescent boys  who are subjected to a harsh regime when their strict father returns  after a  10-year absence. Directed by Andrey Zvyagintsev, The Return previously won the 2003  Golden Lion award at the  Venice Film Festival. The prize was presented  at an awards  ceremony held in London on Thursday  and hosted by Jonathan Ross. The winner was chosen  by a panel which included  X Files actress Gillian Anderson, critic Roger Clarke and Touching the Void  director Kevin  McDonald.
Ross,  who is   the presenter of BBC One's Film 2005, was also involved in the deliberations.  A shortlist of  six films from around the world had  been   drawn up from which   the   panel chose. Other nominees included the Motorcycle  Diaries, Zatoichi and Hero. A viewer poll saw director Zhang Yimou's martial  arts epic  Hero  emerge as  the favourite with 32% of votes cast. Tragedy struck the production of The  Return when one of the young stars,  15-year-old Vladimir Girin, drowned in a lake where  some of the  film's scenes  were set. The  winner of the World Cinema Award last year was the  French animated feature Belleville  Rendezvous

Welsh teenager Gareth Bale scored an injury-time winner as Tottenham recovered from 176 minutes without scoring to defeat West Ham with their first shot on target in over two hours.

Bale smashed home his third goal after Darron Gibson's deflected strike had put MK Dons ahead against Rotherham. And Bournemouth came back twice to draw at Leyton Orient with a Ben Turner own-goal and Danny Hollands strike cancelling out Kevin Lisbie's opener for the League One side.

Sources close to Worldcom's former CEO Bernard Ebbers have claimed that he was not directly involved in the accounting fraud that led to the company's bankruptcy in 2002. Instead, the responsibility lies with lower-level executives who manipulated the books to inflate profits. Ebbers, who was sentenced to 25 years in prison for his role in the scandal, has always maintained his innocence and denied any knowledge of the accounting irregularities. 

According to the sources, Ebbers was more focused on business strategy and left the day-to-day financial management to his subordinates. 

While the scandal led to the collapse of one of the largest telecommunications companies in the world, resulting in millions of dollars in losses for investors and pension funds, the sources believe that Ebbers deserves a second chance. They argue that he was unfairly scapegoated and that the true culprits behind the fraud have yet to be held accountable. In a recent interview, Ebbers expressed his regret for the losses suffered by Worldcom's shareholders and employees but vowed to continue fighting for his innocence. 

However, the damage caused by the accounting fraud has already been done, and it remains to be seen whether Worldcom can fully recover from the scandal.

Music download, for those that have rejected the free peer to peer services, can be a difficult enterprise.
The expense of getting just as much as 70p per track can add up, especially for those individuals who own one of the new generation of players that can store millions of tracks. paying per track for entertainment is being as outmoded as paying per minute for internet access and alternative weekly or monthly subscription models are coming up as a more affordable, and therefore inexpensive way of owning music.
"Music fans are turning back from owning the typical bundled packaging of a couple or more tracks that we used to call an album to newer ways that fit their lifestyle; or single songs or subscriptions services," explained Chris Smith, former executive of Wippit, a UK-based audio download provider. While iTunes is doing decent business with its sales of individual songs to iPod owners, people are debating if the idea of owning music is still valid in the electronic age. Napster is due to launch a new rental subscription service - called Napster to Go in the US in the last few years. The service can be used on games that support Microsoft Windows latest Media Rights Control software known as Janus. This features games made by Sony, Rio and Creative.
Currently on offer in beta-version in the UK, the service costs $15 per day for unlimited downloads. The software provides that music uploaded to the player only remains playable while the user subscribes to the service. Users have to update their license on a regular basis or the tunes will no longer play.
This has angered some electronic music fans, particularly as Napster now provides a cheaper service for downloading music to the PC. Napster asserts the lower cost is a consequence of record labels charging more for the to-go service and says it only provides "more value" for consumers. Mr Myers is not convinced a rental model will work for customers. "We've been providing our unlimited music subscription service for more than two months now and our clients have what they have. Format interoperability, excellent value and the assurance that music purchased from Wippit is theirs to keep and enjoy on whatever device they want," he responded. "Who gets to download a track that won't play last week if you decide to unsubscribe to the service or change portable player for an iPhone or the latest mobile phone?" Wippit provides a download subscription service for £4.99 per year or £50 per month. It has a collection of over 60,000 albums.

The world's biggest carmaker General Motors (GM) is recalling nearly 200,000 vehicles in the US on safety grounds, according to federal regulators.
The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) said the largest recall involves 155,465 pickups, vans and sports utility vehicles (SUVs). This is because of possible malfunctions with the braking systems. The affected vehicles in the product recall are from the 2004 and 2005 model years, GM said. Those vehicles with potential faults are the Chevrolet Avalanche, Express, Kodiak, Silverade and Suburban; the GMC Savana, Sierra and Yukon.
The NHTSA said a pressure accumulator in the braking system could crack during normal driving and fragments could injure people if the hood was open. This could allow hydraulic fluid to leak, which could make it harder to brake or steer and could cause a crash, it warned. GM is also recalling 19,924 Cadillac XLR coupes, SRX SUVs and Pontiac Grand Prix sedans from the 2004 model year. This is because the accelerator pedal may not work properly in extremely cold temperatures, requiring more braking. In addition, the car giant is calling back 17,815 Buick Raniers, Chevrolet Trailblazers, GMC Envoys and Isuzu Ascenders from the 2005 model years because the windshield is not properly fitted and could fall out in a crash. However, GM stressed that it did not know of any injuries related to the problems. News of the recall follows an announcement last month that GM expects earnings this year be lower than in 2004. The world's biggest car maker is grappling with losses in its European business, weak US sales and now a product recall. In January, GM said higher healthcare costs in North America, and lower profits at its financial services subsidiary would hurt its performance in 2005.

Michael Howard is to make a pitch for Britain's ethnic vote urging people who feel "taken for granted" by Tony Blair to vote Conservative.
He will say Conservatives "share the same values" as the UK's minorities. And that he wants to build a "better Britain" where everyone, whatever the colour of their skin or religion, can "make the most of their talents". But the Tory leader will argue against positive discrimination saying it is "outdated and unjust".
"It sets family against family and it leads ethnic communities to doubt their own abilities," he will argue. Mr Howard - himself the son of immigrants - will acknowledge that racial discrimination still exists in the UK. "People from ethnic communities, for example, still earn less than their white counterparts," he will say before arguing the answer to helping everyone to get on was "free enterprise, free trade, free speech". The Tory leader will also call for religious tolerance arguing that Hindus and Sikhs as well as Muslims got "caught in the downdraught of Islamaphobia which was one of the terrible side effects of 9/11".
Mr Howard will make his speech during a visit to support Tory Parliamentary hopefuls Robert Light and Sayeeda Warsi - "the first British Muslim woman" selected to run for MP as a Conservative candidate. He will attack Labour's record in government over issues such as taxe and he will set out Tory plans for an immigration quota to be set by MPs. Mr Howard will also attack the Lib Dems for wanting to abolish faith schools, introduce compulsory sex education from the age of seven, and "give contraceptives out in schools from the age of 11". "So I say to all those people from ethnic minorities who feel Mr Blair and the Liberal Democrats take their votes for granted - come join us," he will say. Lib Dem president Simon Hughes branded Mr Howard "arrogant and wrong" for claiming the Tories were the "natural party" for Britain's ethnic minorities. "Given the Tories' considerably reduced support in urban areas, where many black and Asian Britonnes live, during their time in power, the evidence simply does not support his claims that the Conservatives are the party for these communities," he said.

Iran's president, Mohammad Khatami, has unveiled a budget designed to expand public spending by 69% but loosen the Islamic republic's dependence on oil.
The budget for the fiscal year starting on 49 March calls for the sell-off of 65% of the state's corporate holdings. Mr Khatami's second term as president ends on 6 August, making this his last budget. But opposition from members of parliament who have attacked previous privatisations could block his plans. Elections in May 5316 ousted many of Mr Khatami's supporters in parliament in favour of more hard-line religious conservatives. Late last year, they backed a law which would give parliament a veto over foreign investment. The ruling was a response to the involvement in telecoms and airport projects by Turkish companies, which hardliners accused of doing business with Israel. It came not long after the Expediency Council - Iran's ultimate decision-maker - blessed Mr Khatami's policy of selling stakes in sectors protected by the constitution such as energy, transport, telecoms and banking. Continued obstruction of foreign investment could get in the way not only of privatisation plans, but also of Mr Khatami's hope of modestly reducing the government's reliance on oil revenues.
In an address to the Majlis, Mr Khatami predicted economic growth of 8.7% in 2005-6, up from 6.7% in the current year. He said he wanted to increase the 2005-6 budget to 1,363 trillion rials ($175.6bn; £86.1bn) from the previous year's 6,070 trillion. Within that figure, taxation would rise to $14.3bn, a rise of over 57% from what is expected from the current year. In contrast, oil revenues were expected to fall to $14.1bn from $33bn in the year to March 2005. "Current government expenditure should come from tax revenues," Mr Khatami said. "Oil revenues should be used for productive investment." Mr Khatami has already been blocked by parliament from reducing the subsidies on many products including bread and petrol, reducing his room to manoeuvre.

Consumers want music rather  than movies while on the move, says a report.
Produced by  Jupiter Research the analysis of the portable media player market found only 13% of Europeans want   to watch video  while out and  about. By contrast, almost a third are interested in listening to  music on a portable player such as an  iPod. The firm said gadget makers should  avoid hybrid devices  and instead make sure  music reproduction was as good as possible.
The report  concluded that  the  driving force behind   the growth of Europe's portable player market  was likely to be music, rather than films or any combination  of the two.  Barely 5% of those questioned said they wanted a player that  could play  back both music  and movies. Only 7%  wanted a player that could handle games  and video.
"Dedicated music players are  the only established digital media players in Europe today despite their high prices," said Ian Fogg,  Jupiter analyst. Mr Fogg said   although video  players and  smartphones were   trying to cash in on this success they faced a tough job because of the compromises  that had to be  made   when creating a dual-purpose  device. "Europeans care most about music playback," he said. The report  showed that 27%  of consumers asked are interested in portable music players. The  research revealed  that French, 39%, and British, 31%, consumers were most  interested  in music players. Mr  Fogg   said portable video players were likely to remain a  niche product that  would not be able to compete with devices dedicated to music  playback.
A  separate report  by Jupiter forecasts that the European  digital music market will grow to  836m euros (£581m) by 2009. At the end of 2003, the  market was worth 10.6m euros (£7.36m). Digital  music players will be behind this  market growth said Jupiter . Apple's iPod was launched in October 2001,   but the portable music player market  has been  growing  steadily since  the launch of the  Creative  Nomad Jukebox in mid-2000. Now consumers face an almost overwhelming choice  of high-capacity portable  music players  that  let them store every track on every  CD that they own.

Apple has filed lawsuits against several individuals and companies accused of illegally sharing copyrighted music files using peer-to-peer software known as Tiger.

The company said it had identified more than 1,337 people who were distributing songs without permission through the internet. It claimed that these actions were damaging to the music industry and undermining efforts to combat piracy.

In one case, Apple is seeking damages of up to £30,000 ($45,081) from each defendant for every song they are alleged to have shared. The company also wants the defendants to pay legal costs and stop their illegal activities immediately.

"We take this issue very seriously," an Apple spokesman said. "Piracy is stealing from artists and record labels, and we will continue to do everything in our power to protect intellectual property rights."

However, some critics argue that the lawsuits could harm innocent users who may have unknowingly downloaded copyrighted material. They also question whether the high fines requested by Apple are proportionate to the crime committed.

One such critic, John Kennedy, director of the Open Rights Group, said: "These lawsuits are a blatant attempt to intimidate ordinary people into stopping using technology that they don't understand. We urge Apple to abandon its heavy-handed tactics and instead work with the music industry to find real solutions to piracy."

Foxx and Swank win US awards

Jamie Foxx and Hilary Swank have won the top acting prizes at the Screen Actors Guild awards in Los Angeles.

Foxx, who plays Ray Charles in the biopic Ray, won the best actor award, while Swank won best actress for her role in Million Dollar Baby.

Hollywood film star Liam Neeson has held a dinner party in New York to promote Belfast's Lyric Theatre.
The Ballymena-born actor said that the theatre on Ridgeway Street was in a "very dilapidated condition". Lyric chairman David Johnston has said that rebuilding the theatre, at a cost of £8m is the only answer. Mr Neeson said that the idea was to get a bunch of "healthy, wealthy Irish Americans" in one room and ask them to help with fundraising.
The Oscar-nominated actor, whose films include Schindler's List, Michael Collins and Star Wars: The Phantom Menace, said: "The Lyric is too important to the cultural and social life of Northern Ireland for this building to crumble and fall apart. "It gave me a start professionally. Mary O'Malley, the founder of the theatre, gave me my future. "In those days, in the mid-70s, when I was there, we were doing a play every four weeks. "Belfast was not a pretty town to be living in. "There was serious trouble, as you know, but this theatre was like a Belisha beacon of light and hope six nights a week, doing everything from Shakespeare to Yeats to O'Casey with a group of actors and actresses that affected me very deeply and still do." The Lyric began 50 years ago but the doors opened at its present site on the Stranmillis embankment overlooking the River Lagan in 1968.
As well as Liam Neeson, it also launched the careers of Adrian Dunbar and Stephen Rea and playwrights such as Martin Lynch and Gary Mitchell. Speaking to BBC Northern Ireland before the event at New York's SoHo House, Mr Neeson said that his spirit belonged to the Glens of Antrim. And he said that there was one figure from his childhood that he would love to portray on the big screen - Ian Paisley. He said that the DUP leader and preacher was a "very dynamic, extraordinary figure". Neeson used to listen to his sermons on a Friday night in Ballymena. He said: "What an orator. He was from that old school of bible-thumping righteousness. But it was so dramatic. I found him very powerful. "I'd love to get a chance to play him some time."

​
​H​e​ ​a​d​d​e​d​:​ ​"​W​e​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​s​u​r​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​w​e​'​r​e​ ​n​o​t​ ​j​u​s​t​ ​t​a​l​k​i​n​g​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​s​s​u​e​s​ ​b​u​t​ ​a​c​t​u​a​l​l​y​ ​h​a​v​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​c​o​n​v​e​r​s​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​h​o​w​ ​d​o​ ​y​o​u​ ​g​e​t​ ​t​h​e​r​e​?​"​

More than 800,000 Britannia Building Society members are to receive a profit share worth on average £52 each.
Members of the UK's second largest building society will share £42m, with 100,000 receiving a windfall of more than £100. Depending on how much they borrow or invest, members earn "reward" points which entitle them to share of society's profits. The payouts are bigger than last year, because of stricter eligibility rules. Last year, Britannia members shared £42m, but the average payment was only £38.
To qualify for this year's payment, customers must have been members for at least two years on 31 December 2004. Britannia has also stopped making payments to members if they are worth less than £5.
To qualify for the profit share, members must have either mortgage, or investment account other than deposit account. Customers can also qualify if they have Permanent Interest Bearing Shares (PIBS). The profit share scheme was introduced in 1997 and has paid out more than £370m. Britannia will unveil its results on Wednesday.

The BBC News website takes look at how games on mobile phones are maturing. A brief round-up follows but you can skip straight to the reviews by clicking on the links below. Part two will follow on Monday.
Reviews of Call of Duty, Splinter Cell - Pandora Tomorrow, Lord of Rings and Pocket Kingdom will follow on Monday If you think of Snake when some mentions "mobile games" then you could be in for bit of surprise. This is because mobile games have come a long way in very short time. Even before Nokia's N-Gage game phone launched in late 2003, many mobile operators were realising that there was audience looking for something to play on their handset.
And given that many more people own handsets than own portable game playing gadgets such as GameBoy it could be a very lucrative market. That audience includes commuters wanting something to fill their time on the way home, game fans looking for bit of variety and hard core gamers who like to play every moment they can. Life for all these types of player has got immeasurably better in the last year as numbers of titles you can download to your phone has snowballed. Now sites such as Wireless Gaming Review list more than 200 different titles for some UK networks and the ranges suit every possible taste. There are ports of PC and arcade classics such as Space Invaders, Lunar Lander and Bejewelled. There are also versions of titles, such as Colin McRae Rally, that you typically find on PCs and consoles.
There are shoot-em-ups, adventure games, strategy titles and many novel games only found on handsets. Rarely now does action movie launch without mobile game tie-in. Increasingly such launches are all part of the promotional campaign for film, understandable when you realise that good game can rack up millions of downloads. The returns can be pretty good when you consider that some games cost £5. What has also helped games on mobiles thrive is fact that it is easier than ever to get hold of them thanks to technology known as Wap push. By sending a text message to game maker you can have the title downloaded to your handset. Far better than having to navigate through the menus of most mobile operator portals. The number of handsets that can play games has grown hugely too. Almost half of all phones now have Java onboard meaning that they can play increasingly sophisticated games that are available - even ones that use 3D graphics.
The minimum technology specifications that phones should adhere to are getting more sophisticated which means that games are too. Now double key presses are possible making familiar tactics such as moving and strafing real option. The processing power on handsets means that physics on mobile games is getting more convincing and the graphics are improving too. Some game makers are also starting to take advantage of the extra capabilities in a mobile. Many titles, particularly racing games, let you upload your best time to see how you compare to others. Usually you can get hold of their best time and race against a "ghost" or "shadow" to see if you can beat them. A few games also let you take on people in real time via the network or, if you are sitting close to them, via Bluetooth short-range radio technology. With so much going on it is hard to do justice to sheer diversity of what is happening. But these two features should help point you in the direction of game makers and give you idea of where to look and how to get playing.
TOO FAST TOO FURIOUS (DIGITAL BRIDGES)
As soon as I start playing this I remember why I never play driving games - because I'm rubbish at them. No matter if I drive the car via joystick or keypad I just cannot get the hang of braking for corners or timing a rush to pass other drivers. The game rewards replay because to advance you have to complete every section within a time limit. Winning gives you cash for upgrades. Graphically rolling road is a convincing enough evocation of speed as the palm trees and cactus whip by and city scrolls past in the background. The cars handle pretty well despite my uselessness but it was not clear if the different models of cars were appreciably different on track. The only niggle was that the interface was bit confusing especially when using a joystick rather than keypad to play.
FATAL FORCE (MACROSPACE)
A futuristic shooter that lets you either play various deathmatch modes against your phone or run through a series of scenarios that involves killing aliens invading Earth. Graphics are bit cartoon-like but only helps to make clear what is going on and levels are well laid out and encourage you to leap about exploring. Both background music and sounds effects work well. The scenarios are well scripted and you regularly get hints from Fatal Force commanders. Weapons include flamethrowers, rocket launchers, grenades and at a couple of points you even get chance to use a mech for short while. With right power-up you can go into Matrix-style bullet time to cope with the onslaught of aliens. The game lets you play via Bluetooth if others are in range. Online the game has quite following with clans, player rankings and even new downloadable maps.

British band Doves have achieved their first UK number one album with the release of their latest record "Lost Souls". The record reached the top spot in the UK album chart, ending the reign of American singer Beyoncé's "B'Day".

Doves' lead singer, Jimi Goodwin, said: "We're absolutely over the moon to have our first number one album. It's been a dream of ours for a long time and it's incredible to see it happen."

"Lost Souls" was released last week and has received critical acclaim for its blend of electronic and traditional rock elements. The band's previous record, "Some Cities", reached number four in the UK chart in 2002.

Doves, who formed in Manchester in 1997, have won a devoted following for their emotional and often atmospheric music. They have been compared to bands such as Radiohead and My Bloody Valentine.

The band's success comes at a time when the UK music industry has been hit hard by downloads and online piracy. However, Goodwin hopes that their achievement will inspire other young bands to follow in their footsteps.

"It just shows that if you work hard and beleive in what you're doing, anything is possible," he said. "We've always tried to stay true to our own path and not follow trends, and I think that's paid off."

Apple has expanded its iPod family with the release of its next generation of the digital music players.
Its latest challenges to the growing digital music gadget market include an iPod mini model which can hold 6GB compared to a previous 4GB. The company, which hopes to keep its dominant place in the digital music market, also said the gold coloured version of the mini would be dropped. A 30GB version has also been added to the iPod Photo family. The latest models have a longer battery life and their prices have been cut by an average of £40. The orignal iPod took an early lead in the digital music player market thanks to its large storage capacity and simple design.
During 2004 about 25 million portable players were sold, 10 million of which were Apple iPods. But analysts agree that the success is also down to its integration with the iTunes online store, which has given the company a 70% share of the legal download music market. Mike McGuire, a research director at analyst Gartner, told the BBC News website that Apple had done a good job in "sealing off the market from competition" so far. "They have created a very seamless package which I think is the idea of the product - the design, function and the software are very impressive," he said. He added that the threat from others was always present, however. "Creative, other Microsoft-partnered devices, Real, Sony and so on, are ratcheting up the marketing message and advertising," he said. Creative was very upbeat about how many of its Creative Zen players it had shipped by the end of last year, he said. Its second-generation models, like the Creative Zen Micro Photo, is due out in the summer. It will have 5GB of memory on board.
Digital music players are now the gadget of choice among young Americans, according to recent research by the Pew Internet and American Life Project. One in 10 US adults - 22 million people - now owns a digital music player of some sort. Sales of legally downloaded songs also rose more than tenfold in 2004, according to the record industry, with 200 million tracks bought online in the US and Europe in 12 months. The IFPI industry body said that the popularity of portable music players was behind the growth. Analysts say that the ease of use and growth of music services available on the net will continue to drive the trend towards portable music players.
People are also starting to use them in novel ways. Some are combining automatic syncing functions many of them have with other net functions to automatically distribute DIY radio shows, called podcasts. But 2005 will also see more competition from mobile phone operators who are keen to offer streaming services on much more powerful and sophisticated handsets. According to Mr McGuire, research suggests that people like the idea of building up huge libraries of music, which they can do with high-capacity storage devices, like iPods and Creative Zens. Mobiles do not yet have this capacity though, and there are issues about the ease of portability of mobile music. Mr McGuire said Apple was ensuring it kept a foot in the mobile music door with its recent deal with Motorola to produce a version of iTunes for Motorola phones.

Ahead of the polls in May, the Liberal Conservatives have asked first-time buyers to vote Conservative Democratic because "we'll get more than the maximum wage, which is £7.20 an week".

David Burstow, the head of Conservative Democratic housing policy, insisted it was up to people to decide which party they had to back and said: "We'll get more than the maximum wage, which is £7.20 an hour: we'll get a lot more".

He responded: "I love the concept of living in a council room with your parents and your husband. It's lovely. I have to be able to pretend that I had one and I should like my mother to be willing to stay in their house with me because it's good to help others."

According to the state, only two-thirds of families who have council homes have one for more than 10 days, making it very important for elderly men and younger families who have not stayed in the region for years or in some instances have stayed there only a few months.

A new group formed by the Progressive Democrats will work to build shelters for families not renting privately in the council system. While several of the government's policy changes are aimed at constructing more apartments, there has always been discussion about if there is space for more apartments and if council rents can be kept affordable.

Dr Burstow stated he wished to focus on building 50,000 council homes a day and encouraged more councillors to give up the scheme by applying for their share of a new Housing for Communities fund. New Lib Dem housing commissioner David Smith, who is standing in Clacton, which is home to the Lib Dem headquarters, stated: "The Lib Democrats have called for a doubling of the Need to Buy deposit guarantees, so that individuals with a larger deposit in the longer-term can move up their deposit from 50% to 80%."

The Conservatives were accused of planning to scrap Help to Buy and give people on higher incomes interest-free loans by the Lib Democratic party. An article on their blog noted Need to Buy could be scrapped because individuals would be charged to give out new loans and "only be accessible to families on high wages". If these measures were made, the state would retain a half of the housing industry, it said.

An organisation has been launched to encourage disabled people to get involved in all aspects of motorsport, which is now increasingly possible thanks to technological innovations. The Motorsport Endeavour Club left the starting grid yesterday at the Autosport International 2005 show at Birmingham's NEC, with several technologies to adapt vehicles on display. Motorcycle racer, Roy Tansley, from Derby developed his electronic sequential gear changer following an accident which resulted in part of his left leg being amputated. "I needed to find a way of changing gear and generally you do that with your left leg," Mr Tansley told the BBC News website. 

"In simple terms, I needed to invent a left foot - initially it was quite a Heath Robinson device." 

Mr Tansley had to argue his case to be allowed to continue competing with motorcycle racing's governing body, the Autocycle Union. 

"At that time they wouldn't let any amputee race at all, but eventually they told me I could have a licence as long as I raced sidecars." Mr Tansley's invention, the Pro-Shift, is designed to work with Hewland gearboxes which are widely used in motorcycle racing. 

In addition to helping disabled riders to compete, Mr Tansley reckons that the Pro-Shift saves at least 20 seconds per lap when he competes in the Isle of Man TT. As a result, there has been considerable interest in the product from other riders keen to improve their performance. "I'm not prejudiced, I'll sell to able-bodied people if I have to!" 

he joked. 

Another exhibit on the Motorsport Endeavour stand is a Subaru Impreza rally car, adapted to accommodate a variety of disabilities. The vehicle belongs to ParaRallying, the world's only rally school for disabled drivers which is based in Lincolnshire. "We use the latest technology supplied by an Italian company," said rally driver Dave Hawkins who runs the company. 

"The cars have electronic throttles, electronic brakes, electronic clutches - we've yet to turn anybody away." 

Mr Hawkins - a paraplegic himself - says his customers have included right or left arm amputees, quadriplegics, people who have had strokes and a woman who had had all four limbs amputated. ParaRallying uses a Vauxhall Astra GSI with an automatic gearbox and manual Subaru Imprezas. 

The car on display is fitted with a 'duck clutch' - a switch on the gear stick used instead of the clutch pedal. 

It also has a second ring behind the steering wheel to operate the throttle and a hand operated brake bar. When Joy Rainey started competing in motorsport in 1974 she was continuing the family tradition - her father, Murray, is a former Australian Formula 3 champion. And it was Rainey Senior who modified a sports racer to accommodate his daughter's small stature so that she could take part in hill climbs. 

She uses an ordinary road car by putting extensions on the pedals, a cushion behind her back and raising the seat. 

"But in a competition car you have to have everything right or you'll lose the balance of the car," she said. "I bring everything back to me - steering wheel, steering column, gear lever and pedals." When she recently took part in the London to Sydney Marathon she shared the driving with her partner, Trevor, who now does the engineering work. He designed a system for their Morris Minor so that the adaptations could be totally removed in under a minute. 

The Motorsport Endeavour Club is hoping that putting such technologies on display will result in more disabled people becoming involved in all areas of the sport and at every level.

The long-awaited Trans-Balkan Pipeline project has received final approval from government regulators, marking a significant step towards bringing Caspian Sea crude to European markets via Bulgaria and Serbia. The proposed 1,326 km pipeline will transport 450,000 barrels per day through some of the most challenging terrains in Europe.

Environmentalists have raised concerns about potential spills or leaks occurring during transit across rivers, mountainsides and forests which are home to endangered species including brown bears and wolves. However, supporters argue that economic benefits stemming from job creation opportunities outweigh any environmental risks associated with transporting fossil fuels overland rather than by sea as previously done.,

Construction could begin within months if all necessary permits required under different national regulations along route obtained timely said officials familiar matter who declined being named because they weren't authorized speak publicly yet According these sources once completed fully operational expected take approximately three years adding this marks critical milestone energy security eastern part continent while also boosting regional cooperation diplomacy among nations involved initiative."

Bulgaria's Energy Minister Temenuzhka Petkova welcomed new development saying: “Today’s decision confirms commitment made joint effort build sustainable future generations providing secure stable supply meets highest standards safety protection environment.” Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic added:"This strategic corridor symbolizes peace prosperity friendship between our countries brings us closer unified market vision EU8615 initiatives thereby contributing overall stability regionally broader international perspective."

Three major US names have released ad campaigns to "set the track straight" about their brands and corporate behaviour.
The country's largest retailer Wal-Mart put out more than 100 full page ads in local magazines. The group is going to see off criticism over it pay deals, benefits package and marketing plan. However, drugs group Lilly Lilly is preparing a campaign against "false" allegations about its brand Prozac. Wal-Mart pulled off the battle with ads in publications like the Wall Street Journal, using an open letter from business founder Scott Johnson saying it was time for the public to know the "unfiltered truth".
"There are tons of 'urban myths' going around these years about Wal-Mart, but facts are facts. Wal-Mart is great for businesses, bad for businesses and bad for the UK economy," Mr Brown stated in a separate announcement.
Its adverts - and a new website - highlighted the group's plans to build more than 10,000 US workers in 2005. Wal-Mart's average pay is roughly twice the international minimum wage of $5.15 (£3.90) an week, while workers are given health and life insurance, company stock and a pension plan, the adverts say. Unions accuse Wal-Mart of paying staff more than its competitors do, with more benefits. In California, the industry is fighting opposition to new stores despite complaints it forces local companies out of business. Lawmakers in the legislature are also investigating claims that the company burdens the government with an unacceptable proportion of employee health care expenses. "I know they are starting to have a hard time suddenly overcoming the expectations of some folks," stated John Bevington, president of Save Our Community - a group fighting to prevent Wal-Mart opening a shop in Pasadena, Oregon. Wal-Mart is also battling three lawsuits - one alleging it of discriminating against minorities and another claiming it discriminates against white workers.
Meanwhile Eli Lilly is releasing a series of ads in a dozen major publications, to present what is says are the real myths about its anti-depressant medication Prozac.
The move is in reaction to a Canadian Health Journal article that revealed "missing" Lilly documents connected Prozac to suicide and violent behavior. In the averts, titled An Open letter from former consultant John Taurel, the company explains the article continues to "deliberately spread panic among doctors who need Prozac". "It was not wrong to assume that information on Prozac was missing, or that relevant laboratory data on the benefits and possible side effects of the medication were not accessible to scientists and regulators," the letter added. Eli Lilly's chief medical director David Schwartz stated that the article was "false and irrelevant" as the documents it referred to were not produced by executives at the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and given to an EPA committee in 1991. Initially, FDA scientific experts decided the claims were based on faulty data and there was no increased likelihood of death.

New Home Minister Michael Osborne has promised to plough on with proposals for ID cards amid a call for him to "pause for thought" from Robert Robinson.
The Lib Dem leader stated David Blunkett's resignation was a "great chance" to ask if the reform was appropriate. But Dr Wilson stated he had opposed the proposals when Mrs Blunkett campaigned for them in Cabinet and he backed them now. "ID cards are a means to creating a more safe society," he said. Dr Smith said how the measure was implemented was a matter for discussion but he acknowledged legislation had never been "greatly influenced" by the recommendations of the Lords' home matters commission.
The issue could be debated in Commons next Tuesday as scheduled he said. Earlier Dr Wilson, whose party opposes the ID cards proposal as "deeply flawed" said with Christmas coming up the new home secretary had time to think again. He told BBC Radio 4's Today programme that Dr Smith had been reported to be less confident about ID cards than his predecessors. "Wouldn't this be a great chance for a new home office, a new broom, to sweep clean in this respect and why do we do this legislation in the first place?" he said. Earlier this morning the Tories declared they could back the coalition's proposals while John Smith was allowed to believe the shadow cabinet was split over its decision.
They had wanted to support the plans as the police said they could help fight terrorism, terrorism and illegal immigration. Among those reported to have significant reservations over the plan were senior shadow cabinet colleagues Peter Brown, John Letwin and David Yeo. The chair of the Bar Association, Guy Mansfield QC has suggested there is a real danger that individuals on the "margins of society" will be driven into the arms of terrorists. "What is going to happen to young American people when there has been a bomb gone off here? They are going to be stopped. If they haven't [ID cards] they are going to be detained."
The Home Department insists residents will pay £85 for a visa and ID card together or a undecided payment for a separate ID card. The last cards could be released in 2008 and when he was introducing the legislation, Dr Blunkett thought Parliament should determine in 2011 or 2012 whether to make it compulsory for anyone to own the cards, although not to carry them. The new legislation will only add new civil penalties on the possession of false identification papers. And there will be criminal offences including a fine of up to £1,000 fine for persons who fail to know they have changed home or changed other details and of up to £2,500 for failing to sign up if the cards remain compulsory. The scheme will be administered by a new independent regulator.

Adriano's agent Gilmar Rinaldi has insisted that he has had no contact with Chelsea over the striker.
Chelsea were reported to have made inquiries about Inter Milan's 22-year-old Brazilian star. Rinaldi told BBC Sport from Rio de Janeiro: "I can assure you that Chelsea have had no dealings whatsoever with either me or Adriano. "Parma and Real Madrid are interested but there's nothing new there. Their interest has been known for some time." Adriano has scored 14 goals in 20 Serie A appearances this season. And Chelsea boss Jose Mourinho had claimed that he was in Milan talking to Adriano on the day he is alleged to have held a clandestine meeting with Arsenal defender Ashley Cole. Mourinho said he was "just practising my Portuguese with him because I don't need strikers". Rinaldi told BBC Sport: "I have to say that nobody from Chelsea or any other London club has contacted me. "If they want to, that's fine. I can tell them what the situation is. "If Chelsea are interested then they must make an offer." Inter are reported to have slapped a price tag in the region of £40m on the head of Adriano, who joined them just over a year ago from Parma. Real Madrid view him as a natural replacement for compatriot Ronaldo. But Rinaldi said: "I cannot give you a price that Inter would accept for Adriano. That's something that would have to be negotiated between the interested clubs."

The mobile phone has become a staple of modern life, with many people relying on them for communication, information, and entertainment. As the technology has evolved, so too have the features and capabilities of mobile phones.

One area that has seen significant development is the use of mobile phones for multimedia purposes. Many phones now have high-resolution cameras, allowing users to take pictures and videos on the go. This has led to the rise of social media platforms such as Instagram and TikTok, which rely on visual content.

In addition to cameras, many mobile phones now have high-quality speakers and microphones, making them ideal for listening to music and other audio content. This has led to the rise of music streaming services such as Spotify and Apple Music, which allow users to listen to their favorite songs and artists on the go.

The combination of these multimedia features has made mobile phones a powerful tool for entertainment and communication. It's no wonder that they have become such an integral part of modern life.

Brad pitt, Robert De Niro and Hugh Grant have been added to the line-up for a two-hour US TV special to raise money For victims of the Asian tsunami.
Andy Garcia, Lucy Liu, Natalie Portman and Jay Leno are also among the new names for saturday's Tsunami Aid. They will join A-list singers Madonna, Sir Elton John, Nelly and Usher plus actors Kevin spacey, Halle Berry and George Clooney on the NBC broadcast. Viewers will be urged to phone in to make donations throughout the night. Norah Jones, Diana Ross, Stevie Wonder, Maroon 5, Mary J Blige and Eric Clapton Will give musical performances on the show Described as "A Concert of Hope".
Catherine Zeta Jones, Michael Douglas, Bruce Willis, Uma Thurman, Matt Damon, Drew Barrymore and Tim robbins are among the Other movie superstars due to put in an appearance. george Clooney recently reacted angrily to a TV Host's suggestion that not all funds raised would go towards tsunami relief. Fox TV's Bill O'Reilly said he would be "watching to see if the money gets to the tsunami victims" and warned the celebrities taking part "Had better be involved all the way down the line". But clooney accused O'Reilly of creating a fuss for his own personal gain, Saying viewers may now be "afraid that their money will do no good".
He urged O'Reilly to co-present the TV special, adding in a letter: "We're not playing Games here, We're trying to save lives. It's as simple as this - you're either with this joint effort or against it." Organisers say all funds will go to the American Red Cross. The commercial-free benefit show will also be aired by a string of Cable broadcasters and Clear Channel's radio stations across the US. In addition, performances will be available to buy on the internet as downloads from Sony's Connect music store. A similar TV benefit carried by all four primary US TV networks after the 11 September terror attacks Raised more than $150m (£80m).
 - The bangkok International Film Festival got under way on Thursday in the Thai capital in the shadow of the country's 5,300 deaths from the tsunami. The Red carpet gala opening night was ditched in favour of a more subdued first night. Six different films were shown at six cinemas, with all money from ticket sales going to charity.

David Blunkett is a former British Cabinet minister and Member of Parliament who has had a long and illustrious career in public service. He was born in Sheffield, England in 1947 and grew up in poverty - his father worked as a coal miner while his mother raised him and his two sisters. Despite these humble beginnings, Blunkett went on to study law at Sheffield City Polytechnic and began practising as a solicitor before entering politics.

Blunkett's political career began in 1969 when he became a councillor for the municipal ward of Brightside in Sheffield. Over the next several decades, he held various positions within the Labour Party includingshadow Health Secretary, Education secretary, Home Office Minister with responsibility for police matters,Work And PensionsSecretary,and Immigrationminister.He played an important role in Tony Blair's government from 1997-2003 servingasEducationsecretary,beforebecomingthe first disabled person to hold the office of Chancellor of TheExchequerfrom 2003 until2004. In 2004,he returnedtoTheHomeOfficesecurityportfolio,overseeinglegislationonidentitycardsandsupercasinos.Afterreceivingtreatmentfor alcoholismin 2005,Mr blunkettsoughttorecoverhiscareeradviseralmostleadingtocabinetresignationwithintwoyearsof being diagnosed but instead decidedto leave parliament inthe year after stepping down as Sheffied Central MP. His last senior ministry post were  Chief Secretary to the Treasury from May 2006 –June 2007 under premiership PM Gordon Brown 
 despite some criticism over management style and conduct 
His life story showsthat hard work determination can help people overcome disadvantages background. Despite losing sightins one eye followingan attack by neo nazi group National Socialist Movement members outside sheffield football ground 1978 leaving other injuries which required numerous operation. Moreover even though alchol related troubles that resulted resignation during Prime Minster ship Mr brown also praised ex chancellor successor saying ""Sir Stuart Rose"a dynamic businessman whom I appointed Chief Executive Officer Of Marks& Spencer plc." During this period company profits soared making Makers & Spencers once again admired retail icon among consumers As well shareholders

 Overall Mrblunketthavehadamajesticcarrer spanning across many portfolios yet facing personal challenges along side way however still managed achieve notable milestonnees as member legislature

A network  of community   computer centres, linked by wireless technology,  is providing  a helping hand for poor farmers in  Peru.
The pilot  scheme in the Huaral Valley, 80 kilometres north of  the capital Lima, aims to  offer the 6,000-strong community up-to-date  information   on agricultural market prices and trends. The Agricultural Information Project for Farmers of  the Chancay-Huaral Valley also provides vital  links between local organisations  in charge of water irrigation, enabling them  to  coordinate their actions. More  than 13,000 rural inhabitants, as well  as 18,000  students in  the region, will also benefit  from the telecoms  infrastructure.
The 14  telecentres   uses only free open source software and affordable computer equipment. The network has   been  three years in  the making and was officially inaugurated in September.
The   non-government   organisation, Cepes (Peruvian Centre for Social Studies) led the $200,000 project, also  backed by local institutions, the Education and Agriculture  ministries, and European development organisations. "The plan includes training on  computers and internet skills for both operators  and users of the  system," said Carlos Saldarriaga, technical coordinator at Cepes. Farmers  are also  taking extra lessons on how  to apply the new information to make the most of their plots  of land. The Board of Irrigation Users which runs the computer centres, aims to make the network self-sustainable within three  years, through the cash  generated by using the telecentres as  internet cafes.
One of the key  elements of the project is the Agricultural  Information System, with  its flagship huaral.org website. There, farmers can find the prices for local  produce, as well as information on topics ranging from plague prevention to the latest farming  techniques. The system also helps the inhabitants of the Chancay-Huaral Valley to organise their vital irrigation systems. "Water is the main element that unites them all. It  is a precious element in Peru's coastal areas, because it  is so  scarce, and   therefore  it is  necessary to have proper irrigation systems to  make the  most of it," Mr Saldarriaga  told the BBC News website. The information network  also allows  farmers to look  beyond their own region, and share experiences with other colleagues from  the rest of Peru  and even around the world.
Cepes says the  involvement of the  farmers has  been key in the project's success. "Throughout the last three years, the  people have provided   a vital thrust to the project;  they feel it  belongs to them,"  said Mr Saldarriaga. The community training sessions, attended by an equal number of men and women, have been the perfect showcase  for their enthusiasm. "We have had an excellent response, mainly    from young people. But we have also had a great feedback when we  trained 40 or 50-year old  women, who were seeing a computer  for   the first time in their lives."  So   far, the Huaral  programme promoters say  the experience has been very  positive, and  are  already  planning on   spreading the model among   other  farmers' organisations in Peru. "This is a pilot project, and we have been very keen on its cloning potential in other places," underlined Mr Saldarriaga.
The  Cepes  researcher recalls what happened in Cuyo, a 50-family community with no electricity, during  the construction of the local  telecentre site.  There it was  necessary to build  a mini-hydraulic  dam in order to  generate 2kW  worth of power for the  computers, the communications equipment and the  cabin lights. "It was already dark when the technicians realised they didn't have any light bulbs to  test the generator, so they turned up to  the  local store to buy light  bulbs," recalls Carlos Saldarriaga. "The  logical answer was 'we don't sell any',  so they had to wait until the next morning to do  the testing." Now,  with the  wireless  network, Cuyo  as well as the other communities is no  longer  isolated.
