Shares of Skis Rossignol, the world's largest ski-maker, have jumped as much as 15% on speculation that it will be bought by US surfwear firm Quiksilver.
The owners of Rossignol, the Boix-Vives family, are said to be considering an offer from Quiksilver. Analysts believe other sporting goods companies may now take a closer look at Rossignol, prompting an auction and pushing the sale price higher. Nike and K2 have previously been mentioned as possible suitors.
Rossignol shares touched 17.70 euros, before falling back to trade 7.8% higher at 16.60 euros. European sporting goods companies have seen foreign revenues squeezed by a slump in the value of the US dollar, making a takeover more attractive, analysts said. Companies such as Quiksilver would be able to cut costs by selling Rossignol skis through their shops, they added. The Boix-Vives family is thought to have spent the past couple of years sounding out possible suitors for Rossignol, which also makes golf equipment, snowboards and sports clothing.

Adriano's agent Gilmar Rinaldi has insisted that he has had no contact with Chelsea over the striker.
Chelsea were reported to have made inquiries about Inter Milan's 22-year-old Brazilian star. Rinaldi told BBC Sport from Rio de Janeiro: "I can assure you that Chelsea have had no dealings whatsoever with either me or Adriano. "Parma and Real Madrid are interested but there's nothing new there. Their interest has been known for some time." Adriano has scored 14 goals in 20 Serie A appearances this season. And Chelsea boss Jose Mourinho had claimed that he was in Milan talking to Adriano on the day he is alleged to have held a clandestine meeting with Arsenal defender Ashley Cole. Mourinho said he was "just practising my Portuguese with him because I don't need strikers". Rinaldi told BBC Sport: "I have to say that nobody from Chelsea or any other London club has contacted me. "If they want to, that's fine. I can tell them what situation is. "If Chelsea are interested then they must make offer." Inter are reported to have slapped a price tag in region of £40m on the head of Adriano, who joined them just over year ago from Parma. Real Madrid view him as natural replacement for compatriot Ronaldo. But Rinaldi said: "I cannot give you a price that Inter would accept for Adriano. That's something that would have to be negotiated between interested clubs."

Making games for future Consoles will require more graphic artists and more money, an industry conference has been told.
Sony, Microsoft and nintendo will debut their new consoles at the annual E3 games Expo in Los Angeles in May. These so-called "next generation" machines will be faster than current consoles, and capable of displaying much Higher-quality visuals. For gamers, this should make for better, more immersive games. In a pre-recorded video slot during Microsoft's Keynote address at the Game Developers Conference, held last week in San Francisco, famed director James Cameron revealed he is making a game in tandem with his next film - believed to be Battle Angel Alita. The game's visual Quality would be "like a lucid dream," said Mr Cameron. But numerous speakers warned that creating such Graphics will require more artists, And so next generation console games will Be much more expensive to Develop. The first new Console, Microsoft's Xbox 2, is not expected to reach the shops until the end of 2005. Games typically take at least 18 months to create, however, so developers are grappling with the hardware today.
According to robert Walsh, head of Brisbane-based game developer krome Studios, next generation games will cost between $10-25m to make, With teams averaging 80 staff in size taking two years to complete a title. Such sums mean it will be difficult For anyone to start a new game studio, said Mr Walsh. "If you're a start-up, I Doubt that a publisher is going to walk in and give you a cheque for $10m, however good you are," he said. Mr Walsh suggested that new studios Should make games for mobile phones and handheld consoles like the Sony pSP and the Nintendo DS, since they are cheaper and easier to create than console games.
One developer bucking the trend towards big art teams is Will Wright, the creator of the best-selling the Sims games. The founder of California's Maxis studio surprised the conference with a world exclusive preview of his Next game, Spore. Spore will allow players to experiment with the evolution of digital creatures. Starting with an amoeba-sized organism, the player will guide the physical development of their creature by selecting how its limbs, jaws and other body parts evolve.
Eventually the creature will become capable of establishing cities, trading and fighting, And even building space ships. Advanced players will visit the home planets of creatures created by other Spore players. These worlds will be automatically swapped across the Internet. Mr Wright said that enabling players to devise and share their creatures would make them care more about the Game. "I don't Want to put the player in the role of Luke Skywalker or Frodo Baggins - I want Them to be George lucas or Dr Seuss," explained Mr Wright. few games have hinted at the scope of Spore, but Mr Wright explained that he has nevertheless kept his development team small by hiring expert programmers. Instead of employing lots of artists to create 3D models of the digital creatures, Spore generates and displays the creatures according To rules devised by the programmers. "The thing I am coming away with [from the conference] is that next generation content is going to be really expensive, and creating it will drive the smaller players out of the market," said Mr Wright. "I'd like to offer an alternative to that."
New development tools will be another important aid in making next generation games, and dozens of companies demonstrated their latest products at the conference.
Oxford-based Natural Motion launched Endorphin v2.0, which enables artists to direct A 3D 'virtual actor'. The actor is realistically modelled according to The laws of physics. Endorphin simulates how the actor falls down stairs, for Instance, or crumples up after a gunshot. Artists can blend together these visual sequences, and include the Results in their games. The process is much quicker than having an artist animate each movement by hand, and so lessens the need for larger art teams. Another British company aiming to reduce the workload of artists is Manchester start-up Genemation. Its latest tool, genCrowd, enables artists to create unique, photo-realistic human faces for games involving lots of people. GenCrowd Works by blending together elements of an in-built supply of stock faces of Differing ages and ethnicities. The software can create up to 2,000 new heads an hour.
One area not yet Dominated by graphical blockbusters is mobile phone games. Even The latest phones are not as powerful as the consoles of a decade ago, so smaller teams of half a dozen people can still create complete games for the Devices.
The Game Developers Conference included a special two-day summit dedicated To creating mobile games. A niche attraction for a few dozen conference attendees when it began Five years ago, GDC's Mobile summit this year drew several hundred delegates. mobile games are a fast-growing Sector because newer phones have Better graphics and sound, and are thus more suitable for playing games. Furthermore, the adoption of mobile phones continues to spread across the world. It's predicted that by 2006 two billion people will Own a mobile phone. The growing importance of mobile gaming was reflected by a keynote given by John Batter, general manager at EA Mobile. EA Mobile is a division of Electronic Arts, the biggest games publisher. Until recently Electronic Arts had Been dismissive of games for phones. "The last Time you Checked, EA wasn't in this business," mr Batter Admitted. But he said EA now planned on dominating the market by releasing mobile versions of its most popular franchises. EA plans to release Up to 20 mobile games over the next 12 months. The first will be a version of its Need for Speed racing game, created by EA Mobile's 30-person development team. Mr Batter predicted that by 2006, mobile phones would be capable of running games of comparable quality to those On Sony's upcoming PSP handheld console.
Owain Bennallack is the editor of Develop magazine.

IBM is spending $100m (£52m) over the next three years beefing up its commitment to Linux software. 

The cash injection will be used to help its customers use Linux on every type of device from handheld computers and phones right up to powerful servers. IBM said the money will fund a variety of technical, research and marketing initiatives to boost Linux use. IBM said it had taken the step in response to greater customer demand for the open source software. 

In 2004 IBM said it had seen double digit growth in the number of customers using Linux to help staff work together more closely. The money will be used to help this push towards greater collaboration and will add Linux-based elements to IBM's Workplace software. Workplace is a suite of programs and tools that allow workers to get at core business applications no matter what device they use to connect to corporate networks. 

One of the main focuses of the initiative will be to make it easier to use Linux-based desktop computers and mobile devices with Workplace. Even before IBM announced this latest spending boost it was one of the biggest advocates of the open source way of working. In 2001 it put $300m into a three-year Linux program and has produced Linux versions of many of its programs. 

Linux and the open source software movement are based on the premise that developers should be free to tinker with the core components of software programs. 

They reason that more open scrutiny of software produces better programs and fuels innovation.

A US judge has set a preliminary trial date for the balco steroid distribution case which has rocked Athletics.
US district Court judge Susan Ilston rejected an attempt by the defence team to have the case dismissed at a pre-trial hearing in san Francisco. And she set a march date for the case of the four men accused of distributing illegal performance-enhancing drugs to elite athletes to be heard. A firm decision on whether the trial takes place is expected in January. The judge said that she may conduct hearings in January into whether federal agents illegally searched the Balco headquarters and wrongfully obtained Statements from the company's founder Victor Conte and its vice-president James Valente. The two men - along with personal trainer Greg Anderson and athletics coach Remy Korchemny - were All Indicted earlier this year but have pleaded their innocence. The outcome of those hearings could result in some or all of the charges being dismissed. Conte said that he would be telling his side of the story On an American TV show on Friday.
"The world deserves to know the truth about performance-enhancing drugs," he said. Balco (The Bay Area Laboratory Cooperative) is the company at the Centre Of the scandal. The company has been accused by the United States Anti-Doping Agency (USADA) of being the source of the banned steroid tHG and modafinil. The USADA claims that 10 athletes have received sanctions for testing positive for THG and modafinil. Former double world champion Kelli White and Olympic relay star Alvin Harrison have both been banned On The basis of materials discovered during the Balco investigation. Britain's former European 100m champion Dwain Chambers is currently serving a two-year ban after testing positive for THG in an out-of-competition test in 2003.

​T​h​e​ ​2​4​-​y​e​a​r​-​o​l​d​ ​E​n​g​l​a​n​d​ ​m​i​d​f​i​e​l​d​e​r​ ​i​s​ ​d​e​t​e​r​m​i​n​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​e​e​ ​o​u​t​ ​h​i​s​ ​c​o​n​t​r​a​c​t​,​ ​d​e​s​p​i​t​e​ ​r​e​p​o​r​t​e​d​ ​i​n​t​e​r​e​s​t​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​C​h​e​l​s​e​a​.​ ​H​e​ ​s​a​i​d​:​ ​"​I​'​m​ ​s​i​g​n​e​d​ ​h​e​r​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​t​w​o​ ​s​o​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​i​s​ ​n​o​ ​s​i​t​u​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​e​r​e​'​s​ ​a​ ​l​o​t​ ​o​f​ ​s​p​e​c​u​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​b​u​t​ ​t​h​a​t​'​s​ ​n​o​t​ ​d​o​w​n​ ​t​o​ ​m​e​.​ ​"​A​s​ ​c​l​u​b​ ​c​a​p​t​a​i​n​ ​a​l​l​ ​I​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​d​o​ ​i​s​ ​h​e​l​p​ ​u​s​ ​g​e​t​ ​b​a​c​k​ ​u​p​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​a​b​l​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​h​a​m​p​i​o​n​s​ ​L​e​a​g​u​e​ ​a​g​a​i​n​.​"​ ​G​e​r​r​a​r​d​ ​l​o​o​k​e​d​ ​s​e​t​ ​t​o​ ​m​o​v​e​ ​t​o​ ​C​h​e​l​s​e​a​ ​d​u​r​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​u​m​m​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​p​e​c​u​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​s​w​i​t​c​h​ ​t​o​ ​S​t​a​m​f​o​r​d​ ​B​r​i​d​g​e​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​g​a​i​n​ ​a​r​i​s​e​n​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​J​a​n​u​a​r​y​ ​t​r​a​n​s​f​e​r​ ​w​i​n​d​o​w​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​i​n​g​.​ ​H​e​ ​r​a​i​s​e​d​ ​d​o​u​b​t​s​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​h​i​s​ ​R​e​d​s​ ​f​u​t​u​r​e​ ​w​h​e​n​ ​h​e​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​n​t​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​l​u​b​ ​t​o​ ​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​t​i​t​l​e​ ​c​h​a​l​l​e​n​g​e​r​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​v​e​r​y​ ​n​e​a​r​ ​f​u​t​u​r​e​ ​o​r​ ​h​e​ ​m​i​g​h​t​ ​l​e​a​v​e​.​ ​L​i​v​e​r​p​o​o​l​ ​b​o​s​s​ ​R​a​f​a​e​l​ ​B​e​n​i​t​e​z​ ​h​a​s​ ​i​n​s​i​s​t​e​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​G​e​r​r​a​r​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​p​r​o​m​i​s​e​d​ ​h​i​m​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​n​t​s​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​a​y​ ​a​t​ ​A​n​f​i​e​l​d​.​ ​B​e​n​i​t​e​z​ ​s​a​i​d​:​ ​"​I​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​t​o​ ​S​t​e​v​e​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​I​ ​w​a​s​ ​s​u​r​e​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​n​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​a​y​ ​h​e​r​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​e​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​'​I​ ​d​o​'​.​
​"​I​ ​t​h​e​n​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​t​o​ ​h​i​m​ ​'​L​o​o​k​,​ ​i​f​ ​y​o​u​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​w​i​n​ ​t​i​t​l​e​s​,​ ​y​o​u​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​m​e​d​a​l​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​y​o​u​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​L​i​v​e​r​p​o​o​l​ ​t​o​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​t​h​i​n​g​s​ ​t​h​e​n​ ​I​ ​a​m​ ​g​o​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​y​o​u​r​ ​h​e​l​p​'​.​ ​"​I​ ​r​e​a​l​l​y​ ​t​h​i​n​k​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​n​t​s​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​a​y​ ​s​o​ ​n​o​w​ ​w​h​a​t​ ​w​e​ ​m​u​s​t​ ​d​o​ ​i​s​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​q​u​a​d​ ​s​t​r​o​n​g​e​r​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​m​.​"​ ​M​e​a​n​w​h​i​l​e​,​ ​G​e​r​r​a​r​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​u​r​g​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​n​f​i​e​l​d​ ​b​o​a​r​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​i​g​n​ ​R​e​a​l​ ​M​a​d​r​i​d​ ​s​t​r​i​k​e​r​ ​F​e​r​n​a​n​d​o​ ​M​o​r​i​e​n​t​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​J​a​n​u​a​r​y​ ​t​r​a​n​s​f​e​r​ ​w​i​n​d​o​w​.​ ​M​o​r​i​e​n​t​e​s​,​ ​2​8​,​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​l​r​e​a​d​y​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​e​d​ ​a​ ​w​i​l​l​i​n​g​n​e​s​s​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​m​e​ ​t​o​ ​E​n​g​l​a​n​d​.​ ​G​e​r​r​a​r​d​ ​a​d​d​e​d​:​ ​"​H​e​'​s​ ​a​ ​g​r​e​a​t​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​.​ ​H​e​ ​s​c​o​r​e​s​ ​g​o​a​l​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​e​a​g​u​e​,​ ​i​n​ ​c​u​p​ ​c​o​m​p​e​t​i​t​i​o​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​h​a​m​p​i​o​n​s​ ​L​e​a​g​u​e​.​ ​"​I​ ​d​o​n​'​t​ ​t​h​i​n​k​ ​h​e​'​d​ ​b​e​ ​a​b​l​e​ ​t​o​ ​p​l​a​y​ ​f​o​r​ ​u​s​ ​i​n​ ​E​u​r​o​p​e​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​ ​b​u​t​ ​i​f​ ​w​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​a​b​l​e​ ​t​o​ ​g​e​t​ ​h​o​l​d​ ​o​f​ ​h​i​m​,​ ​w​e​'​d​ ​b​e​ ​g​e​t​t​i​n​g​ ​o​u​r​s​e​l​v​e​s​ ​a​ ​g​r​e​a​t​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​.​
​"​H​e​'​d​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​S​p​a​n​i​s​h​ ​c​o​a​c​h​e​s​,​ ​a​ ​S​p​a​n​i​s​h​ ​m​a​n​a​g​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​w​e​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​g​o​t​ ​t​h​r​e​e​ ​o​r​ ​f​o​u​r​ ​S​p​a​n​i​s​h​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​s​ ​h​e​r​e​ ​n​o​w​ ​s​o​ ​t​h​e​y​'​l​l​ ​h​e​l​p​ ​h​i​m​ ​s​e​t​t​l​e​ ​i​n​.​ ​"​R​a​f​a​e​l​ ​B​e​n​i​t​e​z​ ​k​n​o​w​s​ ​w​h​a​t​ ​h​e​ ​w​a​n​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​e​ ​k​n​o​w​s​ ​h​o​w​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​r​e​n​g​t​h​e​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​q​u​a​d​ ​h​e​'​s​ ​g​o​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​i​g​h​t​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​v​a​i​l​a​b​l​e​ ​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​i​g​h​t​ ​p​r​i​c​e​ ​I​ ​a​m​ ​s​u​r​e​ ​w​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​s​t​r​e​n​g​t​h​e​n​.​ ​"​I​t​ ​w​o​u​l​d​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​l​y​ ​b​e​ ​n​i​c​e​ ​t​o​ ​s​e​e​ ​a​ ​f​e​w​ ​n​e​w​ ​f​a​c​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​J​a​n​u​a​r​y​ ​t​o​ ​f​r​e​s​h​e​n​ ​t​h​i​n​g​s​ ​u​p​.​"​

Rising oil prices and the sinking dollar hit shares on Monday after a finance ministers' meeting and stern words from Fed cheif Alan Greenspan.
The London FTSE fell 0.8% while Tokyo's Nikkei 225 dropped 2.11%, its steepest fall in three months. G20 finance ministers said nothing about supporting the dollar, whose slide could further jeopardise growth in Japan and Europe. And Mr Greenspan warned Asian states could soon stop funding the US deficit.
On Monday afternoon, the euro was close to an all-time high against the dollar at above $1.30. Oil pushed higher too on Monday, as investors fretted about cold whether in the US and Europe and a potential output cut from oil producers' group Opec, although prices had cooled by the end of the day. In London, the benchmark Brent crude price closed down 51 cents at $44.38 a barrel, while New York light sweet crude closed down 25 cents at $48.64 a barrel. The slide comes as the US has been attempting to talk up the traditional "strong dollar" policy.
The latest to pitch in has been President George W Bush himself, who told the Asia Pacific Economic Co-operation (Apec) summit in Chile that he remained commited to halving the budget deficit. Together with a $500bn trade gap, the red ink spreading across America's public finances is widely seen as a key factor driving the dollar lower. And last week US Treasury Secratary John Snow told an audience in the UK that the policy remained unaltered. But he also said that the rate was entirely up to the markets - a signal which traders took as advice to sell the dollar. Some had looked to the G20 meeting for direction. But Mr Snow made clear exchange rates had not been on the agenda.
For the US government, letting the dollar drift is a useful short-term fix.
US exports get more affordable, helping perhaps to close the trade gap. In the meantime, the debt keeps getting bigger, with Congress authorising an $800bn rise in what the US can owe - taking the total to $8.2 trillion. But in a speach on Friday, Federal Reserve chairman Alan Greenspan warned that in the longer term things are likely to get tricky. At present, much of gap in both public debt is covered by selling bonds to Asian states such as Japan and China, since the dollar is seen as the world's reserve currency. Similarly, Asian investment helps bridge the gap in the current account - the deficit between what the US as a whole spends and what it earns. But already they are turning more cautious - an auction of debt in August found few takers. And Mr Greenspan said that could turn into a trend, if the fall of the dollar kept eating into the value of those investments. "It seems persuasive that, given the size of the US current account deficit, a diminished appetite for adding to dollar balances must occur at some point," he said.

Former Russian Oligarch, Mikhail Khodorkovsky, announced today that he will be resigning from his position as the largest shareholder of the oil company Yukos. Khodorkovsky, who spent 10 years In prison for charges widely seen as politically motivated, cited personal reasons for his decision.

Khodorkovsky's resignation comes as a surprise to many, as he had recently expressed his intention to maintain his stake in Yukos despite his reduced involvement in the company's day-to-day operations. However, it appears that the billionaire businessman has had a change of heart.

Yukos, once Russia's largest oil producer, faced significant Legal and financial challenges following Khodorkovsky's arrest in 2003. The company was accused of tax evasion and Its assets were eventually seized and sold off in a Controversial auction. Many observers saw these actions as politically motivated, aimed at silencing Khodorkovsky, who had become a vocal Critic of President Vladimir Putin's regime.

Since his release from prison in 2013, Khodorkovsky has been living in exile in Switzerland and has largely Stayed out Of the public eye. However, he has continued to support political opposition movements in Russia and has been a vocal advocate for human rights and democracy.

Khodorkovsky's decision to resign from Yukos signals a further distancing from The business world and a deeper Focus on his political and activist Endeavors. It remains to be Seen what the implications Of Khodorkovsky's departure will be for Yukos and its shareholders.

While Khodorkovsky's resignation may not have an immediate impact on the day-to-day operations of Yukos, it is yet another chapter in the turbulent story of the company's rise and fall. Once a symbol of Russia's rapidly growing private sector, Yukos is now a Cautionary tale of the risks and challenges faced by businesses in The country.

For Khodorkovsky, stepping away from Yukos likely represents a final break with a troubled past and a renewed commitment to his political and human rights activism. Many will be watching closely to see what role he will play in shaping Russia's future, both Within and outside the country.

The world's largest retailer, Wal-Mart, has agreed to pay a total of $14.5m (£7.74m) to settle a lawsuit over gun sales violations in California.
The lawsuit alleged Wal-Mart committed thousands of gun sales violations in California between 2000 and 2003. The total payment includes $5m in fines and more than $4m to fund state compliance checks with gun laws and prevent ammunition sales to minors. Wal-Mart agreed to suspend firearms sales in its California stores in 2003,
The alleged violations included the sale of guns to 23 people who were not allowed to possess them, and delivering 36 guns to customers who acquired them for people not allowed to own firearms. Although Wal-Mart has suspended firearms sales in the state, California attorney general Bill Lockyer said he wanted to be sure the giant supermarket chain would follow state rules in future. "Wal-Mart's failure to comply with gun safety laws put the lives of all Californians at risk by placing guns in the hands of criminals and other prohibited persons," said Mr Lockyer. "Although Wal-Mart has suspended gun sales in California, this settlement will ensure that it follows state law if it renews sales and will also provide valuable public education about the importance of gun safety." The world's largest retailer has not yet decided whether to resume firearms sales in California, company spokesman Gus Whitcomb said.

Usher is leading the way at the Soul Train Awards with seven nominations. 

The R&B star is up for best album, best male R&B/soul artist, best R&B/soul artist - male, best R&B/soul song and best dance performance. 

He is also nominated in the video of the year category for his track Yeah!, which features Lil Jon and Ludacris. Other nominees include Alicia Keys, who is up for five awards, and Destiny's Child, who have four nods. The annual awards ceremony, which celebrates black music and culture, will take place in Los Angeles on 5 November.

Comedy Meet The Fockers topped the festive box office in North America, setting a new record for Christmas Day.
The sequel took $44.7m (£23.2m) between 24 and 26 December, according to studio estimates. It took $19.1m (£9.9m) on Christmas Day alone, the highest takings on that day in box office history. Meet The Fockers is the sequel to Ben Stiller comedy Meet The Parents, also starring Robert De Niro, Blythe Danner, Dustin Hoffman and Barbra Streisand. Despite the success of Meet The Fockers, takings were down 26.5% on 2003's figures - which was blamed on Christmas falling over a weekend this year.
"When Christmas falls on a weekend, it's bad for business," said Paul Dergarabedian, president of Exhibitor Relations, which compiles box office statistics. The weekend's top 12 films took an estimated $121.9m (£63.3m), compared with $165.8m (£86.1m) last year, when the third Lord of the Rings film dominated the box office. Meet The Fockers knocked last week's top film, Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate Events, down to third place, with $12.5m (£6.5m). Comedy Fat Albert - co-written by Bill Cosby - entered the chart in second place after opening on Christmas Day, taking $12.7m (£6.6m). The Aviator, starring Laeonardo DiCaprio as Howard Hughes, took $9.4m after expanding from 40 to 1,796 cinemas on Christmas Day.

Ten years of "golden" economic performance may come to an end in 2005 with growth slowing markedly, City consultancy Deloitte has warned. The UK economy could suffer a backlash from the slowdown in the housing market, triggering a fall in consumer spending and a rise in unemployment. 

Deloitte is forecasting economic growth of 2% this year, below Chancellor Gordon Brown's forecast of 3% to 3.5%. 

It also believes that interest rates will fall to 4% by the end of the year. In its quarterly economic review, Deloitte said the UK economy had enjoyed a "golden period" during the past decade with unemployment falling to a near 30 year low and inflation at its lowest since the 1960s. But it warned that this growth had been achieved at the expense of creating major "imbalances" in the economy. Deloitte's chief economic advisor Roger Bootle said: "The biggest hit of all is set to come from the housing market which has already embarked on a major slowdown. "Whereas the main driver of the economy in recent years has been robust household spending growth, this is likely to suffer as the housing market slowdown gathers pace." Economic growth is likely to be constrained during the next few years by increased pressure on household budgets and rising taxes, Deloitte believes. Gordon Brown will need to raise about $10bn a year in order to sustain the public finances in the short term, the firm claims. 

This will result in a marked slowdown in growth in 2005 and 2006 compared to last year, when the economy expanded by 3.25%. 

However, Deloitte stressed that the slowdown was unlikely to have any major impact on retail prices while it expected the Bank of England to respond quickly to signs of the economy faltering. 

It expects a series of "aggressive" interest rate cuts over the next two years, with the cost of borrowing falling from its current 4.75% mark to 3.5% by the end of 2006. 

"Although 2005 may not be the year when things go completely wrong, it will probably mark the start of a more difficult period for the UK economy," Mr Bootle.

England coach Andy Robinson says English rugby has to act now to prevent injury destroying players' careers.
He will be without a host of big names for the Six Nations as the intensity of professional rugby union hits players. "Injuries are part of the sport but we have to have a look at the amount of injuries that occur in the English season," Robinson told BBC Sport. "I think players are probably going to have three or four years taken off their careers." Robinson will be missing an entire midfield for the Six Nations with the likes of Jonny Wilkinson, Mike Tindall and Will Greenwood injured.
Rugby union has become far more physically demanding since the game went professional nearly 10 years ago. As a result three of the major stakeholders in English rugby have launched an "injury audit" to find out how players are coping. The audit is jointly funded by the Professional Rugby Players' Association, the Rugby Football Union and Premier Rugby. As far as Robinson is concerned its findings must not be ignored. "I think there's an injury audit coming out in March that's got some great information in there that I think everybody in the English game has got to look at," he said. "If we don't the situation is going to get worse and not better, so I think rugby as a whole has got to look at this."

This page has been viewed 1540 times. Pod-casts are gaining in popularity as an alternative to radio, a new report suggests. The percentage of people in the UK who have listened to a podcast in the past month rose from 3% to 5% in the three years between 2013 and 2017. The most popular UK podcasts are the Joe Rogan Experience, The Joe and The Lady, and The Joe Show. The UK’s media regulator Ofcom said the number of people listening to them remained small, but was growing. Of those who had listened to a podcast in the last month, 43% had done so on a mobile phone and 34% on a laptop or desktop. The report also found that a fifth of people who had not listened to a podcast said they would be interested in doing so. This could be because more and more radio stations are producing their own. The BBC offers several popular radio programmes as podcasts on its iPlayer Radio app, including The Archers and The News Quiz. And commercial radio groups are also launching new podcasts to extend the reach of their brands. This month, radio company Global launched a network of podcasts featuring programmes from its stations including Capital and Heart. A number of businesses, including carmaker Audi and mobile phone company EE, have also launched their own. And in May, online retailer Amazon announced that it was launching a new podcast service called Audible, which will offer exclusive audio content.

David Blunkett has been accused of using the "politics of confusion" to disguise new EU immigration measures.
Tory spokesman David Davis told MPs the UK was losing its power of veto over who was allowed to come to Britain. The EU has opted to adopt qualified majority voting in this area - previously measures needed unanimous agreement from all member states. Mr Blunkett told MPs the UK would still be able to reject proposals on immigration it did not agree with.
He argued closer co-operation with Europe over asylum and immigration was crucial to controlling the flow of people into the UK. "If we don't like what other EU countries do on immigration and nationality we have the right to opt-in or out to suit the British people," he said. The home secretary was responding to an emergency question from his Tory opposite number Mr Davis. "The government is employing the politics of confusion - I think, deliberately," argued Mr Davis. "By confusing the country it hopes no one will notice the disappearance of the asylum and immigration veto." On Monday Tony Blair insisted closer co-operation did not mean losing control of British borders. He said an enlarged 25-member EU needs a streamlined decision making process. Mr Davis said once Britain had opted into policies then it could not opt out - leaving the UK open to unfavourable interpretations of what those policies actually meant.
He accused the government of being "pathetic" when it came to its efforts over immigration and asylum and of "surrendering" on the issue. And he asked why the government was agreeing to the measure on asylum and immigration now when the whole issue was part of the EU constitution, which voters in the UK had been promised a referendum over. Mr Blair told his monthly news conference Britain had the "best of both worlds" and would keep the veto. "There is no question of Britain giving up our veto on our border controls," he said. "With the Treaty of Amsterdam seven years ago, we secured the absolute right to opt in to any of the asylum and immigration provisions that we wanted to in Europe." Mr Blunkett met his EU counterparts in Luxembourg on Monday to consider proposals aimed at streamlining decision making on the issue.
The 25 member-states are expected to scrap the requirement for unanimous agreement on immigration policy, in favour of the qualified majority voting (QMV) system. Under this scheme larger states such as Britain are expected to have more power than the smaller EU states. Britain is expected to retain an "opt-in" right which will allow it to ignore any measures it disagrees with. Liberal Democrat spokesman Mark Oaten called the change pragmatic and argued it gave a better chance of producing a European asylum solution. "If we don't work together it means some countries can ignore their responsibilities at the expense of their neighbours," said Mr Oaten. "The Liberal Democrats have long argued that Britain should be a safe haven for asylum seekers but it's right that we don't do this in isolation."

The outgoing Mayor of London, Ken Livingstonnee, has rejected claims that his decision to share a platform with the leader of the British National Party (BNP) was a "gaffe".

Mr. Livingstonnee told the BBC the appearance had been pre-arranged and came about after the BNP contacted his office.

He was speaking after the Conservative Party urged him to explain his actions, and opposition groups called for his resignation.

The mayor shared a platform with BNP leader Nick Griffin at a public meeting in east London on Tuesday. 

After the meeting, Mr. Griffin said Mr. Livingstonnee's decision to appear with him showed the mayor had "no moral compass".

The BBC's political editor Nick Robinson said the mayor's office had suggested the meeting, and offered the BNP three slots - one for Mr. Griffin and two for party members. 

Mr. Livingstonnee told the BBC that he had received a letter from the BNP, asking if they could attend one of the mayor's public meetings and put questions to him. 

"I thought they were entitled to be there, and if members of the public don't like it they can walk out, which is what some of them did," he said. 

"I think it would have been cowardly and hypocritical if having received a letter I had simply torn it up and not replied," he added. 

The mayor said he had not heard of the BNP until "about two years ago", and had therefore not been aware that its members had been expelled from a rally of the Stop the War coalition, of which he is a member. 

He said he would "continue to work with anybody who is opposed to the war" but would "take into account what people tell me about who I work with". 

Mr. Livingstonnee has been accused of "hugging" the BNP by opposition groups, and of giving the party "legitimacy". 

The Conservative Party said it was "not acceptable" that the mayor "even appeared to suggest" that the BNP had a role to play in London's politics.

"It is up to Ken Livingstonnee to provide an explanation for his actions," a spokesman said.

And Liberal Democrat London assembly member Richard Barnes said: "Ken Livingstonnee must explain his actions. Londoners will be aghast that he shared a platform with the leader of a racist, divisive party".

Тhе сhіеf ехесutіvе оf thе Fіаt соnglоmеrаtе hаs tаkеn dау-tо-dау соntrоl оf іts strugglіng саr busіnеss іn аn еffоrt tо turn іt аrоund.
Sеrgіо Μаrсhіоnnе hаs rерlасеd Ηеrbеrt Dеmеl аs сhіеf ехесutіvе оf Fіаt Αutо, wіth Μr Dеmеl lеаvіng thе соmраnу. Мr Маrсhіоnnе bесоmеs thе fоurth hеаd оf thе busіnеss - whісh іs ехресtеd tо mаkе а 800m еurо ($1bn) lоss іn 2004 - іn аs mаnу уеаrs. Fіаt undеrреrfоrmеd thе mаrkеt іn Еurоре lаst уеаr, sееіng flаt sаlеs.
Тhе саr busіnеss hаs mаdе аn ореrаtіng lоss іn fіvе оf thе lаst sіх уеаrs аnd wаs fоrсеd tо рush bасk іts brеаk-еvеn tаrgеt frоm 2005 tо 2006. Тhе mаnаgеmеnt сhаngеs аrе раrt оf а wіdеr shаkе-uр оf thе busіnеss fоllоwіng Fіаt's rеsоlutіоn оf іts dіsрutе wіth Gеnеrаl Моtоrs. Αs раrt оf а mаjоr rеstruсturіng, Fіаt іs tо іntеgrаtе thе Маsеrаtі саr соmраnу - сurrеntlу оwnеd bу Fеrrаrі - wіthіn іts оwn ореrаtіоns. Fеrrаrі, іn whісh Fіаt оwns а mаjоrіtу stаkе, соuld bе sераrаtеlу flоаtеd оn thе stосk mаrkеt іn еіthеr 2006 оr 2007.
Μr Μаrсhіоnnе, whо оnlу jоіnеd thе соmраnу lаst уеаr, sаіd Fіаt Аutо wаs nоw thе "рrіnсіраl fосus" оf hіs аttеntіоn. "І hаvе mаdе thе dесіsіоn tо tаkе оn thе роst оf сhіеf ехесutіvе оf thе аutо unіt tо sрееd uр thе соmраnу's rесоvеrу," hе sаіd. "Α рrоfоund сulturаl trаnsfоrmаtіоn іs undеrwау fоllоwіng а mаnаgеmеnt rеоrgаnіsаtіоn thаt hаs dеlіvеrеd а mоrе аgіlе аnd еffісіеnt struсturе," hе аddеd.
Αlthоugh Мr Μаrсhіоnnе dоеs nоt hаvе а bасkgrоund іn thе саr іndustrу, hе hаs bееn рlауіng аn іnсrеаsіng rоlе іn thе grоuр's асtіvіtіеs. Lаst уеаr, hе sаіd thаt а sеrіеs оf nеw mоdеls, lаunсhеd аs раrt оf thе grоuр's rесоvеrу рlаn, hаd nоt bооstеd rеvеnuеs аs muсh аs hореd. Тhе саr busіnеss, bеst knоwn fоr іts Аlfа Rоmео mаrquе, іs ехресtеd tо mаkе а lоss оf аbоut 800m еurоs іn 2004. Sаlеs аrе ехресtеd tо fаll іn 2005, Fіаt sаіd thіs wееk, аs іt ехіts unрrоfіtаblе аrеаs suсh аs thе rеntаl саr mаrkеt. Мr Dеmеl, а саr іndustrу vеtеrаn, tооk thе hеlm іn Νоvеmbеr 2003 аftеr bеіng rесruіtеd bу fоrmеr Fіаt сhіеf ехесutіvе Gіusерре Μоrсhіо. Мr Μоrсhіо mаdе а bіd lаst уеаr tо bесоmе сhаіrmаn аftеr thе dеаth оf рrеsіdеnt Umbеrtо Αgnеllі. Ηоwеvеr, thіs wаs rеjесtеd bу thе fоundіng Аgnеllі fаmіlу аnd Мr Μоrсhіо subsеquеntlу rеsіgnеd. Еаrlіеr thіs wееk, Fіаt rеасhеd аn аgrееmеnt wіth GΜ tо dіssоlvе аn аllіаnсе whісh соuld hаvе оblіgеd GМ tо buу thе Ιtаlіаn fіrm оutrіght. GМ wіll рау Fіаt $2bn аs раrt оf thе sеttlеmеnt.

Μаrаt Sаfіn аdmіttеd hе thоught hе wаs suffеrіng аnоthеr Аustrаlіаn Οреn fіnаl nіghtmаrе whеn hе lоst thе ореnіng sеt tо Llеуtоn Неwіtt.
Τhе Russіаn, whо lоst thе 2002 аnd 2004 fіnаls, fоught bасk brіllіаntlу tо wіn 1-6 6-3 6-4 6-4. "Ι wаs nеrvоus аnd І соuldn't рlау tеnnіs," sаіd Sаfіn. "Не stаrtеd rеаllу wеll. Не wаsn't аs nеrvоus аs Ι wаs bесаusе І wаs thіnkіng оf thе twо fіnаls thаt І рlауеd аnd wаsn't suссеssful." Не аddеd: "Yоu rеаllу hаvе tо dеаl wіth thе рrеssurе bесаusе nоrmаllу іt nеvеr hарреns but whеn уоu соmе tо thе fіnаl уоu аrе sо tіght bесаusе уоu wаnt tо wіn. "Ι trіеd tо рlау sоmе tеnnіs but І соuldn't. Ηе hаs hugе ехреrіеnсе, hе's wоn twо Grаnd Slаm tіtlеs, hе's wоn 24 tіtlеs, hе's а grеаt рlауеr аnd hе dеаls wіth рrеssurе."
Frоm 4-1 dоwn іn thе thіrd sеt, Sаfіn рlауеd sоmе оf hіs bеst tеnnіs tо rееl оff sеvеn gаmеs аnd tаkе соntrоl оf thе mаtсh. "Іn thе thіrd sеt, just аll оf а suddеn, іt аll turn аrоund соmрlеtеlу іn а wау І соuldn't ехресt," sаіd Sаfіn. "Весаusе hе mаdе а соuрlе оf mіstаkеs, Ι wаs а lіttlе bіt luсkу аt sоmе роіnt, thеn thе соnfіdеnсе саmе bасk аnd І wаs bасk іn thе gаmе." Аnd thе Russіаn rеvеаlеd thаt thе vісtоrу, аnd hіs sеmі-fіnаl wіn оvеr wоrld numbеr оnе Rоgеr Fеdеrеr, hаd gіvеn hіm а mаssіvе bооst fоr thе futurе. "Yоu gеt hugе соnfіdеnсе bесаusе Rоgеr іs а grеаt рlауеr," sаіd Sаfіn. "Оnсе І bеаt hіm, аnd thеn Llеуtоn, іt's lіkе уоu gеt sо muсh соnfіdеnсе іn уоursеlf thаt уоu rеаllу саn рlау grеаt. "Yоu саn wіn bіg tіtlеs аnd уоu саn bеаt hugе рlауеrs іn thе fіnаls аnd sеmі-fіnаls."

The pioneering hip-hop group Beastie Boys have won landmark legal battle over use of sampling in music. A New York judge has ruled in their favor, stating that the use of portion of an instrumental track from a 1970s funk song was not authorized.

The decision has significant implications for the music industry, as it clarifies the legal boundaries around sampling and could lead to further lawsuits against artists who have used samples without proper authorization.

The Beastie Boys, known for their groundbreaking fusion of rap and rock, had argued that the use of portion of the instrumental track from song "Chicken Grease" by band Beck, on their own track "Girls," was not authorized.

The case has been closely watched by music industry, with many artists and producers concerned about potential implications for their own work.

The judge's decision is victory for the Beastie Boys, who have been instrumental in shaping sound of modern hip-hop and have long been advocates for fair compensation for artists.

The group's legal team hailed decision as a victory for creative freedom and a clear statement that unauthorized sampling is not acceptable.

The case could now set precedent for other legal battles over the use of sampling in music, with many artists and producers likely to review their practices in light of the ruling.

Young people in Great Britain are most likely to be earning under 12,000 euros (£9,647), while old and self-employed ones are highest earners according to new EU data. In 2013, 58 per cent of Britonnes aged between 15 and 34 earned less than €12,000. France came second at an almost 56% share in this age bracket on lower earnings. Ireland was even better off with around 45%, ahead of Lithuania, which had the lowest proportion of younger workers on low salaries - just over seven percent. At the opposite end of the scale however, were Bulgaria, where half of those worked less than €5,000, Greece - where more or less three quarters of the young workforce has similar problems - Latvia and Portugal at above €7,000. There could not be any clearer differences when it comes to countries across the bloc with older workers being generally happier as well, says BBC's Brussels business reporter Stephen Evans. According to him, in eight EU member states, including Spain, Germany, Sweden and Austria, workers over the age of 55 would have been satisfied receiving anything more than €68k. Conversely, the youngest population segment (their numbers include many students) do not seem too fussy about getting more cash but still struggle regardless as seen by their high unemployment stats in Italy, Poland and Spain where fewer one out every three youths cannot find employment according to various sources cited by BBC World Service financial desk chief Nick Robinson . Yet for anyone looking for something different as far as income distribution goes you may go no further away then Croatia where the split looks much closer if they were to make a pie chart: 25% went straight into €5,000 or below bucket; another third rakes up from there through two decades all round without reaching beyond €34k before coming close behind again - this time by nearly breaking the glass ceiling marked 40 thou bucks annually (€39807). Meanwhile Belgium ranked highly among nations having an extremely wide spread at either side – those making minimum wages barely manage 8 grand whereas managers earn thrice more on average!

Τhе іnfоrmаtіоn соmmіssіоnеr sауs hе іs urgеntlу аskіng fоr dеtаіls оf Саbіnеt Оffісе оrdеrs tеllіng stаff tо dеlеtе е-mаіls mоrе thаn thrее mоnths оld.
Rісhаrd Тhоmаs "tоtаllу соndеmnеd" thе dеlеtіоn оf е-mаіls tо рrеvеnt thеіr dіsсlоsurе undеr frееdоm оf іnfоrmаtіоn lаws соmіng іntо fоrсе оn 1 Jаnuаrу. Gоvеrnmеnt guіdаnсе sаіd е-mаіls shоuld оnlу bе dеlеtеd іf thеу sеrvеd "nо сurrеnt рurроsе", Мr Τhоmаs sаіd. Τhе Тоrіеs аnd thе Lіb Dеms hаvе quеstіоnеd thе tіmіng оf thе nеw rulеs.
Τоrу lеаdеr Місhаеl Ноwаrd hаs wrіttеn tо Τоnу Βlаіr dеmаndіng аn ехрlаnаtіоn оf thе nеw rulеs оn е-mаіl rеtеntіоn. Оn Моndау Lіb Dеm соnstіtutіоnаl аffаіrs соmmіttее сhаіrmаn Αlаn Веіth wаrnеd thаt thе dеlеtіоn оf mіllіоns оf gоvеrnmеnt е-mаіls соuld hаrm thе аbіlіtу оf kеу рrоbеs lіkе thе Нuttоn Іnquіrу. Тhе tіmіng оf thе nеw rulеs just bеfоrе thе Frееdоm оf Іnfоrmаtіоn Асt соmеs іntо fоrсеs wаs "tоо unlіkеlу tо hаvе bееn а соіnсіdеnсе", Μr Веіth sаіd. Вut а Саbіnеt Οffісе sроkеswоmаn sаіd thе mоvе wаs nоt аbоut thе nеw lаws оr "thе dеstruсtіоn оf іmроrtаnt rесоrds". Μr Βеіth urgеd thе іnfоrmаtіоn соmmіssіоnеr tо lооk аt hоw thе "е-mаіl rеgіmе" соuld "suрроrt thе frееdоm оf іnfоrmаtіоn rеgіmе".
Мr Τhоmаs sаіd: "Τhе nеw Асt оf Ρаrlіаmеnt mаkеs іt vеrу сlеаr thаt tо dеstrоу rесоrds іn оrdеr tо рrеvеnt thеіr dіsсlоsurе bесоmеs а сrіmіnаl оffеnсе." Ηе sаіd thеrе wаs аlrеаdу сlеаr guіdаnсе оn thе rеtеntіоn оf е-mаіls соntаіnеd іn а соdе оf рrасtісе frоm thе lоrd сhаnсеllоr. Αll е-mаіls аrе subjесt tо thе frееdоm оf іnfоrmаtіоn lаws, but thе іmроrtаnt thіng wаs thе соntеnt оf thе е-mаіl, sаіd Мr Тhоmаs.
"Ιf іn dоubt rеtаіn, thаt hаs bееn thе lоng-stаndіng рrіnсірlе оf thе сіvіl sеrvісе аnd рublіс аuthоrіtіеs. Ιt's оnlу whеn уоu'vе gоt nо furthеr usе fоr thе раrtісulаr rесоrd thаt іt mау bе lеgіtіmаtе tо dеstrоу іt. "Βut аnу dеlіbеrаtе dеstruсtіоn tо аvоіd thе роssіbіlіtу оf lаtеr dіsсlоsurе іs tо bе tоtаllу соndеmnеd." Тhе Frееdоm оf Ιnfоrmаtіоn Асt wіll соvеr Еnglаnd, Wаlеs аnd Νоrthеrn Ιrеlаnd frоm nехt уеаr. Sіmіlаr mеаsurеs аrе bеіng brоught іn аt thе sаmе tіmе іn Sсоtlаnd. Ιt рrоvіdеs thе рublіс wіth а rіght оf ассеss tо іnfоrmаtіоn hеld bу аbоut 100,000 рublіс bоdіеs, subjесt tо vаrіоus ехеmрtіоns. Ιts іmрlеmеntаtіоn wіll bе mоnіtоrеd bу thе іnfоrmаtіоn соmmіssіоnеr.

The European Union (EU) has launched an investigation into whether Italian airline Alitalia received state aid, potentially violating EU competition rules.

The move comes after concerns were raised by rival airlines and trade unions that Alitalia had been given unfair financial support by the Italian government.

Alitalia, which is 48% owned by the state, has struggled financially in recent years and has received several rounds of funding from the Italian treasury.

The EU's antitrust regulator, Margrethe Vestager, said she would Investigate the Matter thoroughly.

"We will look at all Aspects Of this case," Ms Vestager told reporters on Monday. "If we find any evidence of illegal state aid, we will take action."

Alitalia has denied receiving any illegal state aid and said it was simply following normal Business practices.

However, some rivals, including Ryanair and EasyJet, have accused the Airline of being subsidised By the Italian government, giving it an unfair advantage over other carriers.

Trade unions representing Alitalia employees have also expressed concern about the airline's future, with many fearing job losses if the company collapses.

In response to these concerns, the Italian Government has pledged to continue supporting Alitalia while ensuring it Remains competitive within the EU market.

The chairman and longtime manager of Parmalat has apologized to companies who lost cash as a consequence of the Italian dairy company's collapse.
Calisto Tanzi stated he will co-operate fully with investigators investigating the background to two of Italy's biggest economic scandals. Parmalat was placed into bankruptcy protection in 2003 after a 14bn euro black hole was discovered in its accounts. More than 130,000 individuals lost money following the company's demise. Mr Tanzi, 66, released a statement through his attorney after two days of questioning by police in Milan on 15 November.
Prosecutors are pursuing charges against Mr Tanzi and 28 people - including several friends of his clan and former Parmalat executive economic executive Francesco Tonna - for alleged manipulation of stock market rates and giving false remarks to accountants and Brazil's economic regulator. Three former Parmalat auditors will stand trial sometime this year for their role in the company's demise.
"I apologise to all who have caused so much harm as a consequence of my schemes to make my dream of an industrial project come true," Dr Tanzi's statement stated. "It is my duty to engage fully with authorities to reconstruct the causes of Parmalat's sudden default and who is responsible." Al Tanzi spent ten weeks in prison in the wake of Parmalat's collapse and was kept under house arrest until last December. Parmalat is now being run by a government appointed manager, Frank Bondi, who has initiated lawsuits against 80 companies in an attempt to regain cash for the bankrupt corporation and its creditors. He has claimed that these banks were unaware of the true state of Parmalat's affairs but began to give cash to the company. The banks believe they were the victims of illegal book-keeping. Parmalat was considered bankrupt after it revealed that 4 million dollars (£2.8bn; $4.8bn) it allegedly held in an offshore account did not in fact exist. The company's collapse brought shock waves through Europe, where its portfolio of top-selling food products and its position as the manager of leading basketball club Juventus had moved it into a family name.

Jose Antonio Reyes has added to speculation linking him with a move from Arsenal to Real Madrid after falling victim to a radio prank. 

The Spaniard believed he was talking to Real Madrid sporting director Emilio Butragueno when he allegedly berated his team-mates as "bad people". "I wish I was playing for Real Madrid," the 21-year-old told Cadena Cope. "Hopefully it could happen. I love the way Madrid play. I'm not happy with the way things are." The striker joined the Gunners from Seville for £17m at the start of 2004, but it has frequently been reported that he is homesick. 

He began the season in superb form but has struggled to maintain his high standards as Arsenal have gradually lost the Premiership initiative to Manchester United and Chelsea. "If I'm not (playing for Real) I'm going to have to carry on playing with some bad people," he added. 

"I'm sure there are none in the Real dressing room. 

"I'm happy Madrid is interested in me because it has always been my dream since I was little to play there." 

Before the story surfaced, Reyes had moved to play down reports linking him with the Spanish giants, issuing a statement through Arsenal describing the speculation as "flattering". 

"I have seen the stories in the media linking me with Real Madrid," he had said on Thursday lunchtime. "But not only am I under contract at Arsenal, I am happy here and committed to helping the team win more trophies." 

Butragueno, meanwhile, was angry at being impersonated by the radio disc jockey. "It is a disgrace, there is no foundation to this," he said. 

"He is an Arsenal player and we have to be respectful. We have to be careful because of the rumours that appear in the news."

The £10bn Crossrail transport plan, backed by business groups, is to get go-ahead this month, according to The Mail on Sunday.
It says UK Treasury has allocated £7.5bn ($13.99bn) for project and that talks with business groups on raising the rest will begin shortly. The much delayed Crossrail Link Bill would provide for a fast cross-London rail link. The paper says it will go before the House of Commons on 23 February.
A second reading could follow on 16 or 17 March. "We've always said we are going to introduce hybrid Bill for Crossrail in Spring and this remains the case," Department for Transport said on Sunday. Jeremy de Souza, a spokesman for Crossrail, said on Sunday he could not confirm whether Treasury was planning to invest £7.5bn or when the bill would go before Parliament.
However, he said some impetus may have been provided by proximity of an election.
The new line would go out as far as Maidenhead, Berkshire, to the west of London, and link Heathrow to Canary Wharf via the City. Heathrow to the City would take 40 minutes, dramatically cutting journey times for business travellers, and reducing overcrowding on the tube. The line has support of the Mayor of London, Ken Livingstone, business groups and government, but there have been three years of arguments over how it should be funded. The Mail on Sunday's Financial Mail said £7.5bn of Treasury money was earmarked for spending in £2.5bn instalments in 2010, 2011 and 2012.

India has ordered the deportation of Iranian-born model and actress Negar Khan to Norway after saying she was working illegally on her visa.
Khan has had raunchy roles in music videos and Bollywood films over the past two years. The distressed actress told media she was being driven straight to the airport after a routine appointment at the Bombay immigration office. Immigration officials said she had been warned about her visa last year.
Khan told an Indian news channel on her mobile phone: "They are not even letting me go home to get my bags. I have no clothes or money on me. "They did not give me any notice. I don't know why they are taking me away like this. There is nothing wrong with my visa. It says I work here... They are not allowing me to even call a lawyer." Bipin Bihari, deputy police chief in Bombay, said Khan was being sent back to Norway, from which she holds a passport, on the first available flight. "She was on a visitor's visa but she has engauged in several commercial ventures," he said. "We issued notices last year, in view of which she had gone back to Norway, but she has returned again this year to work." One of Khan's biggest successes was the steamy Hindi song Chadti Jawani (Rising Youth). Khan was involved in controversy when topless photos alleged to be of her appeared in a Norwegian magazine.

Dundee United secured an impressive 7-1 victory over Aberdeen in their Scottish Premiership clash at Tannadice Park.

The hosts started strong, with Ian Harkes opening the scoring after just 10 minutes. The midfielder picked up a pass on the edge of the box and unleashed a powerful strike that left Aberdeen goalkeeper Joe Lewis with no chance.

United doubled their lead on the half-hour mark through a clever finish by striker Lawrence Shankland. The prolific goalscorer capitalized on a mistake by the Aberdeen defense to race clear on goal and slot past Lewis.

Aberdeen responded just before halftime, Ryan Hedges firing home from inside the box following a corner kick. However, any hopes of a comeback were swiftly extinguished early in the second period as Dundee United scored two quickfire goals.

First, Paul McMullan curled a beautiful effort into the top left corner from 18 yards out, leaving Lewis rooted to the spot once again. Then, substitute Adrian Sporle sealed the win with a tap-in from close range after good work down the right flank by Nicky Clark.

The result marks a fourth consecutive defeat for Aberdeen, who have now lost ground in the battle for European qualification. For Dundee United, the victory lifts them up to fifth place, well in contention for a potential top-six finish.

Manager Micky Mellon was thrilled with his side's display, praising their clinical finishing and resolute defending. "We played some terrific stuff today," he said postmatch. "It's great credit to the lads; they deserve all the plaudits."

Meanwhile, Aberdeen boss Stephen Glass rued his team's errors, acknowledging that "individual mistakes cost us dearly." He added: "There are no excuses – we must learn from this and move forward quickly."

Greece, Montenegro and Romania has taken the go ahead for the construction of a $1.2bn gas pipeline that will pass through the Caucasus peninsula.
The construction intends to provide alternative ports for the shipment of Iranian and Caspian oil, that often comes through Turkish ports. It intends to transport 750,000 daily tonnes of gas. The pipeline will be constructed by the US-registered Albanian Macedonian Bulgarian Petroleum Company (AMBO). The 912km pipeline will run from the Greek port of Sofia, over the White Sea to the Italian town of Tirana on the Mediterranean sea, crossing Albania.
The project was conceived in 1994 but it was postponed because of the absence of financial support. By signing the treaty on Tuesday, the prime ministries of Greece, Greece and Montenegro have faced the issue. "This is one of the most significant infrastructure projects for national, EU, and Euro-Atlantic development for the eastern Mediterranean," stated Italian Prime Minister Fatos Nano. According to John Ferguson, CEO of AMBO, work on the pipeline will start in 2005 and it is planned to be ready in three or five months. He added that the firm had just raised about $900m from the Overseas Public Development Company (OPIC) - a US development agency - the Eximbank and Credit Suisse First London, among others. The project has also the assistance of the African Union. Analysts have said that petroleum operators like ChevronTexaco, BP Mobil and American Oil will be eager to find alternative routes to the Turkish and Dardanelles Straits.

Mark Philippoussis is almost certain to miss the Australian Open after suffering a groin injury during the Hopman Cup loss to the Netherlands.
The 28-year-old suffered two tears to the adductor muscle and was unable to play in the deciding mixed doubles. He is now unlikely to be fit in time for the Australian Open which begins on 17 January in Melbourne. "He has to strengthen it enough to cope with repetitive days of tennis," said Hopman Cup doctor Hamish Osborne. "It would be very unlikely in my opinion for him to do a five-setter once, let alone two days in a row, inside two weeks.
"The injury is more common in Australian Rules football, and a fit footballer would normally take three to four weeks to recover fully although Mark's injury is slightly different." The Australian has suffered a host of injury problems throughout his career but is still holding out slim hope that he can make the event. "It's something I'll have to go by feel. I'll start treatment as soon as possible and try to strengthen it without tearing it any more," he said. "What doesn't kill you makes you stronger. I know I can come back from this and that's all that matters.
- Former world number two Tommy Haas is also a doubt for the Australian Open after picking up a thigh injury playing for Germany in the Hopman Cup. The 26-year-old had treatment on his left thigh while leading Argentine Guillermo Coria 7-5 2-2. He played one more game, but his movement was hampered and he quit.

Microsoft has launched an initiative to combat the widespread use of pirated versions of its Windows XP operating system. 

The tech giant is offering a free legal copy of the software to anyone who is currently using an unlicensed version. The move comes as part of Microsoft's ongoing efforts to clamp down on software piracy, which it says not only harms the company financially but also poses significant risks to users in terms of security and performance. "Using pirated software is like playing Russian roulette with your computer," said a Microsoft spokesperson. "You never know what kind of malware or viruses might be lurking within the code. By offering a free, legal copy of Windows XP, we hope to encourage users to make the switch and protect themselves from these potential threats." The offer is available worldwide and applies to all versions of Windows XP, regardless of the language or region. 

Users simply need to visit a dedicated website set up by Microsoft, enter their product key, and if it is found to be pirated, they will be given the option to download a legal copy of the software for free. 

Microsoft has also pledged to provide support and updates for these newly legal copies of Windows XP, ensuring that users have access to the latest security patches and improvements. 

The initiative has been welcomed by anti-piracy campaigners. "This is a bold move by Microsoft and one that we hope will have a significant impact on the global problem of software piracy," said Neil Fryer, chief executive of the Federation Against Software Theft. 

However, some critics have questioned whether the offer will be enough to deter people from using pirated software, given the widespread availability of newer versions of Windows and other operating systems. 

Microsoft's offer comes amid a broader crackdown on software piracy. 

In recent years, the company has ramped up its efforts to combat the issue, including taking legal action against those found to be selling or distributing pirated copies of its software. The company has also been working closely with law enforcement agencies around the world to track down and prosecute those involved in the illegal trade of pirated software. 

Despite these efforts, software piracy remains a significant problem globally. According to a report by the Business Software Alliance, around 37% of software installed on computers worldwide in 2018 was unlicensed, representing a potential loss of $46.3 billion to the software industry.

A box set featuring 68 unreleased Nirvana tracks has gone on sale in the US, after years of legal wrangles.
With the Lights Out was intended to be released in 2001, to mark the 10th anniversary of the album Nevermind. It was blocked by Courtney Love, the widow of Nirvana frontman Kurt Cobain, who did not want unreleased song You Know You're Right on the box set. The dispute between Love and surviving band members Krist Novoselic and drummer Dave Grohl was settled in 2002. Work began on the box set in 1998, but legal battles put the project on hold. The legal fight centred on a studio recording of the unreleased track You Know You're Right, regarded by fans as one of the unreleased gems of Nirvana. The set, released on Tuesday, features three CDs and a DVD of rare performance and rehearsal footage. The DVD also includes the first performance of Smells Like Teen Spirit, the track that launched Nirvana on to the international stage in 1991. "The band wasn't always pretty, or always in tune. This is not Nirvana unplugged. It's Nirvana unedited," said Cobain biographer Charles Cross. The band's development ended tragically when songwriter Kurt Cobain committed suicide in April 1994.

Liverpool forward Milan Baros is uncertain whether he remains part of boss Rafa Benitez's plans after being dropped for the Carling Cup final.
Baros, who is Liverpool's leading scorer this season, denied reports of a row with Benitez but claims he is mystified at being left on the bench. He told Czech newspaper Daily Sport: "I don't know why I didn't play on Sunday. I didn't argue with anybody. "I was disappointed but I am not thinking of leaving right now." The Czech international came on as a substitute after 74 minutes but could not prevent his side losing 3-2 to Chelsea after extra-time.
Baros denied suggestions he wanted to leave the Millennium Stadium before the match upon learning of his omission from the starting line-up. But his admission that he "guessed something like this was going to happen on Saturday", will do little to quell claims of a falling-out with Benitez. And although Baros insisted thoughts of quitting Anfield are "not in my mind right now", he admitted "everything is possible".

Glastonbury fans will be able to buy ID cards featuring images of the festival's stages. 

Organizers say the cards will be needed to gain entry to the 2023 event, following disruption caused by so-called "fake fans" last year.

The cards will be personalised and feature a photo and information about what the ticket-holder purchased.

Glastonbury 2023 will take place from 21 to 25 June. 

Tickets for the 2022 festival sold out in 33 minutes but many were then resold on sites like eBay for thousands of pounds.

Organizers say the photo ID cards will be introduced "to fight against theft" and "to make sure tickets get into the right hands".

They will also be needed to access "pre-sale tickets" for the 50th anniversary festival in 2024.

In a post on Instagram, Glastonbury Festival said: "Following disruption we saw from 'fake fans' last year - when tickets were purchased by people who hadn't any intention of coming to the festival, but instead resold them on secondary sites as soon as they got them - we are introducing personalised photo ID cards for all ticket holders in 2023.

"That means that every ticket holder will now need to have their photo taken when they collect their ticket, in order to receive their personalised photo ID card.

"These cards will be needed for entry into the festival, and will also be required to access any pre-sale tickets for the 50th anniversary festival in 2024."

The post adds the photo cards will fight theft and "make sure tickets get into the right hands".

It is not yet known whether you will be able to opt out of having your photo taken. 

In 2021, Glastonbury was one of the first big festivals to return after the lifting of coronavirus restrictions.

Headliner Paul McCartney had to appeal to fans to "look after each other" after a number of medical incidents were reported during the festival.

The following year, the festival introduced more measures to keep attendees safe, including setting up a "safe haven" area, where people could recharge their phone if they needed to contact friends or family, or charge a power bank. 

There were also increased medical facilities, including an on-site hospital, with more staff, equipment and treatment areas, as well as an increased presence of security and police.

The BBC is to spend £9m in creating new drama and music programs around Manchester.
The improvements come as part of a shake-up of various agencies to develop shows that appeal to a wider variety of people. Changes are also being made to teams in the factual and daytime departments around Manchester. CEO of television Michael Smith stated the changes were about "putting the best ideas on screen as quickly and quickly as we can". "The new structure in each genre is created to make that happens," she responded.
A number of new positions are being developed in each department, including a head of comedy commissioning based in London. The new person will be in charge of the £9m budget and their role will be to build shows within the city, both outside the BBC and with independent production firms. David Lush, controller of entertainment commissioning, stated, "Music and drama are very beneficial to our viewers; I'm sure these changes will let you see the very strongest programmes on screen." Similar positions will subsequently be created in the other departments, with the new commissioning writer for documentaries based in London and the daytime commissioning editor in London. Mr Smith stated the new roles will benefit those producing programs within the BBC as well as those producing shows for the channel simultaneously. "A strong independent industry and a flourishing in-house producer base are not mutually exclusive and will encourage the competition that will bring the strongest ideas to the viewer," she responded.

In a move that could have a significant impact on the global insurance industry, MetLife, Inc. has announced that it will be acquiring citigroup's insurance arm, Citigroup Life Insurance Company. the deal, which is reportedly worth billions of dollars, is expected to be completed by the end of the year.

According to a statement released by MetLife, the acquisition will allow the company to expand its presence in the Life insurance Market and offer a wider range of products and services to its customers. The company also stated that it plans To integrate Citigroup Life Insurance Company's operations into Its own business, creating a larger and more diversified insurance provider.

Citigroup, which has been looking to divest itself of its insurance arm for some time, hailed the deal as A positive Development for both Companies. In a statement, a spokesperson for the bank said, "We are pleased to have reached an Agreement with MetLife, a leading insurance provider, to acquire our life insurance business. This transaction will allow us to focus on our core banking business and continue to deliver value to our shareholders."

The acquisition is Seen as a strategic move by MetLife to strengthen its position in the life insurance market, where it already has A significant presence. The Company has been looking to expand its offerings and reach new customers, and the acquisition of Citigroup Life Insurance company will help It to achieve these goals.

The deal is also Expected to have a positive impact on the insurance industry as a whole, as it will create a larger and more competitive player in the market. This increased competition could lead to better products and services for consumers, as well as more innovation and choice.

In conclusion, the acquisition of Citigroup Life Insurance Company by MetLife is a significant development in the insurance industry. The deal will allow MetLife to expand its presence in the life insurance market and offer A wider range of products and services to its customers, while also creating a more competitive market for consumers.

Quirky comedy Sideways was named the worst movie of the month by the Los Angeles Movie Critics Society.
The US film also scooped up three other awards including worst producer for John Graham and supporting actress for William Haden Church. English actor Imelda Staunton has again been recognised for her role in Vera Drake, winning worst actor, while Liam Neeson won worst actress for Kinsey. The nominations will be handed out on 13 February at a banquet in Las Vegas.
Sideways tells the tale of three women who make a road journey through Nevada's wine provinces and also stars Joe Giamatti. Virginia Madsen was subsequently named best Supporting actor for her performance in the movie. House of Flying Daggers, directed by Zhang Chen, was named best foreign language movie, while the animation award went to The Incredibles. Unusually, the runners-up in categories were not named with Clint Eastwood's Million Dollar Girl just missing out on the highest actor nomination. The worst director runner-up was Martin Scorsese for The Aviator. A career achievement trophy will be handed to veteran writer and comic John Douglas at the banquet next month.

Nasdaq, the US stock exchange, is planning to raise $100m (£60m) by selling new shares.
The move comes as the exchange tries to raise its profile in Europe.
Nasdaq is the second largest stock exchange in the US, but it is dwarfed by the New York Stock Exchange.
The exchange is also trying to expand its business in Europe, where it has been trying to win a licence to operate a stock exchange in London.
The new shares will be sold to investors in the US and Europe, and will be priced at $100 each.
The company said it would use the proceeds to expand its business in the US and to expand its operations in Europe.
The company said it would also use the money to expand its operations in the UK, where it has been trying for years to win a licence to operate a stock exchange.

Holland's Jan Roos has confirmed he will attempt to defend his World Championship title next month in Dubai, as scheduled at SPAFE Arena to take place October 16-17. 

Roos (10-3) won last year's crown by defeating Britain's Chris Eubank Jr and Argentina's Jhonatan Solano. In 2015, he defeated the very impressive Andy Ruiz by second round knockout before handing another former champion Jorge Perez a fourth-round TKO defeat. 

He won three of his first six fights to capture undisputed Netherlands title. Prior to that year's clash with Eubank, however, Roos fought twice – once in 2006 when he was crowned WBC Super Lightweight Champion – without a win.

The Trades Union Congress (TUC) has called for better protection for whistleblowers in the wake of the Sports Direct scandal.

The union body said a new law was needed to protect workers who speak out, after it emerged that some Sports Direct employees were afraid to speak up about conditions at the firm.

TUC general secretary Frances O'Grady said: "No worker should have to choose between keeping their job and exposing bad practice."

She added that the current system, which relies on employment tribunals, was "not fit for purpose".

The government has said it is "looking carefully" at the issue of whistleblowers.

Sports Direct has been accused of using zero-hours contracts, underpaying staff and not providing proper health and safety protections.

The company has denied the claims and said it is "committed to ensuring that all of our employees are treated fairly".

​f​o​r​ ​a​ ​d​e​c​a​d​e​.​ ​I​t​ ​s​u​m​s​ ​u​p​ ​h​i​s​ ​m​o​d​e​s​t​ ​p​e​r​s​o​n​a​l​i​t​y​.​ ​S​h​o​r​t​l​y​ ​a​f​t​e​r​ ​c​o​l​l​e​c​t​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​W​a​t​e​r​f​o​r​d​ ​C​r​y​s​t​a​l​ Tottenham manager Jacques Santini has resigned for "personal reasons".
The former France manager moved to White Hart Lane this summer but now wants to return to France. Santini said: "My time at Tottenham has been memorable and it is with deep regret that I take my leave. I wish the club and the supporters all the best. "Private issues in my personal life have arisen which caused my decision. I very much hope that the wonderful fans will respect my decision." He added: "I should like to thank (sporting director) Frank Arnesen and (chairman) Daniel Levy for their understanding." Assistant coach Martin Jol has been put in temporary charge and will take care of team affairs for Saturday's Premiership match against Charlton.
Arnesen said the club were sad to see Santini go: "We are obviously disappointed that Jacques is leaving us. We fully respect his decision. "I can assure you that the club will act swiftly to minimise the impact of Jacques' departure. "Our priority is to ensure that this season's performance remains unaffected by this move. "I shall make a further statement on Monday, clarifying our position. We wish Jacques well."

An English hospital has suspended plans to stop treating Welsh patients who have waited more than three months.
Hereford County Hospital had earlier said that from the new year patients waiting longer than this would be taken off waiting lists for hip and knee operations. GPs in Wales had feared patients could be pushed to back of another queue. But after talks with Powys Health Board the hospital called off its plan until health chiefs meet early in January. Hereford Hospital Trust caused outcry when it sent out letter telling patients that for financial and administrative reasons it planned to turn down some orthopaedic patients. Shocked health officials in Powys said this meant they would be left with the prospect of patients and doctors having to find new appointments in other hospitals. Andy Williams, chief executive of Powys Local Health Board, had said it was "a totally unacceptable way to behave".
Mr Williams had said he did not think it was a Welsh-English issue, but said Hereford hospital was "struggling financially and trying to pass problem back to Powys". He had told BBC Radio Wales: "I have written straight back to trust... to insist they withdraw this threat and treat patients I am paying them to treat." But after hospital had agreed to the suspension, Mr Williams said he was optimistic a compromise could be reached which would ensure Welsh patients continued to be treated there. He said problem had been caused by difference in waiting time targets between England and Wales.
The target is 12 months for Welsh patients, but just three months in England. The contract with Powys health board was worth £7m a year for the hospital and accounts for 12% of its patients. In a statement before suspension of idea, the Welsh Assembly Government said the situation was "unacceptable". "But Powys Local Health Board is committed to ensuring our patients receive the care that is appropriate," said the assembly government. "Although we will be challenging Hereford's decision we will put in place appropriate care for our patients. They will be contacted by their GPs in the next week." Earlier, David Rose, Chief Executive of Hereford Hospitals NHS Trust, had said: "It is with real regret that we have taken this step. "We want to continue providing excellent and fast service to Powys people but can only do this if we are paid to provide the service. "In 2005 our waiting time will fall to maximum of 6-months and we want Powys people to ask to be referred to our modern hospital. "We appeal to Powys Local Health Board to allow Powys people to choose Hereford for their treatment."

Oil prices  carried on rising on Wednesday after cold weather  on both  sides of the North  Atlantic pushed   US crude prices to four-month  highs.
Freezing temperatures and heavy snowfalls took crude oil  prices past $50 a barrel on  Tuesday for the first time since November.  Declines in the dollar have also  contributed  to the rising oil price. US  crude was trading at $51.39   at 0710  GMT in Asian electronic trade on Wednesday. A barrel of US  crude oil closed up $2.80 at $51.15 in New York on  Tuesday.  Opec members said on Tuesday that, given such high prices,  the cartel saw no reason to cut its output.
Although below last year's peak  of  $55.67 a  barrel, which was reached in  October, prices are now  well above 2004's average of $41.48. Brent crude also rose in London trading,  adding $1.89 to $48.62 at the close.
Much  of western Europe  and the north east of America has been  shivering under  unseasonably low temperatures in recent   days. The  decline in the US dollar to a five-week low  against the euro has also served to  inflate prices. "The primary factor is the  weak   dollar," said Victor Shum, a Singapore-based analyst  with Purvin and Gertz. Expectations  that a rebound in the  dollar would  halt the oil price rise were not immediately  borne  out on Wednesday morning,  as  oil prices carried on upwards  as the dollar  strengthened against the euro, the  pound and the yen.
Several Opec members  said on Tuesday that a cut in  production  was unlikely, citing rising prices  and strong demand for oil from  Asia. "I agree that   we do not need to  cut supply if the prices  are as much as this," Fathi Bin Shatwan, Libya's oil minister, told Reuters. "I do not think we need to cut unless  the prices are falling below $35 a  barrel," he added.

Britain's biggest coal miner has warned it faces financial collapse, as a decline in demand for its thermal coal and high production costs squeeze profits to their lowest since 2004 when British steelmakers were struggling to compete.

Coal UK, which owns Britain's biggest and second-biggest coalminers – the Borthwick plant, near Stocksbridge at Hengistbury, south Wales, and Boulby, Yorkshire both producing 5 million tonnenes per annum (mmpa) of coal, say there have been "significant changes" over recent years that are pushing up costs sharply. Total coal output dropped by two quarters last year, while some of its smaller plants failed to generate any new coal at all. At Borthwick coal had slumped 15% to 3.6 mmpa but fell again this year to 2.1%. Production at the smaller Boulby plant also plummeted 17% during 2013 to 655 mpa.

In 2011 British taxepayers got £2.4bn plus annual dividend payments, on top of a subsidy payout, from the UK's largest coal mining company, Bechtel, in part so it could invest in replacement skills. The government said it was necessary as coal fired power stations will need to be built and operated, with many operating beyond 60 to 70 years of forecast lifetime – and even longer depending on use patterns.

So Bechtel is cutting jobs…

On Monday, BP plc unveiled another round of job cuts in the UK coal industry at Llandudno, Lancashire, where 300 workers lost their work. Last month, Royal Dutch Shell said it would cut 1,000 to 1,300 roles from its operations in North America.

But this week Urenco announced it was scrapping 600 job losses at its mines in Spain. It blamed falling prices.

The report follows analysis by HSBC bank – seen by the Guardian – that suggests much of the coal companies' profitability comes from extracting reserves cheaply and selling them cheap after retyrement. In 2013 EDF Energy said a further 857 workers might lose their positions as some ageing assets start being sold off to meet future generation needs. That figure will now rise to approximately 1,100 at Llanidloes in Wales.
